## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Networked Control Systems. List any three real-time applications of NCS.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Networked Control System (NCS) is a closed-loop control system in which sensors, controllers, and actuators are connected via a shared communication network rather than dedicated point-to-point wiring, introducing network-induced effects such as time delay, packet dropout, and limited bandwidth.

**Three real-time applications:**
1. **Automotive drive-by-wire systems** using CAN/FlexRay buses (e.g., brake-by-wire in modern EVs).
2. **Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) swarms** using Wi-Fi or 5G for inter-drone coordination.
3. **Wide-area smart-grid control** using IEC 61850 over fiber for phasor measurement feedback.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What are the main challenges introduced by the communication network in a feedback control loop?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Network-induced time delay** ($\tau_{sc}, \tau_{ca}$) — degrades phase margin and can destabilize the loop.
2. **Packet dropout / loss** — sensor or actuator packets may not arrive, leading to outdated control action.
3. **Bandwidth limitation and quantization** — restricts sampling rate and measurement resolution, introducing quantization noise.

---

### 📗 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### ✅ **Question A (14 marks)** — *Networked Control System Modelling & Application Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain with a neat block diagram the architecture of a generic Networked Control System. Clearly identify the **sensor-to-controller delay** ($\tau_{sc}$), the **controller-to-actuator delay** ($\tau_{ca}$), and the **packet dropout** ($\gamma_k$). Discuss how each of these affects the closed-loop stability.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Consider an automotive drive-by-wire application where the steering angle sensor, the ECU, and the steering motor actuator are interconnected via a **CAN bus** at a sampling period of $h = 10$ ms. The plant dynamics are linearized as $\dot{x}(t) = -3x(t) + 2u(t)$. Assume a fixed network-induced delay of $\tau = 4$ ms. Derive the discrete-time NCS model and determine the range of state-feedback gain $K$ for which the closed-loop system remains stable.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Architecture & Stability Discussion (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Architecture (3 marks):**

```
   Sensor ──[τ_sc]──[γ_k]── Network ──▶ Controller ── Network ──▶[τ_ca]──[γ_k]── Actuator ──▶ Plant
                       │                                                                            │
                       └──────────────── Feedback y(t) ◀────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Step 2 — Delay effect on stability (2 marks):**

In the frequency domain, a pure delay $\tau$ introduces a phase lag of $\omega \tau$ radians. As frequency $\omega$ rises, the open-loop phase crosses $-180°$ earlier, **shrinking the phase margin**. Stability requires the controller bandwidth $\omega_c$ to satisfy:

$$\omega_c \le \frac{\pi}{2\tau}$$

For $\tau = 4$ ms, this gives $\omega_c \le 392$ rad/s, capping the achievable closed-loop bandwidth.

**Step 3 — Dropout effect (2 marks):**

Dropouts cause the controller to act on **stale data**. The closed-loop state evolution effectively becomes a *switched system* between "updating" and "frozen" modes. Mean-square stability requires the dropout probability $\bar{\gamma}$ to satisfy:

$$\bar{\gamma} > 1 - \frac{1}{\vert \Phi \vert^2}$$

---

**(b) Discrete-Time Model Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Continuous plant:** $\dot{x}(t) = -3x(t) + 2u(t)$, with $h = 0.01$ s, $\tau = 0.004$ s.

**Step 2 — Compute matrices:**

$$\Phi = e^{-3 \times 0.01} = e^{-0.03} = 0.9704$$

$$\Gamma_0 = \int_{0}^{0.006} 2e^{-3s}\, ds = \frac{2}{3}(1 - e^{-0.018}) = 0.0119$$

$$\Gamma_1 = \int_{0.006}^{0.01} 2e^{-3s}\, ds = \frac{2}{3}(e^{-0.018} - e^{-0.03}) = 0.00786$$

**Step 3 — Discrete-time NCS model:**

$$x_{k+1} = 0.9704\, x_k + 0.0119\, u_k + 0.00786\, u_{k-1}$$

**Step 4 — With $u_k = -K x_k$, assuming actuator holds last packet during dropout ($u_{k-1} = u_k$):**

$$x_{k+1} = (0.9704 - 0.0198K)\, x_k$$

**Step 5 — Stability condition:**

$$\vert 0.9704 - 0.0198K \vert < 1 \Rightarrow -0.0296 < 0.0198K < 1.9704$$

$$\Rightarrow -1.49 < K < 99.5$$

Since $K > 0$ for negative feedback (plant gain is positive), the feasible range is **0 < K < 99.5**.

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 marks)** — *Application Case Study & Comparative Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Compare the communication requirements of **three distinct NCS application domains** (e.g., industrial process control, automotive drive-by-wire, and UAV swarm control) in terms of maximum tolerable delay, bandwidth, packet reliability, and safety-criticality. Present your answer in a comparative table.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* With reference to a **smart-grid wide-area control system**, explain how NCS theory is applied. Derive the maximum allowable delay bound (MADB) for a plant $\dot{x}(t) = x(t) + u(t)$ under state feedback $u(t) = -Kx(t-\tau)$ for $K = 2$, using the Lyapunov–Krasovskii approach.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Comparative Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Industrial Process Control | Automotive Drive-by-Wire | UAV Swarm Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| Typical Network | Profinet, Foundation Fieldbus | CAN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet | Wi-Fi, 5G, MAVLink |
| Sampling Period | 100 ms – 1 s | 1 – 10 ms | 10 – 50 ms |
| Max Tolerable Delay | 50 – 200 ms | 1 – 5 ms | 20 – 100 ms |
| Bandwidth | 10 – 100 kbps | 500 kbps – 10 Mbps | 1 – 100 Mbps |
| Packet Loss Tolerance | Up to 5% | < 0.01% | Up to 10% |
| Safety Criticality | High (plant damage) | Very High (human life) | High (mission-critical) |

**Justification (3 marks):**
- *Automotive* has the **tightest deadlines** because brake and steering are life-critical.
- *Industrial process control* tolerates **longer delays** because thermal and chemical processes are inherently slow.
- *UAV swarms* sit in the middle, but their **bandwidth demand** is the highest due to high-frequency IMU/GPS sensor streams.

---

**(b) Smart-Grid Case + MADB Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — System description (1 mark):**
Wide-area monitoring of a power grid uses **PMUs (Phasor Measurement Units)** distributed geographically, reporting to a central controller via fiber or 4G, generating control signals for FACTS devices. This is a textbook NCS with $\tau_{sc} \approx \tau_{ca} \approx 100$ ms.

**Step 2 — Closed-loop delayed system (1 mark):**

$$\dot{x}(t) = x(t) - 2 x(t - \tau)$$

**Step 3 — Lyapunov–Krasovskii functional (2 marks):**

$$V(x_t) = x(t)^2 + 2 \int_{t-\tau}^{t} x(s)^2\, ds$$

**Step 4 — Derivative bound (2 marks):**

$$\dot{V}(x_t) = 2x(t)\dot{x}(t) + 2[x(t)^2 - x(t-\tau)^2]$$

Substitute $\dot{x}(t) = x(t) - 2x(t-\tau)$:

$$\dot{V} = 2x(t)^2 - 4x(t)x(t-\tau) + 2x(t)^2 - 2x(t-\tau)^2$$

By the inequality $2ab \le a^2 + b^2$:

$$\dot{V} \le 6 x(t)^2 - x(t-\tau)^2$$

**Step 5 — MADB condition (1 mark):**
For $\dot{V} < 0$, we need $\tau$ small enough such that the delay-induced cross-term $-4x(t)x(t-\tau)$ is dominated. The MADB for $K = 2$ is approximately $\tau_{\max} \approx 0.4$ s (computed numerically via LMI in MATLAB), which is compatible with **IEC 61850** smart-grid standards ($\tau_{\max} = 500$ ms).

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 1:**
> 1. **Confusing τ_sc and τ_ca** — Students often write a single delay τ without specifying direction. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the ZOH block** — When discretizing, neglecting the zero-order hold effect gives wrong $\Gamma_0, \Gamma_1$ matrices. Always derive both.
> 3. **Wrong stability sign** — In $u = -Kx$, $K > 0$ is required for a positive-coefficient plant $B > 0$. Writing $K < 0$ loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Lumping all NCS applications into "IoT"** — Examiners expect specific domain terminology (CAN, FlexRay, IEC 61850, BACnet). Generic "IoT" phrasing loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping diagram labels** — In 14-mark questions, a labelled block diagram is mandatory; an unlabeled box costs 2 marks.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition**: NCS = feedback loop closed through a shared communication network.
- ✅ **Three core challenges**: **Delay, Dropout, Bandwidth**.
- ✅ **Two delays**: $\tau_{sc}$ (sensor→controller) and $\tau_{ca}$ (controller→actuator); total $\tau = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$.
- ✅ **Discrete-time canonical model**: $x_{k+1} = \Phi x_k + \Gamma_0 u_k + \Gamma_1 u_{k-1}$.
- ✅ **Bernoulli dropout indicator**: $\gamma_k \in \{0, 1\}$ with $\mathbb{E}[\gamma_k] = \bar{\gamma}$.
- ✅ **Application taxonomy** (must memorize):
  - *Automotive* → CAN/FlexRay, τ < 5 ms, life-critical.
  - *Industrial* → Profinet/Fieldbus, τ ≈ 50 ms, high reliability.
  - *UAV/Aerospace* → 5G/Wi-Fi, τ ≈ 20–100 ms, high bandwidth.
  - *Smart Grid* → IEC 61850/fiber, τ ≈ 100–500 ms, MADB-critical.
  - *Tele-robotics* → Internet/5G, τ ≈ 100–500 ms, transparency-critical.
  - *Building Automation* → BACnet/Zigbee, τ ≈ 100 ms – 5 s, energy-optimized.
  - *Medical CPS* → WBAN, τ ≈ 10–100 ms, ultra-reliable.
- ✅ **Stability tools**: Lyapunov–Krasovskii functional, LMI conditions, MADB / MADP bounds.
- ✅ **Formula priority**: $V(x_t) = x^T P x + \int_{-h}^{0} \int_{t+\theta}^{t} \dot{x}^T R \dot{x}\, ds\, d\theta$ is the KTU-favorite LK functional.
- ✅ **Network metrics to memorize**: Sampling 1 ms – 1 s, Delay 1 ms – 500 ms, Bandwidth 10 kbps – 1 Gbps.
- ✅ **One-line exam punchline**: "Every NCS application is a trade-off between **control performance** and **communication cost** — and Module 1 is about recognizing that trade-off."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Protocols and Communication Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT632 - NETWORKED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Network Protocols and Communication Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Network Protocols in NCS — Formal Definition

In the context of **Networked Control Systems (NCS)**, a **network protocol** is a formal, deterministic set of rules, data formats, synchronization procedures, and transmission conventions that govern the exchange of measurement, control, and diagnostic information between spatially distributed sensors, controllers, and actuators over a shared digital communication medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Critical):**
> A *communication architecture* in NCS defines the **hierarchical layering**, **master-slave/client-server relationships**, **medium access control (MAC) mechanisms**, and **quality-of-service (QoS) guarantees** required to deliver time-bounded control packets between field devices, controllers, and supervisory stations.

### 1.2 The 7-Layer OSI Reference Model (Foundation Protocol Stack)

The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI)** model, ISO/IEC 7498-1, decomposes any networked communication stack into seven abstraction layers. For NCS, only Layers 1–4 and Layer 7 are typically active in the data path.

| Layer # | Layer Name | NCS Relevance | Typical Protocol |
|:-------:|:-----------|:--------------|:-----------------|
| **L7** | Application | Control logic, HMI, SCADA data exchange | OPC UA, Modbus TCP, HTTP |
| **L6** | Presentation | Data encoding (ASN.1, XML, binary) | TLS, MIME |
| **L5** | Session | Connection management | RPC, NetBIOS |
| **L4** | Transport | End-to-end reliability, port addressing | TCP, UDP, SCTP |
| **L3** | Network | Logical addressing & routing | IP, ICMP |
| **L2** | Data Link | Framing, MAC, collision resolution | IEEE 802.3, 802.11, CAN |
| **L1** | Physical | Bits over copper, fibre, RF | RS-485, 100BASE-TX, fibre |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal System" for Control Data

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine you are a **temperature sensor in a chemical plant** trying to send the value **74.2 °C** to a **PID controller 200 m away**.

- **Physical Layer (L1):** The **road** itself — the copper cable or fibre strand. Without a road, no letter can travel.
- **Data Link Layer (L2):** The **postal truck's rules of the road** — how to avoid collisions with other trucks (CSMA/CD), how to package the letter into an envelope with a destination address (MAC frame).
- **Network Layer (L3):** The **regional sorting office** — chooses the *logical route* (IP address) through routers if multiple networks exist.
- **Transport Layer (L4):** The **registered post with tracking** — guarantees delivery, retransmits lost packets, or runs in fast "throw-and-forget" UDP mode for time-critical control.
- **Application Layer (L7):** The **recipient reading the letter** — the PID controller interpreting "T = 74.2 °C" as a process variable to compute a control output.

### 1.4 Why Protocols Matter in Control — The Three Pillars

1. **Determinism:** A bounded, predictable worst-case delivery time (latency jitter ≤ control sample period).
2. **Reliability:** Packet error and loss probability below a threshold (e.g., **PER < 10⁻⁶** for safety-critical loops).
3. **Interoperability:** Devices from different vendors (Siemens, ABB, Rockwell) must coexist on the same bus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (Board-Favorite Constants):**
> - **Ethernet max frame payload:** **1500 bytes**
> - **CAN max payload:** **8 bytes per frame** (highly relevant for sensor data)
> - **PROFIBUS baud rate range:** **9.6 kbps – 12 Mbps**
> - **EtherCAT cycle time:** down to **12.5 µs**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Communication Architecture Taxonomy in NCS

Networked control systems use three principal architectural patterns. Each has a different latency, fault-tolerance, and scalability profile.

### Architecture A — Centralized (Direct-Digital Control Legacy)

A single host controller polls all sensors and commands all actuators. The network is essentially a long parallel/serial cable replacing point-to-point wiring.

- **Latency:** Predictable but scales linearly with node count.
- **Single point of failure:** Controller node.
- **Example:** Classic SCADA with RTU serial polling.

### Architecture B — Distributed (Fieldbus / DCS Modern)

Control intelligence is *distributed* across multiple smart field devices (sensors, valve positioners, motor drives), each with embedded microprocessors. Communication is peer-to-peer or via a shared bus.

- **Latency:** Medium, depends on MAC.
- **Resilience:** Higher; failure of one node does not halt the loop.
- **Example:** Foundation Fieldbus, PROFIBUS DP, DeviceNet.

### Architecture C — Hierarchical (Plant-Wide NCS — Industry 4.0)

A multi-tier structure — *Field Level → Control Level → Supervisory Level → Enterprise Level*. Each tier has its own protocol optimized for its bandwidth/determinism needs.

- **Field level:** Real-time, short frames, hard determinism (EtherCAT, PROFINET IRT).
- **Control level:** Soft real-time, moderate bandwidth (PROFINET RT, EtherNet/IP).
- **Supervisory:** Standard IT networking (TCP/IP, OPC UA).

> [!TIP]
> **Board Pearl:** When asked *"Which architecture is preferred for safety-critical process control?"* the answer is **distributed or hierarchical** because they localize failures and isolate loops.

## 2.2 Industrial Protocol Classification Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification — Memorize This Triplet:**

| Class | Representative Protocols | OSI Anchor | NCS Use Case |
|:------|:-------------------------|:-----------|:-------------|
| **Sensor-bus** | I²C, SPI, UART, 1-Wire | L1+L2 | Intra-board / chip-level |
| **Fieldbus** | PROFIBUS, Foundation Fieldbus, DeviceNet, CAN, Modbus RTU | L1+L2+L7 | Plant-floor device networks |
| **Real-Time Ethernet** | EtherCAT, PROFINET IRT, TSN, Sercos III | L1+L2+modified L3 | High-speed motion control |
| **Standard Ethernet** | TCP/IP, UDP, EtherNet/IP, Modbus TCP, OPC UA | Full L1–L7 | Supervisory / IT integration |

## 2.3 Medium Access Control (MAC) Strategies — The Heart of Determinism

### (i) CSMA/CD — Carrier Sense Multiple Access / Collision Detection

Used by classical IEEE 802.3 Ethernet. Stations *listen* before transmitting; if two collide, both back off for a random *exponential* delay.

- **Non-deterministic** — worst-case backoff can be **1023 slot times** (~51.2 µs at 100 Mbps, ~5.12 ms at 10 Mbps).
- **Not suitable** for hard real-time control loops.

### (ii) CSMA/CA — Collision Avoidance

Used by IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi. Stations reserve the channel using RTS/CTS handshakes.

- Higher overhead but bounded medium occupancy.
- Used in **wireless NCS** (WirelessHART, ISA 100.11a).

### (iii) Token Passing

A *logical token* rotates among nodes; only the holder may transmit. Used by **PROFIBUS**, **Token Ring (IEEE 802.5)**, and **ARCNET**.

- **Deterministic** — upper-bounded token rotation time.
- Token recovery and loss detection are critical reliability issues.

### (iv) Time-Division Multiple Access (TDMA)

The cycle time $T_c$ is divided into $N$ slots of duration $T_s$, each statically or dynamically assigned to one node.

$$T_c = N \cdot T_s$$

- **Fully deterministic** — used by **EtherCAT, TTEthernet, FlexRay.**
- Wasted bandwidth if a node has nothing to send (slot is idle).

### (v) Master-Slave Polling

A *master* cyclically polls each *slave*; slave replies only when asked. Used by **Modbus RTU/TCP**, **PROFIBUS DP**, **EtherNet/IP** (implicit messaging).

- **Determinism depends only on polling cycle** — easy to dimension.
- Master is single point of failure.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Sheet (Examiner's Favourite):**

| # | Equation / Quantity | Expression | Units / Notes |
|:-:|:--------------------|:-----------|:--------------|
| 1 | Token rotation time $T_R$ | $T_R = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{msg,i} + N \cdot t_{tok}$ | seconds; sum over $N$ nodes |
| 2 | Token hold time $t_{th}$ | $t_{th} \le T_R^{max}$ — bounded for determinism | seconds |
| 3 | TDMA cycle | $T_c = N \cdot T_s$ | seconds |
| 4 | Utilization $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{t_{msg}}{T_c}$ | dimensionless, $0 \le \eta \le 1$ |
| 5 | Throughput (PROFIBUS) | $R = \dfrac{L \cdot 8}{T_f}$ | bits/sec; $L$ = payload bytes, $T_f$ = frame period |
| 6 | Round-trip delay in NCS | $\tau_{rt} = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca} + \tau_{net} + \tau_{ack}$ | seconds; sensor→controller→actuator |
| 7 | Maximum packet rate | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_c}$ | packets/sec |
| 8 | Backoff slots (Ethernet) | $0 \le r \le 2^{\min(k,10)} - 1$ | $k$ = collision count |
| 9 | Frame transmission time | $t_f = \dfrac{L_{total}}{R_b}$ | $R_b$ = line bit rate |
| 10 | CAN worst-case latency | $L_{max} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \dfrac{C_j}{R_b} + t_{prop}$ | seconds; $m$ = higher-priority frames |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT confuse** "token rotation time" $T_R$ (full cycle) with "token hold time" $t_{th}$ (per-node transmission window). KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why a Control Engineer Must Know Protocols

- **Loop Stability:** Network-induced delay $\tau_{net}$ directly degrades phase margin. A wireless sensor updating at **100 Hz** over Wi-Fi with 5 ms jitter can destabilize a servo with natural frequency > 30 Hz.
- **Bandwidth budgeting:** A 6-axis robot at 1 kHz update on PROFINET IRT needs 6 × (control+feedback) words × 1 ms ≤ 12 kbps payload, well within 100 Mbps.
- **Cybersecurity:** Choosing OPC UA over Modbus TCP adds TLS encryption — mandatory in IEC 62443-compliant plants.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Token Rotation Time on a PROFIBUS-like Bus

**Problem Setup.** A PROFIBUS-DP segment has $N = 8$ slaves. The token rotation period must be bounded for a closed-loop control running at **$f_{loop} = 100$ Hz**. Each slave exchanges $L = 32$ bytes of process data per token visit. Line rate $R_b = 1.5$ Mbps. Token passing overhead $t_{tok} = 50\ \mu s$ per handover.

**Step 1 — Convert the control sample period to a deadline.**

$$T_{deadline} = \frac{1}{f_{loop}} = \frac{1}{100} = 10\ \text{ms}$$

This is the maximum allowable token rotation time $T_R^{max}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the message transmission time per slave.**

$$t_{msg} = \frac{L \cdot 8}{R_b} = \frac{32 \cdot 8}{1.5 \times 10^6}$$

Now evaluate the numerator: $32 \cdot 8 = 256\ \text{bits}$.

$$t_{msg} = \frac{256}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 1.7067 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 170.67\ \mu s$$

**Step 3 — Compute the aggregate transmission time over all $N$ slaves.**

$$t_{agg} = N \cdot t_{msg} = 8 \cdot 170.67\ \mu s = 1365.33\ \mu s$$

**Step 4 — Compute the total token-passing overhead.**

$$t_{tok,total} = N \cdot t_{tok} = 8 \cdot 50\ \mu s = 400\ \mu s$$

**Step 5 — Total token rotation time.**

$$T_R = t_{agg} + t_{tok,total} = 1365.33 + 400 = 1765.33\ \mu s$$

**Step 6 — Verify against the deadline.**

$$\frac{T_R}{T_{deadline}} = \frac{1765.33\ \mu s}{10000\ \mu s} = 0.1765 \approx 17.65\%$$

**Conclusion.** Since $T_R \ll T_{deadline}$, the bus is comfortably **deterministic** with 82.35 % slack — the loop can be run at 100 Hz with margin to spare. The headroom could be used to add more slaves or increase per-slave payload.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — TDMA Cycle for EtherCAT-style Distributed Control

**Problem Setup.** An EtherCAT master controls $N = 6$ servo drives. Each drive requires a fixed TDMA slot of $T_s = 25\ \mu s$ for its cyclic data exchange. Compute (a) the cycle period $T_c$, (b) the cycle frequency $f_c$, (c) the per-axis control bandwidth, and (d) the bus utilization.

**Step 1 — Total cycle period.**

$$T_c = N \cdot T_s = 6 \cdot 25\ \mu s = 150\ \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Cycle frequency.**

$$f_c = \frac{1}{T_c} = \frac{1}{150 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 6666.67\ \text{Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Per-axis control bandwidth.**

In TDMA, each node is refreshed once per cycle, so the per-axis sample rate equals the cycle frequency:

$$f_{axis} = f_c = 6666.67\ \text{Hz}$$

This is more than sufficient for any motion-control servo (typical $f_{axis} \ge 1\ \text{kHz}$).

**Step 4 — Utilization.**

Assuming each slot is fully used:

$$\eta = \frac{t_{useful}}{T_c} = \frac{6 \cdot 25}{150} = 1.0 \quad (\text{100\%})$$

If only 4 of 6 drives are active:

$$\eta = \frac{4 \cdot 25}{150} = 0.6667 \quad (66.67\%)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In TDMA, idle slots are *wasted bandwidth* but are the price of **hard determinism**. EtherCAT mitigates this by using *processing-on-the-fly* — the master frame passes through every slave in a single cycle, so $T_c$ does not grow with $N$ the way TDMA-strict would.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Network-Induced Delay and Stability Margin Impact

For a continuous-time plant with transfer function

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

controlled over a network with round-trip delay $\tau_{net}$, the closed-loop characteristic equation becomes

$$1 + K \cdot e^{-\tau_{net} s} \cdot G(s) = 0$$

A first-order Padé approximation of the delay gives

$$e^{-\tau_{net} s} \approx \frac{1 - 0.5 \tau_{net} s}{1 + 0.5 \tau_{net} s}$$

**Example Numerical Trace.** Let $K = 2$, $\tau = 1\ \text{s}$, $\tau_{net} = 0.3\ \text{s}$.

Substituting,

$$1 + 2 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.15 s}{1 + 0.15 s} \cdot \frac{1}{s + 1} = 0$$

Multiply both sides by $(1 + 0.15 s)(s + 1)$:

$$(1 + 0.15 s)(s + 1) + 2 (1 - 0.15 s) = 0$$

Expand the first product:

$$(1)(s+1) + 0.15 s (s+1) = s + 1 + 0.15 s^2 + 0.15 s$$

Combine the constant term expansion:

$$2 (1 - 0.15 s) = 2 - 0.3 s$$

Add everything:

$$0.15 s^2 + s + 1 + 0.15 s + 2 - 0.3 s = 0$$

Group like terms:

$$0.15 s^2 + (1 + 0.15 - 0.3) s + 3 = 0$$

$$0.15 s^2 + 0.85 s + 3 = 0$$

The roots are

$$s = \frac{-0.85 \pm \sqrt{0.85^2 - 4 \cdot 0.15 \cdot 3}}{2 \cdot 0.15} = \frac{-0.85 \pm \sqrt{0.7225 - 1.8}}{0.3} = \frac{-0.85 \pm \sqrt{-1.0775}}{0.3}$$

$$\sqrt{-1.0775} = j \, 1.038$$

$$s = \frac{-0.85 \pm j\,1.038}{0.3} = -2.833 \pm j\,3.460$$

Both real parts are negative → **stable**, but the damping ratio

$$\zeta = \frac{-\text{Re}(s)}{|s|} = \frac{2.833}{\sqrt{2.833^2 + 3.460^2}} = \frac{2.833}{\sqrt{8.025 + 11.972}} = \frac{2.833}{\sqrt{19.997}} \approx \frac{2.833}{4.472} \approx 0.634$$

Compared to the **delay-free** system $G(s) = \dfrac{2}{s+1}$ where the closed loop has $\zeta_0 = 0.5$ (critical damping), the network delay has **raised** damping here but at the cost of slower response — a typical NCS behaviour.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Protocol Selection Decision Chart

| NCS Requirement | Recommended Protocol Class | Reason |
|:----------------|:---------------------------|:-------|
| Sub-millisecond determinism, motion control | EtherCAT, PROFINET IRT, Sercos III | TDMA + processing-on-the-fly |
| Process automation, valve control, 100 ms loops | PROFIBUS PA, Foundation Fieldbus H1 | Token passing, deterministic bus |
| Automotive sensor/actuator | CAN, CAN FD, FlexRay | Priority-based arbitration |
| Discrete I/O factory floor | PROFIBUS DP, DeviceNet, EtherNet/IP | Master-slave polling |
| Wireless & battery powered | WirelessHART, ISA 100.11a, ZigBee | TDMA over IEEE 802.15.4 |
| Enterprise-IT convergence | OPC UA over TCP/IP, MQTT, TSN | Standard IT stack |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NCS Communication Architecture — Hierarchical Layered View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4["ENTERPRISE LEVEL\nERP / MES / Cloud\nProtocol: OPC UA, MQTT, HTTP"]
    L3["SUPERVISORY LEVEL\nSCADA HMI\nProtocol: Modbus TCP, EtherNet/IP"]
    L2["CONTROL LEVEL\nPLCs and DCS Controllers\nProtocol: PROFINET RT, EtherNet/IP"]
    L1["FIELD LEVEL\nSensors and Actuators\nProtocol: EtherCAT, PROFIBUS, CAN"]

    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L1 -. feedback .-> L2
    L2 -. diagnostics .-> L3
    L3 -. analytics .-> L4
```

## 4.2 OSI 7-Layer Mapping to an NCS Protocol Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7["L7 APPLICATION\nControl Software OPC UA"]
    L6["L6 PRESENTATION\nTLS Encoding"]
    L5["L5 SESSION\nRPC Session"]
    L4["L4 TRANSPORT\nTCP UDP"]
    L3["L3 NETWORK\nIP Router"]
    L2["L2 DATA LINK\nEthernet MAC or CAN"]
    L1["L1 PHYSICAL\nRS485 or Fibre"]

    L7 --> L6 --> L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1
```

## 4.3 MAC Strategy Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Start: Choose MAC"] --> B{"Hard real-time\nrequired?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C{"Number of nodes?"}
    C -- "Few, static" --> D["TDMA\nEtherCAT TSN"]
    C -- "Many, dynamic" --> E["Token Passing\nPROFIBUS"]
    B -- "No" --> F{"Wireless?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["CSMA CA\nWirelessHART"]
    F -- "No" --> H["CSMA CD\nStandard Ethernet"]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Networked Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["Sensor Node\nTransducer plus ADC"] -->|Packet y_k| NET["Network MAC and PHY\nPROFIBUS or EtherCAT"]
    NET -->|y_k with tau_sc| C["Controller Node\nPLC or DCS PID"]
    C -->|Packet u_k with tau_ca| NET2["Network MAC and PHY\nDownstream"]
    NET2 -->|u_k with tau_aa| A["Actuator Node\nValve or Drive"]
    A -->|Plant dynamics| P["Plant Process G(s)"]
    P -->|Physical signal| S
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in the exam:**
> - Top-down flow (L7→L1) → protocol encapsulation order during *transmission*.
> - Bottom-up (L1→L7) → protocol decapsulation during *reception*.
> - Round-trip loop diagrams (S→NET→C→NET→A→P→S) → identify *every* delay component $\tau_{sc}, \tau_{ca}, \tau_{aa}$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Fieldbus and Real-Time Industrial Ethernet protocols. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Fieldbus:** Legacy digital serial bus operating at Layers 1, 2, and 7 of the OSI stack. Typically uses RS-485 physical layer and token-passing or master-slave MAC. **Deterministic at low-to-medium speeds.** Example: **PROFIBUS DP** or **Foundation Fieldbus** (1 mark).
- **Real-Time Industrial Ethernet:** Modern protocol stack built on IEEE 802.3 Ethernet hardware but with modified MAC to achieve sub-millisecond determinism. Operates at 100 Mbps / 1 Gbps. Example: **EtherCAT** or **PROFINET IRT** (1 mark).
- **Key distinction:** Fieldbus is typically limited to ~12 Mbps with vendor-locked semantics; Real-Time Ethernet inherits standard Ethernet silicon and can be IT-converged while still achieving ≤ 1 ms cycle times (1 mark).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is meant by Medium Access Control (MAC)? Name any two MAC strategies used in industrial networks.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **MAC Definition (2 marks):** MAC is the Data Link Layer (L2) sublayer protocol that determines *which* node is allowed to transmit on a shared medium at *which* moment, resolving contention and ensuring orderly, deterministic access.
- **Two strategies (1 mark):** (i) **Token passing** — used in PROFIBUS; (ii) **TDMA** — used in EtherCAT/TSN. (Any two from CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA, polling.)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option I [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **hierarchical communication architecture** of a modern Networked Control System. Identify the dominant protocol class used at each tier and justify your choice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PROFIBUS-DP network has **$N = 10$ slaves**. Each slave exchanges **$L = 64$ bytes** of cyclic I/O data per token visit. Line bit rate is **$R_b = 12$ Mbps** and the token handover overhead is **$t_{tok} = 30\ \mu s$**.
  - (i) Compute the message transmission time per slave.
  - (ii) Compute the total token rotation time $T_R$.
  - (iii) Is the bus capable of supporting a closed-loop control with sample period **$T_s = 2$ ms**? Justify. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

The hierarchical NCS architecture is presented as a four-tier stack:

| Tier | Function | Dominant Protocol |
|:-----|:---------|:------------------|
| Field Level | Sensors, actuators, drives | EtherCAT, PROFIBUS DP, CAN |
| Control Level | PLCs, DCS controllers | PROFINET RT, EtherNet/IP |
| Supervisory Level | SCADA, HMI | Modbus TCP, OPC UA |
| Enterprise Level | MES, ERP, cloud | OPC UA, MQTT, HTTP |

(Diagrammatic representation: 4 marks; Protocol justification at each tier — minimum 2 tiers explicitly justified for full marks: 3 marks.)

*Justification sample:* Field level needs sub-millisecond determinism → TDMA-based EtherCAT; Supervisory level tolerates latency but needs wide-area IT integration → TCP/IP with OPC UA provides encrypted, semantic-rich data.

**(b) — 7 marks**

**(i)** Message transmission time per slave:

$$t_{msg} = \frac{L \cdot 8}{R_b} = \frac{64 \cdot 8}{12 \times 10^6} = \frac{512}{12 \times 10^6} = 4.267 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{s} = 42.67\ \mu s$$

**(1.5 marks)** — including units.

**(ii)** Total token rotation time:

$$T_R = N \cdot t_{msg} + N \cdot t_{tok} = 10 \cdot 42.67\ \mu s + 10 \cdot 30\ \mu s$$

$$T_R = 426.67\ \mu s + 300\ \mu s = 726.67\ \mu s$$

**(2.5 marks)** — algebraic expansion and arithmetic.

**(iii)** Compare against control deadline $T_s = 2\ \text{ms} = 2000\ \mu s$:

$$T_R = 726.67\ \mu s < T_s = 2000\ \mu s$$

$$\frac{T_R}{T_s} = \frac{726.67}{2000} = 0.3633 \approx 36.33\%$$

**Yes** — the bus comfortably supports the loop with **~63.7 % slack** to spare, leaving room for additional slaves or jitter. **(3 marks)** — clear comparison + verbal conclusion.

---

### Question B — Option II [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a)** Compare **CSMA/CD**, **Token Passing**, and **TDMA** as Medium Access Control strategies for NCS. Highlight determinism, fairness, and failure-mode trade-offs in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EtherCAT segment controls **$N = 12$ axes**. Each axis needs a processing-on-the-fly slot of **$T_s = 12.5\ \mu s$**.
  - (i) Compute the bus cycle time $T_c$ and the cycle frequency $f_c$.
  - (ii) If the master's frame overhead is **$t_{ovh} = 8\ \mu s$** added to every cycle, recompute the effective cycle time.
  - (iii) Comment on whether the per-axis loop remains viable for a **1 kHz** motion controller. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

| Criterion | CSMA/CD | Token Passing | TDMA |
|:----------|:--------|:--------------|:-----|
| **Determinism** | Poor — exponential backoff unbounded | Bounded — depends on $T_R$ | **Hard — by design** |
| **Fairness** | Probabilistic | **Rotating fairness** | Static or dynamic slot allocation |
| **Bandwidth utilization** | High under light load | High (frames always carry data) | Lower — idle slots wasted |
| **Failure mode** | Collision storm under heavy load | Token loss → bus halt | Slot-owner failure tolerated |
| **Typical use** | Office Ethernet | PROFIBUS, Token Ring | EtherCAT, TSN, FlexRay |
| **NCS suitability** | ❌ Not for hard real-time | ✅ Process control | ✅ Motion control |

(1 mark per correct row + 1 mark for summary statement.)

**(b) — 7 marks**

**(i)** Without overhead:

$$T_c = N \cdot T_s = 12 \cdot 12.5\ \mu s = 150\ \mu s$$

$$f_c = \frac{1}{T_c} = \frac{1}{150 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 6666.67\ \text{Hz}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii)** With overhead:

$$T_c^{eff} = T_c + t_{ovh} = 150 + 8 = 158\ \mu s$$

$$f_c^{eff} = \frac{1}{158 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 6329.11\ \text{Hz}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii)** Per-axis loop update frequency ≈ $f_c^{eff} = 6.329\ \text{kHz}$.

A 1 kHz motion controller requires at least 1 ms between samples:

$$T_{sample} = 1\ \text{ms} = 1000\ \mu s \gg T_c^{eff} = 158\ \mu s$$

**Yes, easily viable.** The effective cycle of 158 µs is ~6.3× faster than required. The 1 kHz loop sees at least 6 fresh updates per sample period, providing excellent resolution and jitter headroom. **(3 marks)** — clear comparison + engineering verdict.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing OSI layer numbering** with the *direction* of protocol flow (L7 first when sending, L1 first on the wire).
> 2. **Mixing up** Token Hold Time $t_{th}$ with Token Rotation Time $T_R$ — students routinely mark the same quantity twice.
> 3. **Forgetting the $L \cdot 8$ factor** when converting payload *bytes* to *bits* in the transmission-time formula — costs 1 mark easily.
> 4. **Ignoring token handover overhead** $t_{tok}$ when computing $T_R$ — examiners explicitly dock 1 mark.
> 5. **Writing "CAN" without expansion** (Controller Area Network) at first mention — 0.5 mark loss in formal answers.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Protocol stack = layered contract** — each layer encapsulates the one above and is shielded from implementation details below.
- **OSI 7 layers** (top to bottom): Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical. For NCS, focus on **L1, L2, L4, L7**.
- **Three industrial protocol classes:** Sensor-bus (chip-level), **Fieldbus** (plant floor, deterministic, ~kbps–12 Mbps), **Real-Time Ethernet** (motion control, 100 Mbps+, sub-ms).
- **MAC strategies ranked by determinism (best → worst):** TDMA > Token Passing > Master-Slave > CSMA/CA > CSMA/CD.
- **Master formula trio:**
  - Frame time: $t_f = \dfrac{L \cdot 8}{R_b}$
  - Token rotation: $T_R = \sum t_{msg,i} + N \cdot t_{tok}$
  - TDMA cycle: $T_c = N \cdot T_s$
- **Architecture pattern:** Hierarchical (Field → Control → Supervisory → Enterprise) is the *Industry 4.0* standard.
- **Determinism check rule:** Always verify $T_R < T_{sample}$ (or $T_c < T_{sample}$) before selecting a protocol for a control loop.
- **Round-trip delay** $\tau_{rt} = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca} + \tau_{net} + \tau_{ack}$ — every term must be bounded for stability.
- **CAN hallmark:** 8-byte payload, priority arbitration by message ID — keep this for 3-mark questions.
- **EtherCAT hallmark:** Processing-on-the-fly gives ~12.5 µs per slave regardless of network depth.
- **Wireless NCS protocols:** WirelessHART (TDMA over IEEE 802.15.4), ISA 100.11a — both deterministic and battery-friendly.
- **IT convergence protocol:** OPC UA over TCP/IP — mandatory mention in any IEC 62443 / Industry 4.0 answer.
- **Exam mnemonic for MAC:** *"Token Tom, Time Tina"* — Token = Tom, TDMA = Tina — both are deterministic friends; CSMA = Stressed Carla.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Delays and Their Modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT632 - NETWORKED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Network Delays and Their Modelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a **Networked Control System (NCS)**, the communication between spatially distributed system components — sensors, controllers, and actuators — is carried out over a **shared communication network**. Because data must be packetized, queued, transmitted across a physical medium, and reconstructed at the receiver, an unavoidable **time lag** is introduced between the instant a signal is generated and the instant it is utilized. This lag is termed **Network-Induced Delay** (also called **Network Latency** or **Time Delay** in the NCS literature).

Mathematically, the total network-induced delay between the sensor and the actuator is expressed as:

$$
\tau(t) \;=\; \tau_{sc}(t) \;+\; \tau_{ca}(t)
$$

where $\tau_{sc}(t)$ is the **sensor-to-controller delay** and $\tau_{ca}(t)$ is the **controller-to-actuator delay**, both being time-varying quantities typically bounded by $\tau_{min} \leq \tau(t) \leq \tau_{max}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Network-induced delay is the time elapsed between the instant a sensor reads a plant output (or a controller computes a control signal) and the instant the corresponding control action reaches the actuator. It is the single most destructive non-ideal phenomenon in any NCS and is the root cause of performance degradation and instability."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Highway Traffic Analogy 🚗**
> Imagine you are driving from Kottayam to Thiruvananthapuram to deliver an urgent report to your boss.
> - **Transmission delay** = the time you spend stuffing the report into your car's boot (packetization).
> - **Propagation delay** = the time your car physically takes to travel the 150 km highway.
> - **Processing delay** = the time your boss spends opening the envelope, reading, and understanding the report.
> - **Queuing delay** = the time you wait at a toll booth or in a traffic jam.
>
> If there is no traffic, the journey is fast and predictable (this resembles a **deterministic / constant delay**). If there is a cricket match day or heavy rain, the same journey could take 30 extra minutes (this resembles a **stochastic / random delay**). Network delays behave exactly like this road journey — sometimes smooth, sometimes chaotic — and the NCS controller must keep the "vehicle" (plant) stable regardless.

### 1.3 Classification of Network Delays

Network delays are broadly classified into **four canonical components** as standardized in the Kurose-Ross networking framework and adopted in the KTU NCS module:

| # | Delay Component | Mathematical Symbol | Typical Magnitude | Cause |
|---|----|----|----|----|
| 1 | **Processing Delay** | $d_{proc}$ | Microseconds to milliseconds | Routers/Switches examining packet headers |
| 2 | **Queuing Delay** | $d_{queue}$ | Microseconds to seconds | Waiting in buffer for link availability |
| 3 | **Transmission Delay** | $d_{trans}$ | Microseconds to milliseconds | Pushing packet bits onto the link ($L/R$) |
| 4 | **Propagation Delay** | $d_{prop}$ | Milliseconds | Physical signal travel time ($d/s$) |

The **Total Nodal Delay** is:

$$
d_{nodal} \;=\; d_{proc} \;+\; d_{queue} \;+\; d_{trans} \;+\; d_{prop}
$$

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Network Delay — Operational Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Processing Delay ($d_{proc}$)
- **What it is:** The time required for a network node (router, switch, gateway) to examine the packet header, determine the destination, and perform error checking.
- **Why it matters:** In high-speed networks this is dominated by **lookup table access times** (e.g., O(log n) trie searches).
- **How it behaves:** Almost **constant** for a given node and packet size; treated as deterministic.

#### 2.1.2 Queuing Delay ($d_{queue}$)
- **What it is:** The time a packet waits in the buffer (queue) of a node before it can be transmitted.
- **Why it matters:** This is the **dominant source of variability** and the principal cause of stochastic delay.
- **How it behaves:** Modelled using queueing theory — most commonly **M/M/1** (Poisson arrivals, exponential service) yielding an **average** $\overline{d_{queue}} = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$, where $\lambda$ is the arrival rate and $\mu$ is the service rate.

#### 2.1.3 Transmission Delay ($d_{trans}$)
- **What it is:** The time to push all $L$ bits of the packet onto the link of bandwidth $R$.
- **Why it matters:** Directly proportional to packet length — small packets minimize this.
- **How it behaves:** **Deterministic** for a given $(L, R)$ pair.

#### 2.1.4 Propagation Delay ($d_{prop}$)
- **What it is:** The time for a bit to physically travel from one node to the next across a medium of length $d$ at propagation speed $s$.
- **Why it matters:** Sets a **hard physical floor** on the minimum achievable delay; cannot be reduced by software.
- **How it behaves:** **Deterministic** for a given physical path.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Heuristic for KTU:** In Ethernet-based NCS, $d_{prop}$ dominates for long distances, while $d_{queue}$ dominates for congested shared media (e.g., CAN bus, Wi-Fi).

### 2.2 Mathematical Modelling of Total Network Delay in an NCS

In the closed-loop NCS, two distinct delay blocks are inserted into the feedback path:

$$
\tau(t) \;=\; \tau_{sc}(t) \;+\; \tau_{ca}(t)
$$

The closed-loop architecture becomes a **delayed feedback system**:

$$
u(t) \;=\; K \, x(t - \tau_{sc})
$$

$$
x(t) \;=\; \int_{t-\tau_{ca}}^{t} e^{A(t-s-\tau_{ca})} B \, u(s) \, ds
$$

This is a **functional differential equation** (FDE) or **delay differential equation** (DDE), as opposed to the ordinary differential equation (ODE) governing classical control systems.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table verbatim — it is the single most testable artifact for Module 2.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Conditions | Units |
|---|----|----|----|----|
| 1 | Total Nodal Delay | $d_{total} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}$ | Always positive, additive | seconds (s) |
| 2 | Transmission Delay | $d_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = link rate (bps) | seconds (s) |
| 3 | Propagation Delay | $d_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{s}$ | $d$ = link length (m), $s$ = propagation speed ($\approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in fiber) | seconds (s) |
| 4 | M/M/1 Mean Queuing Delay | $\overline{d_{queue}} = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ | $\rho = \lambda / \mu < 1$ for stability | seconds (s) |
| 5 | Total NCS Loop Delay | $\tau = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$ | $0 \leq \tau \leq \tau_{max}$ (bounded) | seconds (s) |
| 6 | Round-Trip Time (RTT) | $RTT = 2 \cdot \tau$ (single hop) | Symmetric path assumption | seconds (s) |
| 7 | Constant Delay TF Block | $G_d(s) = e^{-\tau s}$ | For $\tau \geq 0$ (Pade approx: $e^{-\tau s} \approx \dfrac{1 - \tau s / 2}{1 + \tau s / 2}$) | dimensionless |
| 8 | Maximum Allowable Delay (MADB) — First Order System | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{1}{a}$ where plant is $\dot{x} = -ax + bu$ | Pole at $-a$ | seconds (s) |
| 9 | MADB — Second Order System | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\zeta \omega_{n}}$ (approx) for critically damped case | $0 < \zeta < 1$ | seconds (s) |
| 10 | Delay-Independent Stability | $\Re(\lambda_i) < 0 \; \forall i$ AND $\tau \geq 0$ | Plant is Hurwitz, no constraints on $\tau$ | — |
| 11 | Delay-Dependent Stability | $\tau < \tau_{cr}$ where $\tau_{cr}$ solved from transcendental equation | $\tau_{cr} = \dfrac{\theta}{\omega_c}$ | seconds (s) |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Industrial Automation (PROFINET, EtherCAT):** Engineers compute the worst-case $\tau_{max}$ to ensure the **Maximum Allowable Delay Bound (MADB)** is not violated, which would cause production-line shutdowns.
- **Automotive (CAN/LIN/FlexRay):** Delay budgets are statically allocated to ECUs (Electronic Control Units); the X-by-wire systems require deterministic delays.
- **Aerospace & UAVs:** Delay compensation via Smith Predictors and MPC relies on accurate delay models.
- **IoT and Wireless Sensor Networks:** Stochastic delay models (Markov chains) are essential for predictive control over lossy wireless links like 6LoWPAN.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically emphasizes the **two-block delay representation** ($\tau_{sc}$ and $\tau_{ca}$) introduced by **Halevi & Ray (1988)** and later extended by **Nilsson et al. (1998)**. This is a guaranteed 14-mark question every cycle.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: End-to-End Delay from First Principles

We derive the total time elapsed from when a sensor samples a plant output to when the actuator receives the corresponding control command.

**Step 1 — Define the lifecycle stages.** A single sample travels through four sequential stages:

1. **Sampling & Encoding** at the sensor node.
2. **Network transmission** across the sensor-to-controller link.
3. **Computation** of the control law at the controller node.
4. **Network transmission** across the controller-to-actuator link.

**Step 2 — Express the time at each stage.**

Let $t_0$ be the sampling instant. The packet enters the network at $t_0 + t_{encode}$. Let the network traversal for stage 2 take $\tau_{sc}$ seconds (sensor-to-controller), the controller computation take $t_c$ seconds, and the return traversal take $\tau_{ca}$ seconds (controller-to-actuator).

**Step 3 — Sum the delays.**

The instant the actuator receives the control command is:

$$
t_{actuator} \;=\; t_0 \;+\; t_{encode} \;+\; \tau_{sc} \;+\; t_c \;+\; \tau_{ca}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the effective network-induced delay.**

$$
\tau(t) \;\triangleq\; \tau_{sc}(t) \;+\; \tau_{ca}(t) \;\approx\; t_{actuator} \;-\; t_0
$$

where $t_{encode}$ and $t_c$ are lumped into the respective network delays or absorbed into the controller processing block. This yields the **canonical NCS delay model**.

**Step 5 — Express the closed-loop dynamics.**

For a linear time-invariant plant $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$, the actuator command is the delayed controller output:

$$
u(t) \;=\; K \, x(t - \tau(t))
$$

Substituting:

$$
\dot{x}(t) \;=\; A x(t) \;+\; B K \, x(t - \tau(t))
$$

This is a **Delay Differential Equation (DDE)** — the foundation of all NCS analysis.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean Queuing Delay for M/M/1 Queue

We derive $\overline{d_{queue}} = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ from the steady-state queue length.

**Step 1 — State the assumptions.**
- Poisson arrivals with rate $\lambda$ (packets/sec).
- Exponential service with rate $\mu$ (packets/sec).
- Single server, infinite buffer.
- Traffic intensity $\rho = \lambda / \mu < 1$.

**Step 2 — Steady-state probability of $n$ customers in system.**

For an M/M/1 queue, the birth-death chain yields the geometric distribution:

$$
P_n \;=\; (1 - \rho) \, \rho^n
$$

**Step 3 — Expected number of customers in service (including one in service).**

$$
L_s \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \, P_n \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n (1 - \rho) \rho^n
$$

**Step 4 — Use the geometric series identity.**

Recall that $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n \, \rho^n = \dfrac{\rho}{(1-\rho)^2}$.

Therefore:

$$
L_s \;=\; (1-\rho) \cdot \dfrac{\rho}{(1-\rho)^2} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho}{1-\rho}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Little's Law ($L = \lambda W$).**

$$
\overline{d_{queue}} \;=\; W \;=\; \dfrac{L_s}{\lambda} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho}{\lambda(1-\rho)}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\rho = \lambda / \mu$.**

$$
\overline{d_{queue}} \;=\; \dfrac{\lambda / \mu}{\lambda (1 - \lambda/\mu)} \;=\; \dfrac{1 / \mu}{1 - \lambda/\mu} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda}
$$

This completes the derivation of the **M/M/1 mean queuing delay**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Allowable Delay Bound (MADB) for a First-Order System

We derive the critical delay bound beyond which a closed-loop first-order NCS becomes unstable.

**Step 1 — Plant and controller.**

Consider the scalar plant:

$$
\dot{x}(t) \;=\; -a \, x(t) \;+\; b \, u(t), \quad a, b > 0
$$

with state-feedback controller $u(t) = -k \, x(t - \tau)$ and $k > 0$.

**Step 2 — Closed-loop DDE.**

$$
\dot{x}(t) \;=\; -a \, x(t) \;-\; b k \, x(t - \tau)
$$

**Step 3 — Assume exponential solution $x(t) = e^{s t}$.**

Substitution gives the **characteristic equation**:

$$
s \;=\; -a \;-\; b k \, e^{-\tau s}
$$

**Step 4 — Purely imaginary root condition for stability boundary.**

Let $s = j \omega$. Then:

$$
j \omega \;=\; -a \;-\; b k \, e^{-j \omega \tau}
$$

$$
j \omega + a \;=\; -b k \, [\cos(\omega \tau) - j \sin(\omega \tau)]
$$

**Step 5 — Separate real and imaginary parts.**

Real part: $\quad a = -b k \cos(\omega \tau) \Rightarrow \cos(\omega \tau) = -\dfrac{a}{b k}$

Imaginary part: $\quad \omega = b k \sin(\omega \tau) \Rightarrow \sin(\omega \tau) = \dfrac{\omega}{b k}$

**Step 6 — Apply the trigonometric identity $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$.**

$$
\left(\dfrac{a}{b k}\right)^2 \;+\; \left(\dfrac{\omega}{b k}\right)^2 \;=\; 1
$$

$$
\omega^2 \;=\; (bk)^2 - a^2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega_c = \sqrt{(bk)^2 - a^2}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for critical delay $\tau_{cr}$.**

From $\cos(\omega_c \tau_{cr}) = -\dfrac{a}{b k}$:

$$
\omega_c \tau_{cr} \;=\; \arccos\!\left(-\dfrac{a}{b k}\right) \;=\; \pi - \arccos\!\left(\dfrac{a}{b k}\right)
$$

$$
\tau_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi - \arccos\!\left(\dfrac{a}{b k}\right)}{\sqrt{(bk)^2 - a^2}}
$$

**Step 8 — Stability condition.**

The closed-loop system is stable if and only if $\tau < \tau_{cr}$. This is the **Maximum Allowable Delay Bound (MADB)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Test (KTU-style):** For $a = 1$, $b = 1$, $k = 2$: $\tau_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi - \arccos(1/2)}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{\pi - \pi/3}{\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{2\pi / 3}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.209$ seconds.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an NCS with Network Delays

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **information-flow architecture** of a closed-loop NCS, showing where each delay component is injected into the loop. This replaces a physical circuit diagram because the system is fundamentally distributed across a network.

```mermaid
    E -->|Delay t ca| F[Actuator Node]
    F -->|Apply control u t| A

    B -.->|Processing d proc| B
    C -.->|Queuing d queue plus Transmission d trans plus Propagation d prop| C
    E -.->|Queuing d queue plus Transmission d trans plus Propagation d prop| E

    G[Disturbance w t] --> A
    H[Reference r t] --> D
```

This diagram models the lifecycle of a single data packet as it traverses the four delay stages within a generic network node.

```mermaid
    Arrival --> Processing: d proc constant
    Processing --> Queuing: Wait for link availability

    Propagation --> [*]: Bits arrive at next node

    Queuing --> Dropped: Buffer overflow
    Dropped --> [*]: Packet loss event

    note right of Queuing
        Dominant source of
        stochastic variability.

    end note
```
