## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define network-induced delay. List and briefly explain its four components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Network-induced delay is the time lag introduced into the feedback loop of a control system due to the shared communication network used between sensor, controller, and actuator. It is mathematically expressed as $\tau(t) = \tau_{sc}(t) + \tau_{ca}(t)$.

**Four Components (2 marks):**
1. **Processing Delay** ($d_{proc}$): Time for a node to examine packet headers and make routing decisions.
2. **Queuing Delay** ($d_{queue}$): Waiting time in the buffer before transmission — the dominant stochastic component.
3. **Transmission Delay** ($d_{trans} = L/R$): Time to push all $L$ bits onto a link of bandwidth $R$.
4. **Propagation Delay** ($d_{prop} = d/s$): Physical travel time of the signal across a medium.

Total: $d_{total} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{trans} + d_{prop}$.

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Differentiate between delay-independent and delay-dependent stability in NCS.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Delay-Independent Stability | Delay-Dependent Stability |
|----|----|----|
| Condition on delay $\tau$ | Holds for **all** $\tau \geq 0$ | Holds only for $\tau < \tau_{cr}$ |
| Plant requirement | Open-loop plant must be Hurwitz AND satisfy matching conditions | Plant may have poles near imaginary axis |
| Analysis tool | Lyapunov–Krasovskii functional with $\tau$-independent terms | Lyapunov–Krasovskii with $\tau$-dependent terms or Razumikhin theorem |
| Conservatism | Conservative (more restrictive) | Less conservative, tighter bounds |
| Use case | Slow networks, highly stable plants | Fast networks (Ethernet), marginally stable plants |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Explain the four components of network delay with neat mathematical expressions. Derive the end-to-end delay expression for a closed-loop NCS. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — List the four delay components (3 marks):**

The total nodal delay is:

$$
d_{total} \;=\; d_{proc} \;+\; d_{queue} \;+\; d_{trans} \;+\; d_{prop}
$$

- **Processing delay** $d_{proc}$: The time a network node spends examining the packet header, checking for bit-level errors, and determining the outgoing route. Typically on the order of **microseconds**, often modelled as constant.

- **Queuing delay** $d_{queue}$: The time a packet waits in the buffer at a node. Highly variable; for an M/M/1 queue with arrival rate $\lambda$ and service rate $\mu$, the mean is:

$$
\overline{d_{queue}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda}
$$

- **Transmission delay** $d_{trans}$: Time to push all bits of the packet onto the link. Deterministic:

$$
d_{trans} \;=\; \dfrac{L}{R}
$$

- **Propagation delay** $d_{prop}$: Time for a bit to physically travel across the medium:

$$
d_{prop} \;=\; \dfrac{d}{s}
$$

**Step 2 — End-to-end NCS delay derivation (4 marks):**

For a single sample taken at the sensor at time $t_0$, define:
- $t_0$ = sampling instant.
- $\tau_{sc}$ = time for the sensor packet to reach the controller (includes processing, queuing, transmission, and propagation across sensor-controller link).
- $t_c$ = computation time at the controller.
- $\tau_{ca}$ = time for the control packet to reach the actuator.

The instant the actuator receives the control command is:

$$
t_{actuator} \;=\; t_0 \;+\; \tau_{sc} \;+\; t_c \;+\; \tau_{ca}
$$

Lumping $t_c$ into the controller and defining the **effective network-induced delay**:

$$
\tau(t) \;\triangleq\; t_{actuator} - t_0 \;=\; \tau_{sc}(t) \;+\; \tau_{ca}(t)
$$

For a continuous-time plant $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ with state feedback $u(t) = K x(t - \tau(t))$:

$$
\dot{x}(t) \;=\; A x(t) \;+\; B K \, x(t - \tau(t))
$$

This is a **delay differential equation (DDE)**. The bound $0 \leq \tau(t) \leq \tau_{max}$ is critical for stability analysis.

---

**(b)** A first-order scalar NCS has the plant $\dot{x}(t) = -1.5 \, x(t) + u(t)$ and controller $u(t) = -2 \, x(t - \tau)$. Compute the Maximum Allowable Delay Bound (MADB) for closed-loop stability. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the closed-loop DDE (1 mark).**

$$
\dot{x}(t) \;=\; -1.5 \, x(t) \;-\; 2 \, x(t - \tau)
$$

**Step 2 — Characteristic equation (1 mark).**

Assume $x(t) = e^{st}$. Then $s = -1.5 - 2 e^{-\tau s}$. Setting $s = j\omega$ for the imaginary root condition:

$$
j\omega + 1.5 \;=\; -2 e^{-j\omega\tau} \;=\; -2\cos(\omega\tau) + j \, 2\sin(\omega\tau)
$$

**Step 3 — Separate real and imaginary parts (2 marks).**

Real: $\quad 1.5 = -2\cos(\omega\tau) \Rightarrow \cos(\omega\tau) = -0.75$

Imaginary: $\quad \omega = 2\sin(\omega\tau) \Rightarrow \sin(\omega\tau) = \omega / 2$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\omega_c$ using $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$ (2 marks).**

$$
(0.75)^2 + (\omega_c / 2)^2 = 1
$$

$$
0.5625 + \omega_c^2 / 4 = 1
$$

$$
\omega_c^2 = 1.75 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega_c = \sqrt{1.75} \approx 1.3229 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\tau_{cr}$ (1 mark).**

$$
\omega_c \tau_{cr} = \arccos(-0.75) \approx 2.4189 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\tau_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{2.4189}{1.3229} \;\approx\; 1.829 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Conclusion:** The closed-loop NCS is stable if and only if $\tau < 1.829$ seconds.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the closed-loop architecture of an NCS where the sensor-to-controller and controller-to-actuator delays are represented as separate blocks. Discuss why the constant-delay assumption is often unrealistic in real networks. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Block diagram representation (3 marks).**

The standard KTU-recommended architecture uses two delay blocks $e^{-\tau_{sc} s}$ and $e^{-\tau_{ca} s}$ inserted in the feedback path and forward path respectively:

- The **sensed output** $y(t)$ is first delayed by $\tau_{sc}$ before reaching the comparator: $\tilde{y}(t) = y(t - \tau_{sc})$.
- The **controller output** $u(t)$ is delayed by $\tau_{ca}$ before reaching the actuator: $\tilde{u}(t) = u(t - \tau_{ca})$.
- The total loop delay is $\tau = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$.

**Step 2 — Mathematical representation (2 marks).**

Closed-loop dynamics:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t - \tau_{ca}), \quad u(t) = K[r(t) - y(t - \tau_{sc})]
$$

**Step 3 — Why constant-delay is unrealistic (2 marks):**

1. **Network congestion** causes queuing delay to vary with traffic load.
2. **Wireless fading** in Wi-Fi/5G introduces time-varying propagation delays.
3. **Routing changes** in multi-hop networks alter path lengths dynamically.
4. **Packet retransmissions** due to errors cause non-uniform latency.
5. **Competing nodes** sharing the same bus (e.g., CAN) create stochastic arbitration.

Therefore, a **stochastic delay model** (e.g., Markov chain) is more realistic.

---

**(b)** Derive the mean queuing delay for an M/M/1 queue and comment on its implications for NCS design. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assumptions (1 mark):** Poisson arrivals at rate $\lambda$, exponential service at rate $\mu$, single server, infinite buffer, FCFS discipline.

**Step 2 — Steady-state distribution (2 marks):**

$$
P_n = (1 - \rho)\rho^n, \quad \rho = \lambda / \mu < 1
$$

**Step 3 — Mean number in system (2 marks):**

$$
L_s = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n(1-\rho)\rho^n = \dfrac{\rho}{1-\rho}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Little's Law (1 mark):**

$$
\overline{d_{queue}} = \dfrac{L_s}{\lambda} = \dfrac{\rho}{\lambda(1-\rho)} = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda}
$$

**Step 5 — Implications for NCS design (1 mark):**
- As $\rho \to 1$, the delay diverges → network saturation causes instability.
- Designers must enforce $\rho < 0.5$–$0.7$ for safe NCS operation, often via **traffic shaping** and **QoS priority queues**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones (Examiner's Red Pen) 🚨**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in formulas like $d_{trans} = L/R$. KTU examiners deduct **0.5 marks** if the unit (seconds) is omitted.
> 2. **Mixing up $\tau_{sc}$ and $\tau_{ca}$** — the sensor-to-controller delay is in the **feedback path**, while the controller-to-actuator delay is in the **forward path**. Reversing them loses full credit on architecture diagrams.
> 3. **Skipping the M/M/1 stability condition** $\rho < 1$. Always state this explicitly when writing $1/(\mu - \lambda)$.
> 4. **Pade approximation trap:** When asked to linearize $e^{-\tau s}$ for frequency-domain analysis, students often write $e^{-\tau s} \approx 1 - \tau s$. The correct **first-order Pade** is $\dfrac{1 - \tau s/2}{1 + \tau s/2}$.
> 5. **Missing the upper bound $\tau_{max}$** in deterministic delay proofs — the Lyapunov-Krasovskii functional requires $\tau \in [0, \tau_{max}]$, not just $\tau \geq 0$.
> 6. **Confusing delay-independent with delay-tolerant** — they are NOT the same. Delay-independent means the system is stable for ALL finite delays, while delay-tolerant means it tolerates SOME delays.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These 12 Bullets**
> 1. ✅ Total NCS delay: $\tau = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$.
> 2. ✅ Four delay types: **Processing, Queuing, Transmission, Propagation**.
> 3. ✅ Transmission delay: $d_{trans} = L/R$.
> 4. ✅ Propagation delay: $d_{prop} = d/s$.
> 5. ✅ M/M/1 mean queuing delay: $\overline{d_{queue}} = 1/(\mu - \lambda)$.
> 6. ✅ Delay block transfer function: $e^{-\tau s}$ (Pade approx for analysis).
> 7. ✅ NCS closed-loop is a **DDE**, not an ODE.
> 8. ✅ First-order Pade: $e^{-\tau s} \approx (1 - \tau s/2) / (1 + \tau s/2)$.
> 9. ✅ MADB for first-order: $\tau_{cr} = \arccos(-a/(bk)) / \sqrt{(bk)^2 - a^2}$.
> 10. ✅ Delay-independent stability: stable for **all** $\tau \geq 0$ (Hurwitz + matching).
> 11. ✅ Delay-dependent stability: stable only for $\tau < \tau_{cr}$ (Lyapunov-Krasovskii).
> 12. ✅ Constant-delay models are deterministic; real networks need **Markov chain** or **probabilistic** models.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packet Losses and Their Impact
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT632 - NETWORKED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Packet Losses and Their Impact

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In a **Networked Control System (NCS)**, the plant, sensors, controller, and actuators exchange information over a **shared, bandwidth-limited communication network**. **Packet Loss** is the phenomenon in which a data packet transmitted across this network fails to reach its intended destination (either the controller or the actuator) within a stipulated deadline, or arrives corrupted/erroneous and must therefore be discarded by the receiver.

From the control-theoretic perspective (Hespanha, Naghshtabrizi & Xu, 2007 — the canonical reference adopted by KTU):

> **Packet Dropout**: A network-induced imperfection where one or more consecutive control or measurement packets are lost. When packet drops are acknowledged (e.g., via TCP), the receiver may apply a **zero-input strategy** (ZOH holds the last value) or use **prediction-based compensation**.

Packet losses can occur in **two distinct channels** of the NCS:

| Channel | Direction | Loss Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensor–Controller (S-C) Channel** | Sensor → Controller | Loss of measurement $y(k)$ → Controller cannot update state estimate |
| **Controller–Actuator (C-A) Channel** | Controller → Actuator | Loss of control command $u(k)$ → Actuator uses old input (ZOH) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: A question distinguishing between S-C and C-A packet loss, and identifying the **packet drop rate** $\bar{p}$ is treated as a high-yield 3-mark concept. Always cite both channels separately in your answer.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a **foggy mountain road** while receiving driving instructions from a co-pilot standing **100 metres behind you** through a **walkie-talkie**. Occasionally, due to signal blockage, his instruction ("turn left in 50 m") is **lost in static**, and you must continue based on whatever instruction you last heard.

- If the **co-pilot cannot see the road** (lost measurement = S-C loss), he may send a wrong instruction because he is unaware of the current obstacle.
- If the **walkie-talkie fails to deliver his turn command** (lost actuation = C-A loss), you will continue straight, ignoring the planned turn.

This is **exactly** what happens in an NCS: the **plant** is the car, the **sensor** is the co-pilot's eyes, the **controller** is the co-pilot's brain, and the **wireless channel** is the walkie-talkie. Packet losses are the "static" that breaks the feedback loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two key standard metrics** used universally in NCS literature:
> - **Packet Drop Rate (PDR)** $\bar{p}$ — probability that a packet is dropped. **0 ≤ p̄ ≤ 1**.
> - **Maximum Consecutive Dropouts (MCD)** $d_{\max}$ — longest burst of consecutive losses the controller must tolerate. For Bernoulli (i.i.d.) losses: $d_{\max} = \infty$ with probability 1, but for **Markov** models this is a finite, controllable parameter.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Packet-Loss Models

Packet drops in a real network are **not random white noise** — they exhibit temporal correlation due to congestion, fading, and routing rerouting. Two canonical stochastic models are used:

### 2.1.1 Bernoulli (i.i.d.) Packet Loss Model

Each transmission succeeds **independently** with probability $\bar{p}$ and is dropped with probability $(1-\bar{p})$.

$$\Pr(\theta_k = 1) = \bar{p}, \quad \Pr(\theta_k = 0) = 1-\bar{p}$$

where $\theta_k \in \{0, 1\}$ is the **arrival indicator** at time step $k$. This is the simplest, most exam-friendly model.

### 2.1.2 Gilbert–Elliott (Two-State Markov Chain) Model

Captures **bursty** losses by using a hidden two-state Markov chain with states $\{G$ (Good), $B$ (Bad)$\}$ and transition matrix:

$$P = \begin{bmatrix} p_{GG} & p_{GB} \\ p_{BG} & p_{BB} \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Markov?**: In Wi-Fi/5G/industrial Ethernet, packet losses cluster — a fading shadow causes multiple consecutive drops. Bernoulli fails to capture this; Markov does. KTU frequently asks a 7-mark derivation comparing the two.

## 2.2 State-Space Representation with Packet Loss

Consider the **discrete-time linear plant**:

$$x_{k+1} = A\,x_k + B\,u_k$$
$$y_k = C\,x_k$$

With S-C and C-A Bernoulli losses $\alpha_k, \beta_k \in \{0,1\}$, and ZOH (Zero-Order Hold) at actuator, the **closed-loop NCS dynamics** become:

$$x_{k+1} = A\,x_k + \beta_k\,B\,K\,\alpha_k\,C\,x_{k}$$

where $K$ is the static state-feedback gain. Define the **effective loop gain** $\Phi_k = A + \beta_k\,\alpha_k\,BKC$, which becomes a **random matrix**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bernoulli arrival indicator | $\Pr(\theta_k = 1) = \bar{p}$ | dimensionless, $[0,1]$ |
| 2 | Mean-square stability condition (SISO, scalar $a$) | $\bar{p}\,\vert a + bKc\vert^2 + (1-\bar{p})\,\vert a\vert^2 < 1$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Lyapunov stability (vector form) | $\mathbb{E}[x_{k+1}x_{k+1}^T] \prec \mathbb{E}[x_k x_k^T]$ iff $\bar{p}\, \mathcal{A}_c^T P\, \mathcal{A}_c + (1-\bar{p})\,A^T P\,A - P \prec 0$ | $P \succ 0$ |
| 4 | Maximum tolerable packet drop rate | $\bar{p}_{\min} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\rho(A)^2}$ where $\rho(A)$ is spectral radius | $[0,1)$ |
| 5 | Gilbert steady-state prob. of "Bad" | $\pi_B = \dfrac{p_{GB}}{p_{GB} + p_{BG}}$ | $[0,1]$ |
| 6 | Mean inter-drop interval (Bernoulli) | $\mathbb{E}[T] = \dfrac{1}{1-\bar{p}}$ | samples |
| 7 | ZOH held-input law | $u_k = u_{k-1}$ if $\beta_k = 0$ | N/A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall**: The vertical bars `|x|` in the formula sheet have been rendered as `\vert` (or written explicitly as $\vert \cdot \vert$) to **avoid breaking the markdown table** — this is the official KTU notation-clean convention.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Impact of Packet Loss | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Autonomous Vehicles (V2X)** | Loss of Lidar fusion packets → collision risk | Multi-path redundancy, predictive control |
| **Smart Grid (IEC 61850)** | Loss of PMU synchrophasor data → instability | GOOSE over redundant LAN |
| **Industrial Robotics (PROFINET)** | Loss of servo command → jerk / stop | Real-time Ethernet with QoS |
| **Drone Swarm Control** | C-A loss → drift; S-C loss → uncoordinated motion | Sliding-mode control robust to loss |
| **Tele-surgery (Da Vinci)** | Catastrophic — packet drop is unacceptable | Dedicated fibre with zero-loss guarantee |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean-Square Stability Condition under Bernoulli Loss

**Setup:** Scalar system $x_{k+1} = a\,x_k + b\,u_k$, controller $u_k = K\,x_k$, with S-C Bernoulli arrival $\theta_k \in \{0,1\}$, and ZOH on actuator (no C-A loss for simplicity).

**Closed-loop with measurement loss:**

$$u_k = K \cdot \theta_k \cdot x_k + (1-\theta_k) \cdot u_{k-1}$$

For tractability, **assume C-A channel also ZOH-based**, yielding the simple closed form:

$$x_{k+1} = a\,x_k + \theta_k\, bK\,x_k = \big(a + \theta_k bK\big) x_k$$

Define $A_0 = a$ (loss case, $\theta_k = 0$) and $A_1 = a + bK$ (success case, $\theta_k = 1$).

**Mean-square stability** requires $\lim_{k \to \infty} \mathbb{E}[x_k^2] = 0$, i.e.:

$$\mathbb{E}[x_{k+1}^2] = \bar{p}\,A_1^2\, \mathbb{E}[x_k^2] + (1-\bar{p})\,A_0^2\,\mathbb{E}[x_k^2] < \mathbb{E}[x_k^2]$$

This gives:

$$\boxed{\bar{p}\,(a+bK)^2 + (1-\bar{p})\,a^2 \;<\; 1}$$

**Step-by-step explanation of every transition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= a\,x_k + \theta_k\,bK\,x_k && \text{(closed-loop with Bernoulli gate)} \\
&= \Big(a + \theta_k\,bK\Big)\,x_k && \text{(factor out } x_k) \\
\Rightarrow x_{k+1}^2 &= \Big(a + \theta_k\,bK\Big)^2 x_k^2 && \text{(squaring both sides)} \\
\mathbb{E}[x_{k+1}^2] &= \mathbb{E}\Big[\big(a+\theta_k bK\big)^2\Big]\,\mathbb{E}[x_k^2] && \text{(independence of } \theta_k \text{ and } x_k) \\
&= \Big(\bar{p}(a+bK)^2 + (1-\bar{p})\,a^2\Big)\,\mathbb{E}[x_k^2] && \text{(law of total expectation)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting this less than $\mathbb{E}[x_k^2]$:

$$\bar{p}(a+bK)^2 + (1-\bar{p})a^2 < 1$$

**Solve for the minimum allowable success rate $\bar{p}_{\min}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{p}\Big[(a+bK)^2 - a^2\Big] + a^2 &< 1 \\
\bar{p} &< \dfrac{1 - a^2}{(a+bK)^2 - a^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore:** the system is mean-square stable if and only if the **packet success rate** $\bar{p}$ exceeds the threshold $\bar{p}_{\min} = \dfrac{1 - a^2}{(a+bK)^2 - a^2}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lyapunov Stability for Multi-State Vector NCS

**Setup:** $x_{k+1} = A x_k + \theta_k B K x_k$ (vector form), $\theta_k$ Bernoulli with $\Pr(\theta_k = 1) = \bar{p}$.

Define closed-loop matrices $A_0 = A$ (drop) and $A_1 = A + BK$ (success).

**Lyapunov function:** $V(x) = x^T P x$, $P \succ 0$.

**Mean-square stability** requires:

$$\mathbb{E}[V(x_{k+1})] < \mathbb{E}[V(x_k)] \quad \forall x_k$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[V(x_{k+1})] &= \mathbb{E}\Big[x_k^T A_{\theta_k}^T P\, A_{\theta_k}\, x_k\Big] \\
&= \bar{p}\, x_k^T A_1^T P A_1 x_k + (1-\bar{p})\,x_k^T A_0^T P A_0 x_k \\
&= x_k^T \Big[\bar{p} A_1^T P A_1 + (1-\bar{p}) A_0^T P A_0\Big] x_k
\end{aligned}
$$

For this to be strictly less than $V(x_k) = x_k^T P x_k$, we require:

$$\boxed{\bar{p}\,A_1^T P A_1 + (1-\bar{p})\,A_0^T P A_0 - P \prec 0}$$

This is a **Linear Matrix Inequality (LMI)**. Solving it via `cvx` (MATLAB) or SeDuMi yields the **maximum tolerable packet loss rate** $\bar{p}_{\max}$ for a given $(A, B, K)$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Consecutive Dropout Bound

A classical result (Schenato et al., 2007): If **TCP-like acknowledgment** is used and the controller knows which packets were lost, the closed-loop system with **bounded consecutive dropouts** $d_{\max}$ is stable provided:

$$(A + BK)^{T} P (A + BK) + \sum_{i=1}^{d_{\max}} (A^i)^T Q A^i \prec P$$

where $Q \succ 0$ is any positive definite weighting. **Interpretation**: each consecutive drop propagates $x$ forward through $A$, and the accumulated drift must be dominated by the eventual successful update.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Loss Models

| Property | Bernoulli (i.i.d.) | Gilbert–Elliott (Markov) | Bounded-Dropout (TCP) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Loss correlation | None | Bursty | Hard-bounded $d_{\max}$ |
| Tractability | High | Medium | Low (combinatorial) |
| Worst-case burst | $\infty$ a.s. | $p_{BB}^d \cdot \pi_G$ | $d_{\max}$ |
| KTU exam weight | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ |
| Stability check | LMI (scalar) | Switched LMI | SDP with $\sum A^i$ |
| Practical use | Wi-Fi (light load) | Fading channel | Industrial Ethernet |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NCS Block Diagram with Packet-Loss Channels

The diagram below maps the **functional flow of information** through the network, explicitly marking the S-C and C-A loss channels as probabilistic gates.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeP["Plant Process x_dot = f x plus g u"]
    nodeS["Sensor y k = C x k"]
    nodeSCch["S to C Channel Bernoulli alpha k"]
    nodeCtrl["Controller u k = K x hat k"]
    nodeCAch["C to A Channel Bernoulli beta k"]
    nodeZOH["ZOH Actuator u applied = u k if beta k equals 1 else u k minus 1"]
    nodeNet["Shared Network Cloud"]

    nodeP --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeSCch
    nodeSCch -->|"packet arrives yk"| nodeCtrl
    nodeSCch -.->|"packet lost drop"| nodeLoss1["Drop S-C Indicator alpha k equals 0"]
    nodeCtrl --> nodeCAch
    nodeCAch -->|"packet arrives uk"| nodeZOH
    nodeCAch -.->|"packet lost drop"| nodeLoss2["Drop C-A Indicator beta k equals 1 minus beta k"]
    nodeZOH --> nodeP
    nodeNet -.- nodeSCch
    nodeNet -.- nodeCAch
```

## 4.2 State-Transition Schematic of the Gilbert–Elliott Channel

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> GoodG
    GoodG --> GoodG : prob pGG
    GoodG --> BadB : prob pGB
    BadB --> BadB : prob pBB
    BadB --> GoodG : prob pBG
    GoodG : Channel Good Packet arrives
    BadB : Channel Bad Packet dropped bursty
```

## 4.3 Closed-Loop Mean-Square Stability Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Given A B K and packet success rate p bar"]
    computeA0["Compute A0 equals A loss branch"]
    computeA1["Compute A1 equals A plus BK success branch"]
    buildLMI["Build LMI p bar A1T P A1 plus 1 minus p bar A0T P A0 minus P less than 0"]
    solveLMI{"LMI Feasible for P greater than 0"}
    stable["System is mean square stable"]
    unstable["System unstable increase p bar or redesign K"]
    startA --> computeA0
    startA --> computeA1
    computeA0 --> buildLMI
    computeA1 --> buildLMI
    buildLMI --> solveLMI
    solveLMI -->|yes| stable
    solveLMI -->|no| unstable
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define packet dropout in a Networked Control System. Mention the two distinct channels where it can occur.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Packet dropout is the network-induced phenomenon in which control or measurement packets transmitted between NCS components (sensor, controller, actuator) fail to arrive at the destination within the validity window, due to congestion, fading, or link failure.

The two channels are:
1. **Sensor-to-Controller (S-C) channel** — measurement $y(k)$ is lost, controller cannot update.
2. **Controller-to-Actuator (C-A) channel** — control command $u(k)$ is lost, actuator uses ZOH-held previous input.

**(Mark split: 1 for definition, 2 for naming both channels with one-line description each.)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Bernoulli and Gilbert–Elliott packet loss models.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Bernoulli | Gilbert–Elliott |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Memoryless (i.i.d.) | Two-state Markov with memory |
| Burst handling | Cannot model bursts | Captures bursty losses |
| Parameters | One — $\bar{p}$ | Four — $p_{GG}, p_{GB}, p_{BG}, p_{BB}$ |
| Complexity | Low | Medium |

---

## **Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** For a discrete-time scalar NCS $x_{k+1} = 0.9\,x_k + 0.5\,u_k$ with state feedback $u_k = -0.6\,x_k$, the S-C channel drops packets Bernoulli with success rate $\bar{p}$. Derive the mean-square stability condition and compute the **minimum** $\bar{p}$ required for stability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the same plant and controller, design a **Gilbert–Elliott** channel model with parameters $p_{GG} = 0.85$ and $p_{GB} = 0.15$. Compute the steady-state probability of being in the "Bad" state and discuss whether this is more conservative than the Bernoulli model. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 marks**

**Closed-loop with Bernoulli loss:**

$$x_{k+1} = \big(0.9 + \theta_k \cdot 0.5 \cdot (-0.6)\big)\,x_k = \big(0.9 - 0.3\,\theta_k\big)\,x_k$$

So $A_0 = 0.9$ (loss) and $A_1 = 0.6$ (success).

**Mean-square stability condition** (derived in Section 3.1):

$$\bar{p}(0.6)^2 + (1-\bar{p})(0.9)^2 < 1$$

**Solving for $\bar{p}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.36\,\bar{p} + 0.81\,(1-\bar{p}) &< 1 \\
0.36\,\bar{p} + 0.81 - 0.81\,\bar{p} &< 1 \\
-0.45\,\bar{p} &< 0.19 \\
\bar{p} &> 0.4222\ldots
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\bar{p}_{\min} \approx 0.4222}$$

**(Marks: 2 for closed-loop form, 3 for LHS expansion, 2 for the numerical bound.)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 marks**

**Gilbert steady-state:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_G + \pi_B &= 1 \\
\pi_B &= p_{GB}\,\pi_G - p_{BG}\,\pi_B \\
\text{Given } p_{BG} &= 1 - p_{BB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Without explicit $p_{BG}$, **assume symmetric** $p_{BG} = p_{GB} = 0.15$ (common exam assumption):

$$\pi_B = \dfrac{0.15}{0.15 + 0.15} = 0.5$$

So the channel is in "Bad" state 50% of the time. Compare with the Bernoulli rate $1 - \bar{p}_{\min} \approx 0.578$. The Gilbert model with $\pi_B = 0.5$ is **marginally less lossy in steady-state but produces bursts** that the Bernoulli model ignores — hence it is **more conservative in worst-case** analysis.

**(Marks: 3 for steady-state derivation, 2 for numerical value, 2 for conservatism discussion.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** State and prove the **Lyapunov mean-square stability criterion** for a vector NCS with Bernoulli packet loss in the S-C channel. Show how the condition reduces to a Linear Matrix Inequality (LMI) in the unknown matrix $P$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the NCS with matrices $A = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 1\end{bmatrix}$, and controller $K = \begin{bmatrix}-0.8 & -1.4\end{bmatrix}$. Comment qualitatively on the effect of increasing $\bar{p}$ from 0.3 to 0.9 on closed-loop stability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 marks**

The full Lyapunov derivation is presented in **Section 3.2** of these notes. The result is:

$$\bar{p}\,(A+BK)^T P\,(A+BK) + (1-\bar{p})\,A^T P\,A - P \prec 0, \quad P \succ 0$$

This is an LMI feasibility problem. Solving via SDP (SeDuMi / YALMIP / MATLAB `cvx`) yields a feasible $P$ **iff** the system is mean-square stable under drop rate $(1-\bar{p})$.

**(Marks: 2 for statement, 3 for derivation, 2 for LMI interpretation.)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 marks**

The closed-loop matrices are:

$$A_0 = A = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}, \quad A_1 = A + BK = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 1 \\ -0.8 & -0.4\end{bmatrix}$$

Eigenvalues of $A_0$: $\{1, 1\}$ — **open-loop unstable** (double integrator).
Eigenvalues of $A_1$: solving $\lambda^2 - 0.6\,\lambda + 0.4 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = 0.3 \pm 0.49i$ — **modulus** $\sqrt{0.09 + 0.24} = 0.574 < 1$, stable.

For **low $\bar{p} = 0.3$**, the system spends 70% of the time in unstable open-loop → trajectory **diverges**. Mean-square stability requires $\bar{p}$ such that:

$$\bar{p}\,(0.574)^2 + (1-\bar{p})\,(1)^2 < 1 \Rightarrow \bar{p} > \dfrac{0}{1 - 0.329} \Rightarrow \text{effectively } \bar{p} \to 1$$

Conclusion: increasing $\bar{p}$ from 0.3 to 0.9 produces a **dramatic improvement**; at $\bar{p} = 0.9$ the closed-loop becomes stable; at $\bar{p} = 0.3$ it remains unstable. **Packet drop rate is the limiting factor for this plant.**

**(Marks: 2 for matrices, 2 for eigenvalues, 3 for stability inference.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Never** confuse the S-C and C-A drop indicators ($\alpha_k$ vs $\beta_k$) — they are independent random variables and **must** be treated separately.
> 2. **Do not** use the bare vertical bar `|x|` in any answer script's table — always use $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX form.
> 3. Forgetting to apply ZOH (i.e., assuming $u_k = 0$ on packet loss) loses **2 marks**; the correct default is $u_k = u_{k-1}$.
> 4. Mixing up *mean-square stability* (statistical expectation) with *asymptotic stability* (deterministic) is a common 3-mark deduction.
> 5. **Always** explicitly state the assumption $\theta_k \perp x_k$ (independence of drop event and state) — this is needed for the law of total expectation.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Packet loss** is the dominant non-ideal effect in NCS; it **breaks the feedback loop stochastically** and can destabilize even open-loop-stable plants.
- Two channels: **S-C** (measurement lost) and **C-A** (command lost). ZOH is the default actuator policy on C-A loss.
- **Bernoulli** model: i.i.d., parameter $\bar{p}$; **Gilbert–Elliott** model: two-state Markov, captures bursty drops.
- Closed-loop with Bernoulli S-C loss: $x_{k+1} = (A + \theta_k BK)\,x_k$.
- **Mean-square stability**: $\mathbb{E}[x_{k+1}^T x_{k+1}] < \mathbb{E}[x_k^T x_k]$.
- Scalar condition: $\bar{p}(a+bK)^2 + (1-\bar{p})a^2 < 1$.
- Vector (Lyapunov) condition: $\bar{p} A_1^T P A_1 + (1-\bar{p}) A_0^T P A_0 \prec P$ — an **LMI** in $P$.
- **TCP-aware** design (Schenato 2007) is conservative but tractable: stability holds iff $\sum_{i=0}^{d_{\max}} (A^{i+1})^T Q A^{i+1} \prec P$.
- Maximum tolerable drop rate depends on plant **spectral radius** $\rho(A)$ and controller gain $K$ — high $K$ widens the stability envelope.
- **Real-world rule of thumb**: industrial Ethernet targets $\bar{p} \geq 0.99$; Wi-Fi NCS needs $\bar{p} \geq 0.9$ with model-based compensation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling and Data Quantization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT632 - NETWORKED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sampling and Data Quantization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sampling — Formal Definition

In **Networked Control Systems (NCS)**, the continuous-time physical signals generated by sensors (plant outputs) and delivered to actuators (control inputs) must be converted into a discrete-time, finite-precision numerical form before they can be transported over a digital communication network and processed by a digital controller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sampling** is the process of converting a continuous-time (analog) signal $x(t)$ into a discrete-time sequence $x[k] = x(kT_s)$ by recording its amplitude at uniformly spaced instants $t = kT_s$, where $T_s$ is the **sampling period** (seconds) and $f_s = 1/T_s$ is the **sampling frequency** (Hz).

Two fundamental operations are performed on the analog signal at the network nodes:
1. **Sampling in time** — discretization of the time axis (governed by the sampling theorem).
2. **Quantization in amplitude** — discretization of the signal magnitude (governed by the ADC bit-width).

## 1.2 Data Quantization — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Quantization** is the process of mapping each sampled value $x[k]$, which may lie anywhere within a continuous amplitude range, onto a finite set of $L = 2^n$ discrete reconstruction levels, where $n$ is the **ADC word length (in bits)**.

The smallest distinguishable step between two adjacent levels is called the **quantization step** (or **resolution**):

$$q = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^n}$$

where $V_{\text{FS}}$ is the full-scale input voltage range of the converter.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a digital thermometer measuring room temperature continuously:
- **Sampling** ≈ A photographer who clicks a photo of the thermometer **once every 5 seconds**. The continuous mercury column is "frozen" at that instant.
- **Quantization** ≈ The photographer also rounds the reading to the **nearest whole degree** because the digital display cannot show "23.47 °C", only "23 °C".

If the photographer clicks too slowly (under-sampling), they miss rapid temperature swings — like a slow-shutter camera blurring a fan blade. If they use only a 2-digit display (coarse quantization), tiny fluctuations are lost.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a Networked Control System, the *sensor node* does the sampling + quantization, the *network* transports the bits, and the *controller/actuator node* does the reconstruction (typically via a Zero-Order Hold). Both the choice of $T_s$ and the choice of $n$ directly determine **stability, accuracy, and bandwidth** of the closed loop.

## 1.4 Key Physical / System Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Range (NCS) |
|-----------|--------|---------------|---------------------|
| Sampling period | $T_s$ | **seconds (s)** | 1 ms – 100 ms |
| Sampling frequency | $f_s$ | **Hertz (Hz)** | 10 Hz – 1 kHz |
| Network bit-rate | $R_b$ | **bits/second (bps)** | 10 kbps – 1 Mbps |
| ADC resolution | $n$ | **bits** | 8 – 16 |
| Quantization step | $q$ | **volts (V)** | 1 mV – 100 mV |

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sampling Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Continuous signal** $x(t)$ exists for all $t \geq 0$.
2. An **impulse train sampler** multiplies $x(t)$ by $\delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \delta(t - kT_s)$.
3. The output is an **ideal sampled signal** $x^*(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x(kT_s)\,\delta(t - kT_s)$.
4. The discrete sequence $x[k] = x(kT_s)$ is what the digital controller actually processes.
5. A **reconstruction device** (ZOH or FOH) converts $x[k]$ back into a staircase or piecewise-linear signal $y(t)$ for the analog actuator.

## 2.2 The Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A band-limited signal with maximum frequency component $f_{\max}$ (Hz) can be **perfectly reconstructed** from its samples if and only if the sampling frequency satisfies:
> $$f_s \;\geq\; 2\,f_{\max} \quad \text{equivalently,} \quad T_s \;\leq\; \frac{1}{2\,f_{\max}}$$
> The minimum allowed sampling frequency $f_N = 2f_{\max}$ is called the **Nyquist frequency**.

**The Why & The How:**
- Sampling replicates the signal spectrum at every integer multiple of $f_s$.
- If $f_s < 2f_{\max}$, these spectral replicas **overlap** → *aliasing*, which is irreversible information loss.
- A practical safeguard: insert an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** with cutoff $f_c \leq f_s/2$ *before* the sampler.

## 2.3 Hold Devices — Reconstructing the Continuous Signal

### 2.3.1 Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) — *most common in NCS*

The ZOH keeps each sample value constant over the entire interval $[kT_s,\,(k+1)T_s)$:

$$y(t) = x[k], \quad \text{for } kT_s \leq t < (k+1)T_s$$

Its Laplace-domain transfer function (derived in Section 3):

$$G_h(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-sT_s}}{s}$$

### 2.3.2 First-Order Hold (FOH)

The FOH performs **linear extrapolation** between the current sample $x[k]$ and the previous one $x[k-1]$:

$$y(t) = x[k] + \frac{x[k] - x[k-1]}{T_s}\,(t - kT_s)$$

> [!TIP]
> **ZOH vs FOH Trade-off:** ZOH is simpler and introduces a half-sample **delay** ($T_s/2$), which acts as additional phase lag and can destabilize a control loop. FOH gives smoother reconstruction and slightly less phase lag but is rarely implemented in practice due to complexity.

## 2.4 Quantization — Step-by-Step Logic

1. The continuous-range sample $x[k]$ lies in the interval $[x_q,\; x_q + q)$.
2. A **quantizer** with step size $q$ rounds $x[k]$ to the nearest reconstruction level $x_q$.
3. The difference $e_q[k] = x_q[k] - x[k]$ is the **quantization error**.
4. For uniform mid-tread quantizers, $e_q$ is bounded as $-\tfrac{q}{2} \leq e_q \leq \tfrac{q}{2}$.

## 2.5 Types of Quantizers

| Type | Step Pattern | Typical Application |
|------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Uniform (linear)** | All steps equal size $q$ | Sensor ADC front-ends, high-SNR regimes |
| **Mid-tread** | Has a level at zero | Audio codecs, control signals |
| **Mid-riser** | No level at zero | Image/video coding |
| **Non-uniform (log/μ-law/A-law)** | Smaller steps for small amplitudes | Speech (telephony), wide-dynamic-range signals |
| **Vector quantizer** | Quantizes blocks jointly | Image compression, advanced NCS event-triggered schemes |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every high-yield formula for this module. Use `\vert` for absolute value to keep markdown tables intact.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $f_s = 1/T_s$ | Sampling frequency | Hz |
| 2 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | Nyquist criterion | Hz |
| 3 | $L = 2^n$ | Number of quantization levels | dimensionless |
| 4 | $q = V_{\text{FS}} / 2^n$ | Quantization step size | V (or signal unit) |
| 5 | $\vert e_q \vert \leq q/2$ | Max quantization error bound | V |
| 6 | $\sigma_{e_q}^2 = q^2 / 12$ | Quantization noise variance (uniform) | V$^2$ |
| 7 | $\text{SQNR} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ | Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio | dB |
| 8 | $\text{SQNR} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_{e_q}^2}\right)$ | General SQNR definition | dB |
| 9 | $G_h(s) = (1 - e^{-sT_s})/s$ | ZOH transfer function | — |
| 10 | $G_h(z) = (1 - z^{-1})/s$ | ZOH in discrete domain (approx.) | — |
| 11 | $T_{\text{dealy,ZOH}} \approx T_s/2$ | Average ZOH delay | s |
| 12 | $\Delta f = 1/(N T_s)$ | Frequency resolution (DFT) | Hz |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Automation (PROFIBUS, CAN):** Sampling periods in the 1–10 ms range; 12-bit ADC typical.
- **Automotive (CAN, FlexRay, Ethernet):** Engine control loops sampled at 1 ms; some safety-critical systems use 16-bit ADC.
- **IoT & Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN):** Coarse 8/10-bit ADCs and large $T_s$ to save energy; aggressive quantization is a power-saving lever.
- **Audio (PCM telephony):** 8-bit µ-law quantization at 8 kHz sampling.
- **Smart Grid / SCADA:** Quantization-aware state estimators compensate for meter bit-depth.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — ZOH Transfer Function $G_h(s)$

**Starting Point:** The ZOH output is a staircase waveform:

$$y(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot \big[ u(t - kT_s) - u(t - (k+1)T_s) \big]$$

Take the Laplace transform term-by-term. Using the time-shift property $\mathcal{L}\{u(t-a)\} = e^{-as}/s$:

$$Y(s) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot \left( \frac{e^{-kT_s s}}{s} - \frac{e^{-(k+1)T_s s}}{s} \right)$$

Factor out $e^{-kT_s s}$ and telescope the geometric series. After algebraic simplification (shown below step-by-step):

$$\begin{aligned}
Y(s) &= \frac{1}{s}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x[k]\,e^{-kT_s s}\,\Big(1 - e^{-T_s s}\Big) \\[4pt]
     &= \frac{1 - e^{-T_s s}}{s}\;\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x[k]\,e^{-kT_s s}
\end{aligned}$$

The summation is the **starred transform** $X^*(s)$ of the sampled input. Hence:

$$\boxed{\;G_h(s) \;=\; \frac{Y(s)}{X^*(s)} \;=\; \frac{1 - e^{-sT_s}}{s}\;}$$

This is the textbook ZOH transfer function. Note that $G_h(0) = T_s$, i.e., the DC gain equals the sampling period.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Frequency Response of ZOH (Magnitude)

Substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$G_h(j\omega) = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega T_s}}{j\omega}$$

Use Euler's identity: $1 - e^{-j\theta} = 2\sin(\theta/2)\,e^{-j\theta/2}$, so with $\theta = \omega T_s$:

$$\begin{aligned}
G_h(j\omega) &= \frac{2\sin(\omega T_s / 2)\,e^{-j\omega T_s/2}}{j\omega} \\[4pt]
             &= T_s \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega T_s/2)}{\omega T_s/2}\;e^{-j\omega T_s/2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Magnitude response:**

$$\vert G_h(j\omega) \vert = T_s \left\vert \operatorname{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega T_s}{2}\right) \right\vert$$

where $\operatorname{sinc}(x) = \sin(x)/x$. This low-pass character is what filters out the high-frequency spectral replicas produced by sampling.

**Phase response (extra half-sample delay):**

$$\angle G_h(j\omega) = -\frac{\omega T_s}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{\text{delay}} = \frac{T_s}{2}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Quantization Noise Variance

Assume the quantization error $e_q$ is **uniformly distributed** over $[-q/2,\, +q/2]$ and **statistically independent** of the signal (a standard assumption for high-resolution quantizers).

For a uniform distribution on $[a, b]$, the variance is $\sigma^2 = (b-a)^2 / 12$. With $a = -q/2$, $b = +q/2$:

$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \frac{(q - (-q))^2}{12} = \frac{(2q)^2}{12} = \frac{q^2}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{?}$$

Correcting: $\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \dfrac{(b-a)^2}{12} = \dfrac{(q)^2}{3}$ — wait, $b - a = q$, so:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_{e_q}^{2} \;=\; \frac{q^{2}}{12}\;}$$

(The factor is **12**, not 3, because $b-a = q$, and $(q)^2/12$.) ✔

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — SQNR for a Full-Scale Sinusoid

Consider a sinusoid $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$ quantizer with $L = 2^n$ levels spanning $\pm V_{\text{FS}}/2$, so $q = V_{\text{FS}}/2^n$.

**Signal power** (assuming full-scale, $A = V_{\text{FS}}/2$):

$$\sigma_x^2 = \frac{A^2}{2} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}^2}{8}$$

**Noise power** (from Derivation 3):

$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \frac{q^2}{12} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}^2}{12 \cdot 4^{n}}$$

**SQNR ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} &= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\sigma_x^{2}}{\sigma_{e_q}^{2}}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{FS}}^{2}/8}{V_{\text{FS}}^{2}/(12\cdot 4^{n})}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{12 \cdot 4^{n}}{8}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\cdot 4^{n}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 10\log_{10}(1.5) + 10\,n\log_{10}(4) \\[4pt]
&= 1.76 + 6.02\,n
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{SQNR} \;=\; 6.02\,n \;+\; 1.76 \text{ dB}\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> **The famous "6 dB-per-bit rule":** Each additional bit of ADC resolution improves SQNR by approximately **6.02 dB**. Hence going from 8-bit to 12-bit quantization improves dynamic range by ~24 dB, equivalent to a factor of 16 in noise power.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Discrete-Time Plant Model with ZOH

For a continuous plant $G_p(s)$ preceded by a ZOH, the exact discrete equivalent is:

$$G_p(z) = (1 - z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left[\,\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\,\right]\,\right\}\bigg\vert_{t=kT_s}$$

**Worked Example:** Let $G_p(s) = \dfrac{a}{s+a}$ (a first-order plant).

Step 1: Form $\dfrac{G_p(s)}{s} = \dfrac{a}{s(s+a)}$.

Step 2: Partial-fraction decomposition:

$$\frac{a}{s(s+a)} = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{1}{s+a}$$

Step 3: Inverse Laplace:

$$\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\frac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\} = 1 - e^{-a t}$$

Step 4: Sample at $t = kT_s$ and apply the $\mathcal{Z}$-transform:

$$\mathcal{Z}\{1 - e^{-a k T_s}\} = \frac{z}{z-1} - \frac{z}{z - e^{-aT_s}}$$

Step 5: Multiply by $(1 - z^{-1}) = (z-1)/z$:

$$\begin{aligned}
G_p(z) &= \frac{z-1}{z}\!\left[\frac{z}{z-1} - \frac{z}{z - e^{-aT_s}}\right] \\[4pt]
       &= 1 - \frac{z-1}{z - e^{-aT_s}} \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{(1 - e^{-aT_s})\,z^{-1}}{1 - e^{-aT_s}\,z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the discrete-time pulse-transfer function suitable for direct digital controller design.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Choice of Sampling Period (Practical Rule-of-Thumb)

A widely used engineering heuristic for control loops:

$$T_s \;\approx\; \frac{1}{10\,\omega_c} \;\;\text{to}\;\; \frac{1}{30\,\omega_c}$$

where $\omega_c$ is the **closed-loop bandwidth** (rad/s). This keeps the sampling-induced phase lag $\omega_c T_s/2$ small (between 1.9° and 5.7°) while keeping the data-rate modest.

For Nyquist-limited signal acquisition (no control feedback, e.g., data logging):

$$T_s \;\leq\; \frac{1}{2\,f_{\max}}$$

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | ZOH | FOH | 1-bit Sigma-Delta |
|-----------|-----|-----|-------------------|
| Reconstruction order | 0 (constant) | 1 (linear) | oversampled noise-shaped |
| Introduced phase lag | $\approx T_s/2$ | $\approx T_s/3$ | effective (filtered) |
| Computational cost | Very low | Low–Medium | High (digital filter) |
| Effective SQNR (typical) | $6.02n + 1.76$ dB | $6.02n + 1.76$ dB | $> 100$ dB |
| Typical NCS use | Default | Rare | High-fidelity audio, instrumentation |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem. What is aliasing and how is it prevented?**

**Model Answer:**
The Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem states that a band-limited continuous signal with maximum frequency component $f_{\max}$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2f_{\max}$.

**Aliasing** is the phenomenon where, when $f_s < 2f_{\max}$, the spectral replicas of the sampled signal overlap, causing different frequency components to become indistinguishable. The high-frequency content "folds back" into the baseband as spurious low-frequency components.

**Prevention methods:**
1. Sample at or above the Nyquist rate: $f_s \geq 2f_{\max}$.
2. Insert an **anti-aliasing analog low-pass filter** before the sampler with cutoff $f_c \leq f_s/2$.
3. Use **oversampling** (sample at $f_s \gg 2f_{\max}$) followed by digital decimation.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define quantization step size and quantization error. For a uniform quantizer with input range $\pm 10$ V and 12-bit resolution, compute the step size and maximum error.**

**Model Answer:**

The **quantization step size** $q$ is the smallest amplitude difference between two adjacent reconstruction levels. For an $n$-bit ADC covering full-scale range $V_{\text{FS}}$:

$$q = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^n}$$

The **quantization error** $e_q = x_q - x$ is the deviation between the quantized output and the true sample; for a uniform mid-tread quantizer, $\vert e_q \vert \leq q/2$.

**Computation:**

Given $V_{\text{FS}} = 20$ V (from $-10$ to $+10$), $n = 12$:

$$q = \frac{20}{2^{12}} = \frac{20}{4096} \approx 4.883 \text{ mV}$$

$$\vert e_q \vert_{\max} = \frac{q}{2} \approx 2.441 \text{ mV}$$

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of a Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) and explain why it introduces an effective time delay of $T_s/2$ in a sampled-data control system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A digital control system samples a 50 Hz sinusoidal signal using a ZOH. Compute (i) the minimum sampling frequency required to avoid aliasing, (ii) the magnitude of the ZOH output at the signal frequency, and (iii) the average phase lag introduced. Use $T_s = 5$ ms. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.A (a):

**Step 1 — ZOH output expression:**

A ZOH outputs a staircase waveform:

$$y(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x[k]\,\big[u(t-kT_s) - u(t-(k+1)T_s)\big]$$

**Step 2 — Laplace transform:**

Using $\mathcal{L}\{u(t-a)\} = e^{-as}/s$:

$$Y(s) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x[k]\,\frac{e^{-kT_s s} - e^{-(k+1)T_s s}}{s} = \frac{1-e^{-T_s s}}{s}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x[k] e^{-kT_s s}$$

**Step 3 — Identify starred transform:**

$X^*(s) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x[k] e^{-kT_s s}$, so:

$$\boxed{G_h(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X^*(s)} = \frac{1 - e^{-sT_s}}{s}}$$

**Step 4 — Phase / delay argument:**

Substituting $s = j\omega$ and using Euler's identity gives:

$$G_h(j\omega) = T_s\,\operatorname{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega T_s}{2}\right)e^{-j\omega T_s/2}$$

The factor $e^{-j\omega T_s/2}$ corresponds to a pure time delay of $T_s/2$ seconds. This is because the staircase output is, on average, half a sampling period "behind" the continuous signal it is reconstructing.

---

#### Solution to Q.A (b):

**(i) Nyquist frequency:**

$$f_{\min} = 2 f_{\max} = 2 \times 50 = 100 \text{ Hz} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_s \leq 10 \text{ ms}$$

**(ii) Magnitude of ZOH output at 50 Hz with $T_s = 5$ ms:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega &= 2\pi f = 100\pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 314.16 \text{ rad/s} \\[4pt]
\frac{\omega T_s}{2} &= \frac{100\pi \times 0.005}{2} = 0.25\pi \approx 0.7854 \text{ rad} \\[4pt]
\vert G_h(j\omega) \vert &= T_s \left\vert \frac{\sin(\omega T_s/2)}{\omega T_s/2} \right\vert = 0.005 \times \frac{\sin(0.7854)}{0.7854} \\[4pt]
&= 0.005 \times \frac{0.7071}{0.7854} \approx 0.005 \times 0.9003 \\[4pt]
&\approx 4.502 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V/V·s}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Average phase lag:**

$$\phi = -\frac{\omega T_s}{2} = -0.7854 \text{ rad} \approx -45^{\circ}$$

Equivalent delay: $T_s/2 = 2.5$ ms.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the working of a uniform mid-tread quantizer with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for its **signal-to-quantization-noise ratio (SQNR)** for a full-scale sinusoid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10-bit ADC quantizes an audio signal with $\pm 5$ V full-scale range. Calculate (i) the quantization step size, (ii) the maximum quantization error, (iii) the SQNR in dB, and (iv) the quantization noise RMS voltage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.B (a):

**Working Principle:**
A uniform mid-tread quantizer divides the input range into $L = 2^n$ equal intervals of width $q$ and assigns each interval a discrete reconstruction level at its center (except that a reconstruction level exists exactly at zero — hence "mid-tread"). Each input sample is mapped to the nearest reconstruction level.

**Block diagram (described in words):**
Input → Comparator Bank → Decision Logic → Level Selector (Look-up Table / Encoder) → Binary Output.

**Derivation of SQNR for full-scale sinusoid:**

For a sinusoid of peak amplitude $A = V_{\text{FS}}/2$:

$$\sigma_x^2 = \frac{A^2}{2} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}^2}{8}$$

Quantization error variance (uniform distribution):

$$\sigma_{e_q}^2 = \frac{q^2}{12} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}^2}{12 \cdot 4^n}$$

SQNR in dB:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{SQNR} &= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{FS}}^2/8}{V_{\text{FS}}^2/(12\cdot 4^n)}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\cdot 4^n\right) \\[4pt]
&= 10\log_{10}(1.5) + 10\,n\log_{10}(4) \\[4pt]
&= 1.76 + 6.02\,n \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### Solution to Q.B (b):

**Given:** $n = 10$ bits, $V_{\text{FS}} = 10$ V (from $-5$ V to $+5$ V).

**(i) Quantization step size:**

$$q = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{1024} \approx 9.766 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) Maximum quantization error:**

$$\vert e_q \vert_{\max} = \frac{q}{2} \approx 4.883 \text{ mV}$$

**(iii) SQNR:**

$$\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \times 10 + 1.76 = 60.2 + 1.76 = 61.96 \text{ dB}$$

**(iv) RMS quantization noise voltage:**

$$\sigma_{e_q} = \frac{q}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{9.766 \times 10^{-3}}{3.464} \approx 2.819 \text{ mV (RMS)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the full-scale range** when computing $q$: students often write $q = V_{\text{FS}}/2^n$ but plug in the peak amplitude instead of the *total* range. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Confusing quantization noise variance ($q^2/12$) with step-size $q$**: a single factor of 12 error propagates throughout the SQNR calculation. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Forgetting the half-sample delay** of ZOH when assessing closed-loop stability. Always include $\angle G_h = -\omega T_s/2$ in the loop phase budget. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 4. **Misapplying Nyquist:** stating $f_s \geq f_{\max}$ instead of $f_s \geq 2f_{\max}$ is a textbook error costing the "definition" mark.
> 5. **Unit mistakes:** SQNR is in dB, but quantization noise RMS must be in V (or mV), not in dB. Mark this clearly with units on every numerical answer.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Nyquist criterion:** $f_s \geq 2f_{\max}$ — anything less causes **irreversible aliasing**.
- **Anti-aliasing filter** must precede the sampler with cutoff $\leq f_s/2$.
- **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** is the default reconstruction device in NCS; introduces a **half-sample delay** ($T_s/2$) and a sinc-shaped low-pass magnitude response.
- **ZOH transfer function:** $G_h(s) = (1 - e^{-sT_s})/s$.
- **Quantization step:** $q = V_{\text{FS}}/2^n$ — halves for every additional bit.
- **Quantization error bound:** $\vert e_q \vert \leq q/2$.
- **Quantization noise variance (uniform):** $\sigma_{e_q}^2 = q^2/12$.
- **SQNR formula:** $\text{SQNR} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ dB (the **6-dB-per-bit rule**).
- **Sampling-period heuristic for control:** $T_s \approx (1/10 \text{ to } 1/30) \times (1/\omega_c)$.
- **Mid-tread quantizer** has a level at zero (preferred for control signals); **mid-riser** does not (preferred for image coding).
- **Aliasing** is preventable; **quantization error** is *not* — it is inherent and only reducible by increasing $n$.
- **Discrete equivalent of a continuous plant + ZOH:** $G_p(z) = (1-z^{-1})\mathcal{Z}\{G_p(s)/s\}$.
- **Effect on stability:** both coarse quantization (small $n$) and slow sampling (large $T_s$) reduce phase margin and can destabilize a closed loop.
- **Practical ADC word-lengths in NCS:** 8-bit (coarse, low-power WSN), 12-bit (industrial), 16-bit (precision instrumentation).
- **Network implications:** quantization directly affects the **packet payload size** = $n \times$ (number of signals) bits per sample, hence influences bandwidth and energy consumption.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Predictive Control
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT632 - NETWORKED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Predictive Control

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Predictive Control in Networked Control Systems (NCS)** is a class of advanced control strategies that uses an explicit internal **process model** to forecast (predict) the future dynamic behavior of the plant over a finite time horizon, and optimizes a sequence of future control actions by minimizing a predefined cost function—subject to system constraints—while explicitly compensating for network-induced imperfections such as **time-varying delays**, **packet dropouts**, and **limited bandwidth**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, the three principal predictive-control paradigms examined are:

1. **Smith Predictor (SP)** – a classical delay-compensation technique.
2. **Generalized Predictive Control (GPC)** – an adaptive, multi-step-ahead receding-horizon strategy.
3. **Networked Predictive Control System (NPCS)** – a state/output-prediction framework designed for packet-based networks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Commuter Who Checks Google Maps"

Imagine you are driving from Kochi to Thiruvananthapuram, but the highway has unpredictable traffic jams (≈ *network delay*) and occasionally the GPS signal drops (≈ *packet loss*). A naive driver only reacts to whatever is in front of the bumper. A **predictive commuter**, however:

- **Predicts** traffic 10 km ahead using a real-time map (≈ *internal process model*).
- **Pre-computes** an optimal speed profile for the next few kilometers (≈ *control horizon*).
- **Re-plans** continuously as new information arrives (≈ *receding horizon principle*).
- **Buffers** against uncertainty by looking further than needed (≈ *prediction horizon*).

This is exactly what predictive control does for an NCS — it looks into the future, decides the best sequence of control moves, and only the first move is applied before the horizon shifts forward.

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics in Predictive Control

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Value (NCS context) |
|---|---|---|
| $N_p$ | **Prediction horizon** | $5 \le N_p \le 30$ |
| $N_c$ | **Control horizon** | $N_c \le N_p$, often $N_c = 2$–$5$ |
| $\lambda$ | **Control-weighting factor** | $0 < \lambda \le 1$ |
| $\tau_{sc}$ | **Maximum networked delay tolerated** | $1$–$10$ sampling intervals |
| $\delta$ | **Packet dropout rate** | $0 < \delta < 0.5$ |

> [!NOTE]  
> The **receding horizon principle** states that at every sampling instant $k$, only the first element $u(k)$ of the optimized control sequence is applied to the plant, after which the horizon slides forward by one step and the optimization is repeated with updated measurements.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Logic of a Networked Predictive Control System

The NPCS architecture (developed by Liu, Hu, and colleagues — standard NCS literature) consists of three synchronized blocks working across two network links:

- **Controller Side (CC → NC)**
  1. **State/Output Predictor** — uses the plant model to estimate $x(k+1), x(k+2), \ldots, x(k+N_p)$ for every received (or missing) packet.
  2. **Optimization Solver** — minimizes the cost $J$ over horizon $N_p$ to yield the optimal control sequence $\{u^*(k), u^*(k+1), \ldots, u^*(k+N_c-1)\}$.
  3. **Networked Transmitter** — packs the **entire predicted control sequence** (not just one value) into a single data packet and transmits it to the actuator side.

- **Actuator Side (NC → CP)**
  4. **Networked Receiver / Buffer** — holds the received control sequence.
  5. **Selector** — when the next control packet is delayed or lost, it **falls back to the pre-stored next value** of the previously received sequence. This is the "key trick" that masks packet dropout.

## 2.2 Why Predictive Control Works in NCS

| Network Imperfection | How Prediction Helps |
|---|---|
| **Random delay** $\tau_k$ | Predictor advances plant state by $\tau_k$ steps so the controller sees a "current" state. |
| **Packet dropout** | Pre-buffered future control values $u(k+1), u(k+2), \ldots$ are activated in order. |
| **Out-of-order packets** | Time-stamped predictions are reordered via a selection logic. |
| **Varying sampling** | Prediction horizon absorbs jitter up to $N_p$ steps. |

> [!TIP]  
> **Why this matters (KTU 2024 NEP alignment):** This addresses CO3 of the PECPT632 syllabus — *design delay and packet-loss compensation strategies* — and demonstrates **higher-order thinking** (Analyze / Evaluate levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Use `\vert` instead of the pipe character in every cell to preserve the markdown table.

| \# | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| F1 | $\hat{x}(k+i \vert k) = A^i x(k) + \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} A^{i-1-j} B\, u(k+j)$ | $i$-step-ahead **state prediction** from model $\,x^+ = Ax+Bu$ |
| F2 | $\hat{y}(k+i \vert k) = C\hat{x}(k+i \vert k)$ | Predicted output |
| F3 | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{N_p} \hat{y}(k+i\vert k)^T Q \, \hat{y}(k+i\vert k) + \sum_{j=0}^{N_c-1} u(k+j)^T R \, u(k+j)$ | **GPC / MPC cost function**; $Q \succ 0$, $R \succ 0$ |
| F4 | $U^*(k) = \arg\min_U J$ subject to $u_{\min} \le u \le u_{\max}$ | **Constrained optimization** yielding control sequence |
| F5 | $u(k) = \big[1\;0\;\cdots\;0\big] U^*(k)$ | **Receding-horizon law** — only first move applied |
| F6 | $G_{SP}(s) = \dfrac{G_c(s)\,G_p(s)\,e^{-\tau s}}{1 + G_c(s)\,G_p(s)\big(1 - e^{-\tau s}\big)\,(1 - G_m(s))}$ | Smith Predictor **closed-loop TF** with $G_m \approx G_p$ |
| F7 | $G_{SP}^{ideal}(s) = \dfrac{G_c(s)\,G_p(s)\,e^{-\tau s}}{1 + G_c(s)\,G_p(s)\,e^{-\tau s}}$ | Smith Predictor when $G_m = G_p$ (delay removed from loop) |
| F8 | $\Delta u(k) = K_{GPC}\,\Delta Y_{past}(k)$ | GPC incremental control law (Diophantine-based) |
| F9 | $\eta(k) = \dfrac{1}{N_p}\sum_{i=1}^{N_p} \big(e(k+i)\big)^2$ | Mean squared prediction error over horizon |
| F10 | $\tau_{max} = (N_p - N_c)\,T_s$ | **Maximum tolerable delay** for stable NPCS operation |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Engineering utility:** Predictive control is the de-facto standard in **process industries** (refineries, cement plants), **autonomous vehicles** (look-ahead path planning), **smart-grid** load dispatch, and **tele-operation** of robotic manipulators — every scenario where communication is imperfect.

## 2.4 Operational Logic — Stepwise Flow of an NPCS Tick

- **Step 1:** Sensor samples plant at $k$, packet $P_k = \big[\,x(k),\; \text{time-stamp}\,\big]$ sent to controller.
- **Step 2:** If packet arrives within deadline $d_{max}$ → update state estimate $\hat{x}(k)$.
- **Step 3:** Predict outputs over horizon $\hat{y}(k+1),\ldots,\hat{y}(k+N_p)$ using F1–F2.
- **Step 4:** Solve min-cost problem F3 → F4 → obtain $U^* = [u(k), u(k+1), \ldots]$.
- **Step 5:** Bundle entire $U^*$ (length $N_c$) into a single packet and send to actuator.
- **Step 6:** Actuator applies $u(k)$ **now**; if next packet is lost/delay-out, it automatically applies $u(k+1), u(k+2), \ldots$ from the stored sequence.
- **Step 7:** Repeat at $k+1$ (receding horizon).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the $i$-Step State Predictor (F1)

**Given discrete plant model:**

$$x(k+1) = A\,x(k) + B\,u(k)$$

$$y(k) = C\,x(k)$$

with $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$, $C \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$.

**Step 1 — One-step prediction (i = 1):**

$$\hat{x}(k+1 \vert k) = A\,x(k) + B\,u(k)$$

This is direct substitution of the current state into the model. It uses the **measured or estimated** $x(k)$ and the most recent applied input $u(k)$.

**Step 2 — Two-step prediction (i = 2):**

$$\hat{x}(k+2 \vert k) = A\,\hat{x}(k+1\vert k) + B\,u(k+1)$$

Substitute Step 1 result:

$$\hat{x}(k+2 \vert k) = A\big(Ax(k)+Bu(k)\big) + B\,u(k+1)$$

$$\hat{x}(k+2 \vert k) = A^2 x(k) + AB\,u(k) + B\,u(k+1)$$

**Step 3 — Three-step prediction (i = 3):**

$$\hat{x}(k+3 \vert k) = A\,\hat{x}(k+2\vert k) + B\,u(k+2)$$

Substitute the Step 2 expression:

$$\hat{x}(k+3 \vert k) = A^3 x(k) + A^2 B\,u(k) + AB\,u(k+1) + B\,u(k+2)$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the pattern (Mathematical Induction):**

For any horizon $i$:

$$\hat{x}(k+i \vert k) = A^i x(k) + \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} A^{i-1-j}\,B\,u(k+j)$$

This is **formula F1**, which is the cornerstone of every model-based predictor in an NCS. The corresponding output prediction is F2: $\hat{y}(k+i \vert k) = C\,\hat{x}(k+i \vert k)$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Constrained MPC Cost (F3 → F4)

The unconstrained, regulation-form cost to be minimized is:

$$J\big(x(k), U\big) = \sum_{i=1}^{N_p} \hat{x}(k+i\vert k)^T Q\, \hat{x}(k+i\vert k) + \sum_{j=0}^{N_c-1} u(k+j)^T R\, u(k+j)$$

**Step 1 — Stack predictions in matrix form.** Let $X = [\hat{x}(k+1 \vert k)^T, \ldots, \hat{x}(k+N_p \vert k)^T]^T$ and $U = [u(k)^T, \ldots, u(k+N_c-1)^T]^T$. Then:

$$X = \Phi\,x(k) + \Gamma\,U$$

where $\Phi$ and $\Gamma$ are block-Toeplitz matrices built from powers of $A$ and $B$ (computed at controller design time).

**Step 2 — Express J as a quadratic in U:**

$$J = U^T \big(\Gamma^T \bar Q\, \Gamma + \bar R\big) U + 2\,x(k)^T \Phi^T \bar Q\, \Gamma\, U + \text{const}(x(k))$$

with $\bar Q = \text{blkdiag}(Q,\ldots,Q)$ ($N_p$ blocks) and $\bar R = \text{blkdiag}(R,\ldots,R)$ ($N_c$ blocks).

**Step 3 — Unconstrained optimum (set $\partial J / \partial U = 0$):**

$$U^*(k) = -\big(\Gamma^T \bar Q\, \Gamma + \bar R\big)^{-1} \Gamma^T \bar Q\, \Phi\, x(k)$$

**Step 4 — Apply receding horizon (F5):** Extract $u(k) = K_{MPC}\,x(k)$ where $K_{MPC}$ is the first $m$ rows of the matrix above. The state-feedback law is **time-invariant** in the unconstrained case.

**Step 5 — Constrained case (F4):** When actuator limits $u_{\min} \le u \le u_{\max}$ exist, the QP above is replaced by a **quadratic program (QP)** solved at every step:

$$U^*(k) = \arg\min_U \; \tfrac{1}{2} U^T H U + g(k)^T U$$

$$\text{subject to } u_{\min} \le U \le u_{\max}$$

The matrix $H$ is constant (positive-definite), so **warm-starting** the QP solver with the previous $U^*(k-1)$ drastically reduces on-line computation — a critical feature for embedded NCS controllers.

## 3.3 Smith Predictor Transfer-Function Derivation (F6 → F7)

**Given:** Plant $G_p(s)$ with transport delay $\tau$, nominal model $G_m(s)$ (delay-free), controller $G_c(s)$.

**Step 1 — Classical feedback (delay inside loop):** Closed-loop transfer function of a plant with delay is $G_c G_p e^{-\tau} / (1 + G_c G_p e^{-\tau})$. The delay is inside the characteristic equation, which limits achievable bandwidth.

**Step 2 — Smith Predictor augmentation:** Place a parallel branch that contains a **delay-free model** $G_m(s)$ and a **pure delay model** $e^{-\tau s}$:

$$G_{SP}(s) = \frac{G_c(s)\,G_p(s)\,e^{-\tau s}}{1 + G_c(s)\big(G_p(s)\,e^{-\tau s} - G_m(s)\,e^{-\tau s} + G_m(s)\big)}$$

**Step 3 — Plant-model matching assumption ($G_m = G_p$):** Substitute:

$$G_{SP}^{ideal}(s) = \frac{G_c G_p e^{-\tau s}}{1 + G_c G_p e^{-\tau s}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** The delay $e^{-\tau s}$ has been **factored out of the characteristic polynomial** and now appears only as a gain $1$ on the reference (or as a time-shift on the output). The remaining feedback loop sees only the delay-free plant $G_p(s)$, so the controller $G_c(s)$ can be designed using standard methods (PID, lead-lag, etc.) as if no delay existed.

**Step 5 — Robustness caveat:** If $G_m \ne G_p$ (model mismatch), the cancellation is imperfect and stability degrades. Robust variants — **Modified Smith Predictor**, **Filtered Smith Predictor**, **Gain-Scheduled Smith Predictor** — are commonly asked in KTU exams.

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget that the Smith Predictor is **not a universal cure** — it fails for *time-varying* or *uncertain* delays beyond the design value $\tau$. For random NCS delays, NPCS or MPC is preferred.

## 3.4 GPC Control Law Derivation (F8)

**Step 1 — Controlled Auto-Regressive Integrated Moving Average (CARIMA) plant model:**

$$A(z^{-1})\,y(k) = B(z^{-1})\,z^{-d}\,u(k-1) + \dfrac{C(z^{-1})}{\Delta}\,\xi(k)$$

where $\Delta = 1 - z^{-1}$ is the differencing operator, $d$ is the dead-time, and $\xi$ is white noise.

**Step 2 — Diophantine equation:** Solve for polynomials $E_j, F_j$ satisfying:

$$1 = E_j(z^{-1})\,A(z^{-1})\,\Delta + z^{-j}\,F_j(z^{-1})$$

The optimal $j$-step-ahead predictor is:

$$\hat{y}(k+j \vert k) = G_j(z^{-1})\,\Delta u(k+j-d) + F_j(z^{-1})\,y(k)$$

**Step 3 — Cost minimization:** Setting $\partial J / \partial \Delta U = 0$ yields:

$$\Delta U = (G^T G + \lambda I)^{-1} G^T (Y_r - F)$$

where $G$ is the dynamic matrix formed from step-response coefficients $g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_{N_p}$, and $Y_r$ is the reference trajectory.

**Step 4 — Receding-horizon implementation:**

$$u(k) = u(k-1) + \big[1\;0\;\cdots\;0\big] \Delta U$$

This is **formula F8** — the incremental GPC law that drives the plant to track a future reference while rejecting disturbances and tolerating the known dead-time $d$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 General Networked Predictive Control System (NPCS) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensorA[Plant Sensor] -->|packet Pk| netA((Network CC to NC))
    netA --> pred[State Predictor]
    pred --> opt[Optimization Solver QP]
    opt --> enc[Control Sequence Packeter U1 UNc]
    enc -->|packet Uk sequence| netB((Network NC to CP))
    netB --> buf[Actuator Buffer]
    buf --> sel{New Packet Arrived}
    sel -->|Yes| fresh[Apply u of k]
    sel -->|No Loss Delay| prev[Apply u of k plus 1 from stored]
    fresh --> plantA[Plant Actuator]
    prev --> plantA
    plantA --> plantB[Physical Plant]
    plantB --> sensorA
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **State Predictor + Optimization Solver** pair lives on the controller side and uses F1–F3 to compute a *sequence* of $N_c$ future controls.
- The **Actuator Buffer + Selector** pair is the *fall-back mechanism*: when the network drops the next packet, the actuator does not stall — it simply plays the next pre-stored value.
- The double network nodes `netA` and `netB` are explicitly drawn to emphasize that **two independent communication channels** exist, each with its own delay and loss characteristics.

## 4.2 Smith Predictor — Functional Block Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ref[R reference] --> sumA((+))
    sumA --> ctrl[Controller Gc of s]
    ctrl --> act[Plant Gp of s]
    act --> delay[Delay e power minus tau s]
    delay --> out[Y output]
    out --> fb[Feedback path]
    fb --> sumA
    fb --> spm[Smith Predictor branch]
    spm --> sumA
    spmBlock[Model Gm of s minus tau s] --> spm
    plantModel[Delay free model Gm] --> spmBlock
```

**Note:** The Smith Predictor is essentially a **two-degrees-of-freedom** structure — the primary feedback path carries the true (delayed) plant output $y(t)$, while the predictor branch carries the *delay-free model output* used to cancel the delay in the loop.

## 4.3 Receding-Horizon Visualization (Time-Map)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Receding Horizon Shift at Sampling Instant k
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Horizon at k
    Predicted Np equals 8    :a1, 0, 8
    Optimized Nc equals 3    :a2, 0, 3
    Applied u of k           :crit, 0, 1
    section Horizon at k plus 1
    Predicted shifted        :a3, 1, 8
    Optimized shifted        :a4, 1, 3
    Applied u of k plus 1    :crit, 1, 1
    section Horizon at k plus 2
    Predicted shifted again  :a5, 2, 8
    Optimized shifted again  :a6, 2, 3
    Applied u of k plus 2    :crit, 2, 1
```

> [!NOTE]  
> Each step slides the prediction window forward by one sampling interval; only the *first* optimized control value is applied, hence "receding".

## 4.4 Predictive Control with Packet-Dropout Compensation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Sampling instant k]) --> meas[Measure output y of k]
    meas --> tx1[Transmit y of k over network]
    tx1 --> ack1{Packet received by controller}
    ack1 -->|No| holdc[Hold last state estimate]
    ack1 -->|Yes| pred[Predict states k plus 1 to k plus Np]
    holdc --> pred
    pred --> opt2[Solve min J to get sequence U of length Nc]
    opt2 --> pack[Pack U of k to U of k plus Nc minus 1 in one packet]
    pack --> tx2[Transmit to actuator]
    tx2 --> ack2{Packet received by actuator}
    ack2 -->|No| buf[Use next value from previous buffer]
    ack2 -->|Yes| rep[Replace buffer with new U]
    buf --> app[Apply selected u to plant]
    rep --> app
    app --> inc[Increment k]
    inc --> start
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q1. Distinguish between Model Predictive Control (MPC) and Smith Predictor in the context of Networked Control Systems.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

- **Smith Predictor (SP):** A *single-degree-of-freedom*, open-loop predictor designed only to **cancel a known, fixed delay** $\tau$ from the closed-loop characteristic equation. It uses a delay-free nominal model $G_m(s)$ in parallel with the real plant; no optimization is performed and no constraints are handled. Suitable when the delay is **deterministic and small**.
- **Model Predictive Control (MPC):** A *finite-horizon, receding-horizon* strategy that uses an internal model to predict future outputs over $N_p$ steps and **optimizes a cost function** subject to input/state constraints. Naturally handles **multi-variable systems**, **time-varying delays**, and **soft/hard constraints**. Computationally heavier; relies on a QP solver at each step.
- **In NCS terms:** SP is best for *fixed* industrial delays (e.g., SCADA links); MPC is preferred when the network induces *random* delay and packet loss, and when actuator saturation must be respected.

> **Mark split (KTU pattern):** 1 mark for SP definition, 1 mark for MPC definition, 1 mark for the NCS-context contrast.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q2. Define "receding horizon principle" as applied to GPC. Why is only the first control move applied?*** (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

- The **receding horizon principle** states that at every sampling instant $k$, the controller solves an optimization over a future window $[k+1,\ldots,k+N_p]$, obtains a sequence of $N_c$ control moves, **applies only the first move** $u(k)$, and then shifts the entire window one step forward to repeat the procedure.
- Only the first move is applied because (i) the *plant* is a dynamic system — applying the full sequence at once would ignore feedback corrections; (ii) future measurements $y(k+1), y(k+2), \ldots$ will reveal disturbances and model errors, so a **re-optimization** at the next step is essential; and (iii) this implements a **closed-loop feedback policy** rather than open-loop trajectory tracking.
- This principle converts the open-loop optimal-control problem into a **time-varying state-feedback law** with guaranteed stability under standard assumptions (terminal cost, invariant set).

> **Mark split:** 1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for justification.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern, Module 3]**

### ⭐ Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**Q.A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Networked Predictive Control System (NPCS)**. Show clearly the role of the **control sequence packeter** and the **actuator buffer** in handling network delay and packet dropout. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Architecture (3 marks):** Draw the three-block NPCS — *(i) Controller node* (predictor + optimizer + packeter), *(ii) Network* (with delay $\tau_k$ and dropout probability $\delta$), and *(iii) Actuator node* (receiver + buffer + selector). State that the *entire control sequence* $U^*=[u(k),u(k+1),\ldots,u(k+N_c-1)]$ is transmitted per packet.
2. **Control Sequence Packeter (2 marks):** Explain that bundling the whole sequence allows the actuator to execute the *next* stored value if a future packet is delayed or lost, transforming a random-delay NCS into a **deterministic-input system** from the actuator's viewpoint.
3. **Actuator Buffer (2 marks):** Describe the buffer's state machine: *receive → overwrite* on a fresh packet; *fall back* to next stored value on timeout. Note that buffer length $N_c$ must exceed the maximum expected dropout run length $L_{max}$ — otherwise stability is lost.

---

**Q.A (b)** A discrete plant is modelled by $x(k+1) = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0.1\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix} x(k) + \begin{bmatrix}0\\ 0.1\end{bmatrix} u(k)$. Using $N_p=2$, $N_c=1$, $Q=I_2$, $R=1$, and $x(0)=[1,\;0]^T$, compute the **one-step-ahead and two-step-ahead state predictions** and form the MPC cost $J$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Given $A = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0.1\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix}0\\ 0.1\end{bmatrix}$, $x(0) = [1,\;0]^T$.

**Step 1 — One-step prediction (using F1 with $i=1$):**

$$\hat{x}(1 \vert 0) = A\,x(0) + B\,u(0) = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0.1\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}1\\ 0\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}0\\ 0.1\end{bmatrix} u(0)$$

$$\hat{x}(1 \vert 0) = \begin{bmatrix}1\\ 0\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}0\\ 0.1\end{bmatrix} u(0) = \begin{bmatrix}1\\ 0.1\,u(0)\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Two-step prediction (using F1 with $i=2$):**

$$\hat{x}(2 \vert 0) = A^2 x(0) + A\,B\,u(0) + B\,u(1)$$

Compute $A^2 = A \cdot A = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0.1\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}1 & 0.1\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0.2\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$

Compute $A\,B = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0.1\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}0\\ 0.1\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.01\\ 0.1\end{bmatrix}$

Therefore:

$$\hat{x}(2 \vert 0) = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0.2\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}1\\ 0\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}0.01\\ 0.1\end{bmatrix} u(0) + \begin{bmatrix}0\\ 0.1\end{bmatrix} u(1)$$

$$\hat{x}(2 \vert 0) = \begin{bmatrix}1 + 0.01\,u(0)\\ 0.1\,u(0) + 0.1\,u(1)\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Form the cost (F3):**

$$J = \hat{x}(1)^T Q\, \hat{x}(1) + \hat{x}(2)^T Q\, \hat{x}(2) + u(0)^T R\, u(0)$$

Substituting:

$$J = \big(1^2 + (0.1u_0)^2\big) + \big((1+0.01u_0)^2 + (0.1u_0+0.1u_1)^2\big) + u_0^2$$

With $N_c = 1$, set $u_1 = u_0$ (single control move repeated) or hold $u_1 = 0$ (free terminal move). For $u_1=0$:

$$J(u_0) = 1 + 0.01\,u_0^2 + (1+0.01u_0)^2 + 0.01\,u_0^2 + u_0^2$$

$$J(u_0) = 1 + 0.01\,u_0^2 + 1 + 0.02\,u_0 + 0.0001\,u_0^2 + 0.01\,u_0^2 + u_0^2$$

$$J(u_0) = 2 + 0.02\,u_0 + 1.0201\,u_0^2$$

Minimize: $dJ/du_0 = 0.02 + 2 \times 1.0201\,u_0 = 0 \Rightarrow u_0^* = -0.0098 \approx -0.01$.

Thus $u^*(0) = -0.01$ is applied to the plant (receding-horizon step).

> **Mark split:** 2 marks for $i=1$ prediction, 2 marks for $i=2$ prediction, 2 marks for cost assembly, 1 mark for optimum.

---

### ⭐ Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**Q.B (a)** Explain the **Smith Predictor** for compensating a fixed time delay in a feedback control loop. Derive the closed-loop transfer function and show that the delay is removed from the characteristic equation when the plant model is perfect. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Block diagram (2 marks):** Plant $G_p(s)$ followed by delay $e^{-\tau s}$; controller $G_c(s)$ in feedback; Smith Predictor branch with $(G_m(s) - G_m(s)e^{-\tau s})$ placed around the controller.
2. **Derivation (4 marks):** Start from the block diagram, write the input to $G_c(s)$ as $E(s) = R(s) - Y(s) - \big(G_m - G_m e^{-\tau s}\big)U(s)$. With $U(s) = G_c(s) E(s)$ and $Y(s) = G_p(s) e^{-\tau s} U(s)$, eliminate $E$ to obtain the closed-loop transfer function F6:

$$G_{SP}(s) = \frac{G_c G_p e^{-\tau s}}{1 + G_c \big(G_p e^{-\tau s} - G_m e^{-\tau s} + G_m\big)}$$

3. **Model-matching limit (1 mark):** Substituting $G_m = G_p$ yields F7:

$$G_{SP}^{ideal}(s) = \frac{G_c G_p e^{-\tau s}}{1 + G_c G_p e^{-\tau s}}$$

The delay $e^{-\tau s}$ no longer multiplies $G_c G_p$ inside the characteristic denominator — it has been *factored out*, so the controller $G_c$ is designed as if the loop were delay-free.

---

**Q.B (b)** A networked DC motor has a discrete model $G(z^{-1}) = \dfrac{0.1\,z^{-2}}{1 - 0.9\,z^{-1}}$ with a known network-induced delay of $d=2$ samples. Design a **GPC controller** by stating the CARIMA model, the Diophantine equation, and the predicted $j$-step output for $j=1, 2, 3$. Use $N_p=3$, $N_c=2$, $\lambda=0.5$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **CARIMA model (2 marks):** Identify $A(z^{-1}) = 1 - 0.9 z^{-1}$, $B(z^{-1}) = 0.1$, $C(z^{-1}) = 1$ (assumed), $d=2$. Hence:
