$$(1 - 0.9 z^{-1})\,y(k) = 0.1\,z^{-2}\,u(k-1) + \dfrac{\xi(k)}{\Delta}$$

Multiplying by $\Delta = 1-z^{-1}$:

$$(1 - 0.9 z^{-1})(1-z^{-1})\,y(k) = 0.1\,z^{-2}\,(1-z^{-1})\,u(k-1) + \xi(k)$$

2. **Diophantine equation (2 marks):** Solve $1 = E_j(z^{-1}) \tilde A(z^{-1}) + z^{-j} F_j(z^{-1})$ where $\tilde A = A\Delta$.

   For $j=1$: $E_1 = 1$, $F_1 = 0.9(1-z^{-1}) = 0.9 - 0.9 z^{-1}$.
   For $j=2$: $E_2 = 1$, $F_2 = 0.9(1-z^{-1}) + 0.9^2 z^{-1}(1-z^{-1}) \Rightarrow$ continue Diophantine expansion.
   For $j=3$: similar recursive step.

3. **Predicted outputs (2 marks):** The general predictor form is:

$$\hat{y}(k+j) = G_j(z^{-1})\, \Delta u(k+j-2) + F_j(z^{-1})\, y(k)$$

   with step-response coefficients $G_j = [g_1, g_2, \ldots]$ obtained from a step test on the plant. With $\lambda=0.5$ weighting, the control law F8 becomes:

$$\Delta U = (G^T G + 0.5 I)^{-1} G^T Y_r$$

4. **Final control move (1 mark):** Apply only the first element of $\Delta U$ to the plant, $u(k) = u(k-1) + [\Delta u(0)]$.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**  
> *(i) Forgetting the receding-horizon step* — many students apply the *entire* control sequence, which is **wrong** and costs 2–3 marks. *(ii) Confusing Smith Predictor assumptions* — writing "Smith Predictor works for any delay" is false; mention $G_m=G_p$ assumption explicitly. *(iii) In GPC derivations* — students often skip the Diophantine equation, losing 2 marks. *(iv) Failing to specify units / horizons* — always state $N_p, N_c, \lambda$ values. *(v) Hand-waving the packet-dropout compensation* — explicitly draw the *selector* block and explain the buffer state machine.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Predictive Control (Module 3)**

- **Predictive control** uses an *internal model* + *optimization* to anticipate and compensate for network imperfections.
- The **receding horizon principle**: optimize, apply only the **first** move, slide the window forward.
- Two horizons matter: **prediction horizon** $N_p$ (look-ahead) and **control horizon** $N_c$ (number of free moves); typically $N_c \le N_p$.
- The cost function (F3) trades off **tracking error** ($Q$-weighted) against **control effort** ($R$-weighted).
- **Smith Predictor** is *open-loop compensation* — removes a *known fixed* delay from the closed-loop characteristic equation when $G_m = G_p$.
- **GPC** uses a *CARIMA* model, *Diophantine equation* to compute $j$-step predictors, and an explicit $\lambda$-regularized QP.
- **NPCS** transmits a *sequence* of $N_c$ future controls, allowing the actuator to fall back when packets are lost — buffer length must exceed maximum expected dropout run.
- The MPC solution is *time-invariant* (unconstrained) or obtained from a *quadratic program* (constrained) — warm-starting speeds up on-line computation.
- Stability is guaranteed by the **terminal cost + terminal set** machinery (Module 5 connection in KTU syllabus).
- **Maximum tolerable delay** for stable NPCS operation is $\tau_{max} = (N_p - N_c)\,T_s$ (F10) — design accordingly.
- **Robustness** issues: Smith Predictor fails under model mismatch; GPC tolerates modest plant uncertainty; NPCS handles random delays but is sensitive to long burst losses.
- **Key KTU keywords to memorize:** *prediction horizon, control horizon, receding horizon, CARIMA, Diophantine equation, QP, control sequence packeter, actuator buffer, network-induced delay, packet dropout compensation, closed-loop characteristic equation*.
- **Common exam traps:** applying the full control sequence; forgetting the $G_m = G_p$ assumption; mis-stating the direction of the cost gradient; confusing Smith Predictor with MPC.
- **Engineering applications to mention in answers:** process control (refineries), autonomous vehicles, smart-grid load dispatch, tele-operated robotics, drone swarms over wireless links.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robust Control Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT632 - NETWORKED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robust Control Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Robust Control Techniques in NCS?

**Formal Definition:**
Robust Control Techniques refer to a family of controller design methodologies explicitly engineered to guarantee **closed-loop stability and prescribed performance** in the presence of *structured and unstructured uncertainties*, *time-varying delays*, *packet dropouts*, *quantization effects*, and *external disturbances* that are inherent in a Networked Control System (NCS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Robust control in NCS aims to synthesize a controller $K(s)$ such that the closed-loop system remains stable and satisfies a performance bound (typically $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ or $\mathcal{H}_2$ norm) **for all admissible uncertainties** $\Delta(s)$ and bounded network-induced phenomena.

**Core Uncertainties Addressed in NCS:**

| Phenomenon | Source | Mathematical Model |
|---|---|---|
| Network-Induced Delay $\tau_k$ | Sensor-to-Controller / Controller-to-Actuator | $\tau_k \in [\tau_{\min}, \tau_{\max}]$ |
| Packet Dropout | Network congestion, link failure | Bernoulli process $p(\delta_k)$ |
| Quantization Error | Finite word-length of digital channel | $\|e_q\| \leq \Delta_q$ |
| External Disturbance $w(t)$ | Load disturbance, sensor noise | $w(t) \in \mathcal{L}_2[0,\infty)$ |
| Parametric Uncertainty | Plant parameter drift | $\Delta A, \Delta B$ |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Smart Car on a Foggy Highway"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Imagine driving a car on a foggy highway:**
> - The **plant** is your car (the system to be controlled).
> - The **network** is the foggy highway (introduces random delays, dropped GPS signals, distorted lane markings).
> - The **controller** is the driver (steering, accelerating).
> - **Uncertainties**: Fog density varies (delay), GPS signal disappears (packet loss), lane markings are blurry (quantization).
> - A **robust controller** is a driver trained to handle *any* visibility condition without crashing, even if the exact fog density is unknown in advance. The driver does not need to know the exact delay — only that "some" delay exists within reasonable bounds.

## 1.3 Why is Robust Control Essential for NCS?

In classical control design, we assume ideal communication (zero delay, perfect transmission). In NCS, this assumption is **violated**. Robust control provides:

1. **Stability Margin** — Guarantees stability under worst-case delay scenarios.
2. **Performance Guarantee** — Maintains $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ disturbance attenuation $\gamma$ regardless of network condition.
3. **Design Discipline** — Uses convex optimization (LMIs) to systematically handle multi-constraint problems.

## 1.4 Fundamental Robust Control Paradigms in NCS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four Pillars of Robust NCS Design (KTU Module 3 Focus):**
> 1. **$\mathcal{H}_\infty$ Control** — Minimize worst-case gain from disturbance to regulated output.
> 2. **$\mathcal{H}_2$ Control** — Minimize the energy of the output due to white-noise disturbance.
> 3. **$\mu$-Synthesis** — Handle structured (parametric) uncertainty using structured singular value $\mu$.
> 4. **LMI-based Lyapunov-Krasovskii Design** — Delay-dependent stability and stabilization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Standard Robust NCS Block Diagram (in Frequency Domain)

Consider a generalized plant $G(s)$ with inputs partitioned as:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} z(s) \\ y(s) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} G_{11}(s) & G_{12}(s) \\ G_{21}(s) & G_{22}(s) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} w(s) \\ u(s) \end{bmatrix}, \quad u(s) = K(s)\,y(s)
$$

Where:
- $w(s)$ → exogenous disturbance input
- $u(s)$ → control input
- $z(s)$ → regulated output (performance signal)
- $y(s)$ → measured output
- $K(s)$ → robust controller to be designed

The **closed-loop transfer function** from $w$ to $z$ (lower linear fractional transformation) is:

$$T_{zw}(s) = \mathcal{F}_l(G, K) = G_{11} + G_{12}K(I - G_{22}K)^{-1}G_{21}$$

## 2.2 The $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ Control Problem

**Objective:** Find $K(s)$ such that:

$$\|T_{zw}(s)\|_\infty = \sup_{\omega \in \mathbb{R}} \bar{\sigma}\big(T_{zw}(j\omega)\big) < \gamma$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** The maximum energy amplification from any bounded disturbance $w(t) \in \mathcal{L}_2$ to the regulated output $z(t)$ is at most $\gamma$. A smaller $\gamma$ implies better disturbance rejection.

**Derivation Trigger — Bounded Real Lemma:**
For a stable system $T(s)$ with realization $(A,B,C,D)$, the condition $\|T\|_\infty < \gamma$ holds **if and only if** there exists $P = P^T > 0$ such that:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} A^T P + P A + C^T C & P B + C^T D \\ B^T P + D^T C & D^T D - \gamma^2 I \end{bmatrix} < 0
$$

## 2.3 The $\mathcal{H}_2$ Control Problem

**Objective:** Minimize

$$\|T_{zw}(s)\|_2^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \text{Trace}\big[T_{zw}^*(j\omega)\,T_{zw}(j\omega)\big]\,d\omega$$

**Dual Lyapunov Condition (for $\mathcal{H}_2$):** Requires $D = 0$ and existence of $P = P^T > 0$ with $A^T P + PA < 0$, with cost trace $(CPC^T) < \nu^2$.

## 2.4 The Structured Singular Value ($\mu$)

For a matrix $M \in \mathbb{C}^{n \times n}$ and a block-diagonal uncertainty structure $\Delta = \text{diag}(\Delta_1, \ldots, \Delta_k)$:

$$\mu_\Delta(M) = \frac{1}{\min\{\bar{\sigma}(\Delta) : \Delta \in \mathcal{B}_\Delta, \ \det(I - M\Delta) = 0\}}$$

**Robust Stability Criterion:** The closed-loop system is robustly stable for all $\|\Delta\|_\infty \leq 1$ if and only if

$$\sup_{\omega} \mu_\Delta\big(T_{zw}(j\omega)\big) < 1$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Key Condition / Use |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ norm | $\|T\|_\infty = \sup_\omega \bar{\sigma}(T(j\omega))$ | Disturbance rejection bound |
| $\mathcal{H}_2$ norm | $\|T\|_2^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int \text{tr}(T^*T)\,d\omega$ | White-noise response |
| Small-Gain Condition | $\|T_1\|_\infty \cdot \|T_2\|_\infty < 1$ | Robust stability baseline |
| Bounded Real Lemma | LMI in $P > 0$ | $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ feasibility |
| Lyapunov-Krasovskii (delay) | $V(t) = x^T P x + \int_{-h}^{0} x^T(t+\theta) Q x(t+\theta)\,d\theta$ | Time-delay stability |
| Razumikhin Function | $V(x(t)) \leq \theta V(x(t-h))$ for $\theta > 1$ | Delay-independent criterion |
| LMI Feasibility | $\mathcal{F}(X) = F_0 + \sum x_i F_i > 0$ | Convex optimization |
| $\mu$ Bound | $\mu_\Delta(M) \leq \inf_{D} \bar{\sigma}(DMD^{-1})$ | $D$-scale approximation |
| Guaranteed Cost | $J = \int_0^\infty (x^T Q x + u^T R u)\,dt \leq J^*$ | $H_\infty$ + quadratic cost |
| Network Delay Bound | $\tau_k \in [\tau_{\min}, \tau_{\max}]$ | Discrete-time NCS |
| Markov Jump System | $P\{\theta_{k+1} = j \vert \theta_k = i\} = \pi_{ij}$ | Random delay/dropout model |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Robust NCS techniques are deployed in **autonomous vehicle platoons** (handling V2V delay), **smart grid SCADA systems** (packet drop tolerance), **industrial IoT process control** (wireless sensor networks), and **telerobotic surgery** (guaranteed stability over long-distance networks).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Delay-Dependent $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ Stabilization via Lyapunov-Krasovskii Functional (LKF)

### 3.1.1 Plant Model with Network Delay

Consider a continuous-time NCS with state-space plant:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t - \tau(t)) + B_w w(t)
$$

$$
z(t) = C x(t) + D u(t - \tau(t))
$$

Where:
- $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the state
- $u(t) \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the delayed control input
- $w(t) \in \mathbb{R}^q$ is the disturbance
- $\tau(t)$ is the **time-varying network-induced delay** with $0 \leq \tau(t) \leq h$ and $\dot{\tau}(t) \leq \mu < 1$

### 3.1.2 Closed-Loop Substitution with Static State Feedback $u(t) = K x(t)$

After substitution, the closed-loop becomes:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B K x(t - \tau(t)) + B_w w(t)
$$

$$
z(t) = C x(t) + D K x(t - \tau(t))
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 1 — Choose the Lyapunov-Krasovskii Functional

We choose the augmented LKF:

$$
V(t, x_t) = x^T(t) P x(t) + \int_{t-\tau(t)}^{t} x^T(s) Q x(s)\,ds + \int_{-h}^{0} \int_{t+\theta}^{t} \dot{x}^T(s) R \dot{x}(s)\,ds\,d\theta
$$

Where $P > 0$, $Q > 0$, $R > 0$ are matrices to be determined.

### 3.1.4 Step 2 — Compute the Time Derivative of $V$

Differentiating term-by-term:

**Term 1:** $\frac{d}{dt}[x^T P x] = 2x^T P \dot{x} = 2x^T P (A x + BK x_\tau + B_w w)$

**Term 2:** $\frac{d}{dt}\int_{t-\tau(t)}^{t} x^T Q x\,ds = x^T Q x - (1-\dot{\tau}) x_\tau^T Q x_\tau \leq x^T Q x - (1-\mu) x_\tau^T Q x_\tau$

**Term 3:** $\frac{d}{dt}\int_{-h}^{0}\int_{t+\theta}^{t} \dot{x}^T R \dot{x}\,ds\,d\theta = h\dot{x}^T R \dot{x} - \int_{t-h}^{t} \dot{x}^T R \dot{x}\,ds$

### 3.1.5 Step 3 — Establish the $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ Performance Index

For disturbance attenuation level $\gamma > 0$, we require:

$$
J = \int_0^\infty \big[z^T z - \gamma^2 w^T w\big]\,dt < 0
$$

This is equivalent to showing $\dot{V} + z^T z - \gamma^2 w^T w \leq 0$ along trajectories.

### 3.1.6 Step 4 — Formulate the LMI

Using the descriptor-type bounding (Newton-Leibniz: $x(t) - x(t-\tau(t)) = \int_{t-\tau(t)}^{t} \dot{x}(s)\,ds$) and the Jensen's inequality, we arrive at the **delay-dependent LMI condition**:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \Phi_{11} & \Phi_{12} & P B_w & h A^T R \\ * & \Phi_{22} & 0 & h (BK)^T R \\ * & * & -\gamma^2 I & h B_w^T R \\ * & * & * & -h R \end{bmatrix} < 0
$$

Where:
- $\Phi_{11} = P A + A^T P + Q$
- $\Phi_{12} = P B K$
- $\Phi_{22} = -(1-\mu) Q$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why LMI Form?** The LMI is a **convex constraint** in variables $(P, Q, R, K, \gamma)$. It can be solved efficiently using interior-point methods (e.g., MATLAB LMI Toolbox, YALMIP, SeDuMi).

### 3.1.7 Step 5 — Linearization via Change of Variable

Since $P$ and $K$ appear as products (non-convex), perform congruence transformation. Define $X = P^{-1}$ and $Y = K X$. Pre- and post-multiply the LMI by $\text{diag}(X, X, I, X)$ to obtain the equivalent LMI in $(X, \tilde{Q}, \tilde{R}, Y, \gamma)$, which is **convex**.

### 3.1.8 Final Closed-Form Stability Theorem

> **Theorem (Delay-Dependent $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ Stabilization):**
> The closed-loop NCS with delay $\tau(t) \in [0,h]$, $\dot{\tau}(t) \leq \mu$, and controller $K = Y X^{-1}$ satisfies:
> 1. Asymptotic stability when $w(t) = 0$
> 2. $\|z\|_2 < \gamma \|w\|_2$ for all $w \in \mathcal{L}_2\setminus\{0\}$
>
> **if and only if** there exist matrices $X > 0$, $\tilde{Q} > 0$, $\tilde{R} > 0$, $Y$, and scalar $\gamma > 0$ satisfying the LMI derived above.

## 3.2 Derivation: Small-Gain Robust Stability Theorem

For two interconnected subsystems $T_1(s)$ and $T_2(s)$ in feedback with $\Delta$ (uncertainty block, $\|\Delta\|_\infty \leq 1$):

$$
T(s) = T_1(s) \big(I - T_2(s) \Delta\big)^{-1}
$$

**Step 1:** Use the identity: $\det(I - T_2 \Delta) = 0 \Rightarrow$ instability
**Step 2:** Suppose $T_2$ is stable. By norm inequality:

$$
\|T_1(I - T_2 \Delta)^{-1}\|_\infty \leq \frac{\|T_1\|_\infty}{1 - \|T_2\|_\infty \cdot \|\Delta\|_\infty}
$$

**Step 3:** For the closed-loop to remain stable for all $\|\Delta\|_\infty \leq 1$, we need the denominator strictly positive. This requires:

$$\|T_2\|_\infty < 1$$

> **Step 4 (Conclusion):** The **small-gain theorem** states that the uncertain closed-loop is robustly stable if and only if the **nominal loop gain's $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ norm is strictly less than unity**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generalized Robust NCS Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a Robust Networked Control System, capturing all components: plant, network-induced phenomena, and the robust controller in the loop.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Reference r of t"] --> B["Robust Controller K of s"]
    B -- "Control u of t" --> C["Network Actuator Channel"]
    C -- "Delayed u of t minus tau" --> D["Plant G of s"]
    D -- "Output y of t" --> E["Network Sensor Channel"]
    E -- "Delayed y of t minus tau" --> B
    D -- "Output y of t" --> F["Performance Output z of t"]
    G["Disturbance w of t"] -- "Injected" --> D
    H["Uncertainty Block Delta s"] -.->|"Norm less than gamma"| D

    classDef ctrl fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef net fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef plant fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef ext fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000
    class B ctrl
    class C,E net
    class D,F plant
    class G,H ext
```

**Interpretation of the Diagram:**

- **Orange Block (Controller $K(s)$):** The robust controller designed to minimize the $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ norm of the closed-loop transfer $T_{zw}$.
- **Blue Blocks (Network Channels):** Introduce time-varying delays $\tau_{sc}$ (sensor-to-controller) and $\tau_{ca}$ (controller-to-actuator), and possible packet dropouts.
- **Green Block (Plant $G(s)$):** The physical process with output $y(t)$ and performance output $z(t)$.
- **Pink Blocks (External Inputs):** Disturbance $w(t)$ and structured uncertainty $\Delta(s)$ perturbing the plant.

## 4.2 $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ Control Design Flow (Sequential Process)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Identify NCS Uncertainties"] --> S2["Step 2: Formulate Generalized Plant G of s"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Augment with Delay and Disturbance Weights W1 and W2"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Compute Nominal Controller via Riccati or LMI"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Solve LMI for Minimum Gamma"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Extract Robust Controller K of s"]
    S6 --> S7{"Step 7: Validate via mu analysis"}
    S7 -- "Pass" --> S8["Step 8: Deploy to NCS"]
    S7 -- "Fail" --> S2

    classDef step fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#6A5ACD,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6 step
    class S7 decision
    class S8 final
```

**Process Logic (Reading the Flow):**

1. **Step 1** identifies the specific NCS phenomena: delays, dropouts, disturbances.
2. **Steps 2–3** build the augmented plant $P(s)$ with weighting functions $W_1(s)$ (performance) and $W_2(s)$ (uncertainty).
3. **Step 4** applies either Riccati-based (Doyle-Glover) or LMI-based (Gahinet-Apkarian) solution.
4. **Step 5** minimizes $\gamma$ via a bisection or LMI eigenvalue problem.
5. **Step 7** performs $\mu$-analysis to confirm robust performance, not just stability.
6. **Step 8** deploys $K(s)$ to the actual networked hardware.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: Define $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ norm of a transfer function matrix. Why is it relevant to robust NCS design?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ norm of a stable transfer function $T(s)$ is defined as $\|T(s)\|_\infty = \sup_{\omega \in \mathbb{R}} \bar{\sigma}(T(j\omega))$, where $\bar{\sigma}$ denotes the maximum singular value. **(1 Mark)**
- It quantifies the **worst-case energy gain** from input to output across all frequencies. **(1 Mark)**
- In NCS, it is used to bound the effect of disturbances, delay-induced errors, and structured uncertainties on the regulated output, ensuring $\|z\|_2 \leq \gamma \|w\|_2$ for some prescribed attenuation level $\gamma$. **(1 Mark)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: State the Small-Gain Theorem. What role does it play in robust NCS stability analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The Small-Gain Theorem states that a feedback interconnection of two stable systems $T_1(s)$ and $T_2(s)$ is internally stable **if and only if** $\|T_1(s)\|_\infty \cdot \|T_2(s)\|_\infty < 1$. **(2 Marks)**
- In NCS, where delays and packet dropouts introduce multiplicative uncertainty, the theorem provides a sufficient condition for robust stability against bounded network-induced perturbations. **(1 Mark)**

---

## Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often **confuse $\mathcal{H}_2$ and $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ objectives** — remember $\mathcal{H}_2$ is the white-noise RMS response, while $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ is the worst-case energy gain.
> - In LMI problems, **forgetting the Schur complement** or mis-applying the congruence transformation results in incorrect convex formulations.
> - In delay-dependent analysis, students often **omit the $\dot{\tau} \leq \mu < 1$ bound**, which is critical for the LKF derivation.
> - **Always state assumptions explicitly** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks for ambiguous problem statements.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** For a continuous-time NCS described by
$$
\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t - \tau(t)) + B_w w(t), \quad z(t) = C x(t) + D u(t - \tau(t))
$$
where $\tau(t) \in [0, h]$ and $\dot{\tau}(t) \leq \mu < 1$, derive the **delay-dependent $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ stabilization conditions** using the Lyapunov-Krasovskii functional approach. Clearly state the LMI feasibility problem. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the plant
$$
G(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + 0.5 s + 1}
$$
with multiplicative output uncertainty $\Delta(s)$ satisfying $\|\Delta\|_\infty \leq 0.2$, design the weighting function $W(s)$ and apply the **Small-Gain Theorem** to determine the **maximum allowable controller gain** $K_{\max}$ such that robust stability is guaranteed. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Delay-Dependent $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ Stabilization (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Closed-Loop Dynamics (1 Mark)**
Apply static state-feedback $u(t) = K x(t)$:
$$
\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B K x(t - \tau(t)) + B_w w(t)
$$
$$
z(t) = C x(t) + D K x(t - \tau(t))
$$

**Step 2: Lyapunov-Krasovskii Functional (2 Marks)**
$$
V(t) = x^T(t) P x(t) + \int_{t-\tau(t)}^{t} x^T(s) Q x(s)\,ds + \int_{-h}^{0}\int_{t+\theta}^{t} \dot{x}^T(s) R \dot{x}(s)\,ds\,d\theta
$$
with $P, Q, R > 0$.

**Step 3: Derivative Bound (1 Mark)**
$$
\dot{V} \leq 2x^T P (A x + B K x_\tau + B_w w) + x^T Q x - (1-\mu) x_\tau^T Q x_\tau + h \dot{x}^T R \dot{x} - \int_{t-h}^{t} \dot{x}^T R \dot{x}\,ds
$$

**Step 4: $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ Performance Condition (1 Mark)**
Require $\dot{V} + z^T z - \gamma^2 w^T w \leq 0$. By completing the square and applying the descriptor method:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} \Phi_{11} & P B K & P B_w & h A^T \\ * & -(1-\mu)Q & 0 & h K^T B^T \\ * & * & -\gamma^2 I & h B_w^T \\ * & * & * & -h R^{-1} \end{bmatrix} < 0
$$
where $\Phi_{11} = P A + A^T P + Q + C^T C$.

**Step 5: Convexification via Change of Variable (1 Mark)**
Let $X = P^{-1}$, $\tilde{Q} = X Q X$, $\tilde{R} = X R X$, $Y = K X$. Pre/post-multiply by $\text{diag}(X, X, I, X)$ to obtain the LMI in $(X, \tilde{Q}, \tilde{R}, Y, \gamma)$.

**Step 6: Feasibility Statement (1 Mark)**
The system is delay-dependently stabilizable with $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ attenuation $\gamma$ **iff** this LMI has a solution. Solve using YALMIP/SeDuMi to get the minimal $\gamma$ and the controller $K = Y X^{-1}$.

---

**Part (b) — Small-Gain Design (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Form the Multiplicative Uncertainty Weight (2 Marks)**
The plant family is $\tilde{G}(s) = G(s)(1 + W(s) \Delta(s))$. Given the magnitude bound $|\Delta(j\omega)| \leq 0.2$ across all frequencies, the natural choice is the constant weight:
$$
W(s) = 0.2
$$
This bounds the multiplicative deviation: $|G(j\omega) - \tilde{G}(j\omega)| / |G(j\omega)| \leq 0.2$.

**Step 2: Form the Closed-Loop Sensitivity Function (1 Mark)**
With proportional controller $K$, the sensitivity is:
$$
S(s) = \frac{1}{1 + K G(s)}, \quad T(s) = \frac{K G(s)}{1 + K G(s)}
$$
The complementary sensitivity satisfies $T(s) \approx K G(s)$ for small $K$.

**Step 3: Apply Robust Stability Condition (2 Marks)**
Robust stability requires: $\|W(s) T(s)\|_\infty < 1$. Substituting $W = 0.2$:
$$
0.2 \cdot \|T(s)\|_\infty < 1 \Rightarrow \|T(s)\|_\infty < 5
$$
For low frequencies, $T(j\omega) \approx K G(j\omega)$. At the resonant peak $\omega_n = 1$ rad/s where $|G(j\omega_n)| = 1/|1 \cdot e^{j\theta}| = 1$:
$$
\|T(j1)\|_\infty = \frac{K \cdot 1}{\sqrt{(1-K \cdot 1)^2 + (K \cdot 0.5)^2}} < 5
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $K_{\max}$ (1 Mark)**
For $K = 1$: $\|T\| = 1/\sqrt{0 + 0.5^2} = 2 < 5$ ✓
For $K = 4$: $\|T\| = 4/\sqrt{9 + 4} = 4/\sqrt{13} \approx 1.11 < 5$ ✓
For $K = 5$: $\|T\| = 5/\sqrt{16 + 6.25} = 5/\sqrt{22.25} \approx 1.06 < 5$ ✓
The binding constraint becomes the small-gain bound $\|T\|_\infty < 5$, which is satisfied for $K \leq 5$. Therefore:
$$
\boxed{K_{\max} = 5}
$$

**Step 5: Concluding Statement (1 Mark)**
The choice $K \in (0, 5]$ ensures robust stability against the bounded multiplicative uncertainty $\|\Delta\|_\infty \leq 0.2$. A practical design uses $K = 2$ for comfortable stability margins.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of **structured singular value ($\mu$)** and the **$\mu$-synthesis** procedure for robust controller design. How does it differ from classical $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ design? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For an NCS with sensor-to-controller delay $\tau_{sc} = 0.05$ s, controller-to-actuator delay $\tau_{ca} = 0.08$ s, and packet dropout probability $p = 0.1$, model the NCS as a **Markov Jump Linear System (MJLS)** and write the **stochastic stability condition** using a Lyapunov function. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — $\mu$-Synthesis (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Definition of $\mu$ (2 Marks)**
The structured singular value $\mu_\Delta(M)$ of a complex matrix $M$ with respect to a block-diagonal uncertainty structure $\Delta = \text{diag}(\Delta_1, \ldots, \Delta_k)$ is:
$$
\mu_\Delta(M) = \frac{1}{\min\{\bar{\sigma}(\Delta) : \Delta \in \mathcal{B}_\Delta, \ \det(I - M\Delta) = 0\}}
$$
If no $\Delta$ makes $\det(I - M\Delta) = 0$, then $\mu_\Delta(M) = 0$.

**Step 2: Robust Performance Criterion (1 Mark)**
The closed-loop system has **robust performance** (stability + performance under all $\|\Delta_i\| \leq 1$) **if and only if**:
$$
\sup_{\omega} \mu_{\tilde{\Delta}}\big(F_l(P, K)(j\omega)\big) < 1
$$
where $\tilde{\Delta}$ is the augmented uncertainty including a fictitious performance block.

**Step 3: The $\mu$-Synthesis Procedure (2 Marks)**
1. Form the interconnection structure $P(s)$ with $\Delta$-blocks.
2. Solve a series of $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ problems:
   - At iteration $i$, compute $D_i(\omega)$ to minimize $\bar{\sigma}(D_i F_l(P,K) D_i^{-1})$.
   - Scale: $P_i = D_i P D_i^{-1}$ and solve $\min_K \|F_l(P_i, K)\|_\infty$.
3. Iterate until $\sup_\omega \mu < 1$ is achieved (this is the **D-K iteration**).

**Step 4: Comparison with $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ (2 Marks)**

| Aspect | $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ | $\mu$-Synthesis |
|---|---|---|
| Uncertainty | Unstructured (full $\Delta$) | Structured (per-block) |
| Conservativeness | Often conservative | Less conservative |
| Performance Criterion | $\\|T_{zw}\\|_\infty < 1$ | $\mu_{\tilde{\Delta}}(T_{zw}) < 1$ |
| Computational Cost | Single LMI/Riccati | Iterative $D$-$K$ |
| KTU Exam Tip | One-shot design | Multi-step (D-K iteration) |

---

**Part (b) — MJLS Modeling of NCS with Delay and Dropout (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Identify the Markov Modes (2 Marks)**
The NCS has two stochastic phenomena:
- **Delay state $\theta_k$**: The total round-trip delay $\tau_k = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$ takes values in $\{0.05, 0.08, 0.13, 0.16\}$ s.
- **Dropout state $\delta_k \in \{0, 1\}$**: Bernoulli with $P(\delta_k = 1) = 1 - p = 0.9$.

Combine into the mode $\sigma_k = (\theta_k, \delta_k) \in \mathcal{S} = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$.

**Step 2: Define the Transition Probability Matrix $\Pi$ (1 Mark)**
$$
\Pi = [\pi_{ij}] \in \mathbb{R}^{8 \times 8}, \quad \pi_{ij} = P\{\sigma_{k+1} = j \vert \sigma_k = i\}, \quad \sum_j \pi_{ij} = 1
$$
The transition rates are estimated from the network QoS statistics.

**Step 3: Write the MJLS Dynamics (1 Mark)**
$$
x_{k+1} = A_{\sigma_k} x_k + B_{\sigma_k} u_k + B_{w,\sigma_k} w_k
$$
$$
z_k = C_{\sigma_k} x_k + D_{\sigma_k} u_k
$$
where the matrices $A_{\sigma_k}, B_{\sigma_k}, C_{\sigma_k}$ switch according to the current delay/dropout mode.

**Step 4: Stochastic Stability via Lyapunov (2 Marks)**
Define the mode-dependent Lyapunov function $V(\sigma_k, x_k) = x_k^T P_{\sigma_k} x_k$ with $P_i > 0$ for all $i \in \mathcal{S}$.

**Theorem (Mean-Square Stability):** The MJLS is stochastically stable if and only if there exist $P_i > 0$ for each mode $i$ such that:
$$
\sum_{j \in \mathcal{S}} \pi_{ij} \big[A_i^T P_j A_i - P_i\big] < 0, \quad \forall i \in \mathcal{S}
$$
This is an LMI feasibility problem in $\{P_1, \ldots, P_8\}$.

**Step 5: $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ MJLS Extension (1 Mark)**
For disturbance attenuation $\gamma$, require additionally:
$$
\sum_{j \in \mathcal{S}} \pi_{ij} \big[A_i^T P_j A_i - P_i + C_i^T C_i + \gamma^{-2} A_i^T P_j B_{w,i} B_{w,i}^T P_j A_i\big] < 0
$$

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Robust Control Techniques):**

- ✅ **$\mathcal{H}_\infty$ Norm** = $\sup_\omega \bar{\sigma}(T(j\omega))$ → worst-case energy gain.
- ✅ **$\mathcal{H}_2$ Norm** = RMS response to white noise → energy minimization.
- ✅ **$\mu$-Synthesis** handles structured uncertainty with less conservatism than $\mathcal{H}_\infty$.
- ✅ **Small-Gain Theorem** is the simplest robust stability test: $\|M\|_\infty \cdot \|\Delta\|_\infty < 1$.
- ✅ **Bounded Real Lemma** is the LMI bridge to $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ feasibility.
- ✅ **Lyapunov-Krasovskii Functional** is the go-to tool for **time-delay** stability (uses integral terms over delay interval).
- ✅ **Razumikhin Function** is the simpler delay-independent alternative.
- ✅ **MJLS** models random delay and packet dropout in NCS with mode-dependent Lyapunov matrices $P_i$.
- ✅ **Linearization Trick**: Use $X = P^{-1}$ and $Y = KX$ to convert $P, K$ bilinear terms to convex LMIs.
- ✅ **LMI Tools**: YALMIP, SeDuMi, MATLAB LMI Toolbox for computational solution.
- ✅ **Key Delay Bound**: $0 \leq \tau(t) \leq h$ and $\dot{\tau}(t) \leq \mu < 1$ (derivative bound is **essential**).
- ✅ **Multiplicative Uncertainty** $\to$ weight on $T(s)$; **Additive Uncertainty** $\to$ weight on $K(s)S(s)$.
- ✅ **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern**: Always has 2 sub-parts (7 + 7), one theoretical + one numerical.
- ✅ **For 3-mark questions**: State the definition + 1-line significance + 1 example — concise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event-Triggered and Self-Triggered Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT632 - NETWORKED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Event-Triggered and Self-Triggered Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

In Networked Control Systems (NCS), traditional periodic (time-triggered) sampling wastes communication bandwidth, battery power, and computational resources when the system state is nearly constant or varies predictably. The KTU 2024 Scheme defines two modern resource-aware control paradigms under Module 3:

**Event-Triggered Control (ETC):** A control execution strategy in which the control input is updated only when a predefined state- or output-dependent triggering condition is violated. The condition is evaluated continuously (or at a fast inner-loop rate) and fires an "event" when the deviation between the current state $x(t)$ and the last transmitted state $x(t_k)$ exceeds a designer-specified threshold.

**Self-Triggered Control (STC):** A predictive variant in which the controller itself computes the next execution instant $t_{k+1}$ based on the current state $x(t_k)$, eliminating the need for a dedicated event-detection hardware module. STC embeds the triggering logic into the controller's algorithm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:** The fundamental distinction is **"who decides when to recompute the control"** — in ETC, the *plant-side monitor* decides; in STC, the *controller itself* predicts.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are driving a car on a long, straight highway with cruise control set to **100 km/h**:

- **Time-Triggered (Periodic):** You press the accelerator pedal every **5 seconds**, regardless of whether the car needs it. This wastes fuel when the car is already steady.
- **Event-Triggered:** A sensor continuously watches the speedometer. You press the pedal *only when* the speed drops below **99 km/h** or rises above **101 km/h**. A "tolerance band" $\sigma$ controls how often you act.
- **Self-Triggered:** Based on the current speed, road slope, and wind, you *predict* — *"I will need to press the pedal again in 7 seconds."* No continuous speed monitoring is required; your brain does the math upfront.

The "tolerance band" is the **relative threshold $\sigma$**, and "how long until the next press" is the **inter-event time $\tau_k$**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (in **bold**)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Relative triggering threshold | $\sigma$ | $\mathbf{0 < \sigma < 1}$ |
| Inter-event time | $\tau_k = t_{k+1} - t_k$ | $\mathbf{> 0}$ (Zeno-free) |
| Measurement error | $e(t) = x(t_k) - x(t)$ | bounded by design |
| Lyapunov decay rate | $\alpha$ | $\mathbf{> 0}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The condition $\tau_k > 0$ for all $k$ is known as the **Zeno-free property** — KTU examiners frequently test this concept to ensure students understand that an infinite number of events in finite time must be avoided.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Sampling Paradigms — A Structured Comparison

Let the plant be modeled as a continuous-time LTI system:

$$
\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t), \quad x(0) = x_0
$$

where $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the state and $u(t) \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the control input.

**A. Time-Triggered Control (Baseline)**

- The control is updated at fixed instants $t_k = k h$, where $h > 0$ is the sampling period.
- Why: Simple to analyze, predictable bandwidth usage.
- How: $u(t) = K x(t_k)$ for $t \in [t_k, t_{k+1})$.
- Limitation: Wastes resources when the system is in steady state.

**B. Event-Triggered Control (ETC)**

- The error signal is defined as $e(t) = x(t_k) - x(t)$ for $t \in [t_k, t_{k+1})$.
- The event detector monitors the condition:
  $$\| e(t) \| > \sigma \| x(t) \|$$
- Why: Communication/sensing happens only when the state has drifted "enough" from its last sampled value.
- How: A trigger fires the instant the inequality is violated; the new state is broadcast to the controller.
- Limitation: Requires a dedicated, continuously running monitoring subsystem (extra hardware/software burden).

**C. Self-Triggered Control (STC)**

- The next triggering time is computed as $t_{k+1} = t_k + \tau(x(t_k))$, where $\tau(\cdot)$ is a state-dependent function.
- Why: Eliminates the dedicated monitor; embeds the trigger decision inside the controller firmware.
- How: At every execution instant, the controller solves a small optimization to estimate the maximum time before the ETC condition would be violated, and schedules the next update accordingly.
- Limitation: Often more conservative (longer average inter-event times than ETC, but at the cost of more controller computation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** STC = ETC with *precomputed* triggering times. STC trades **online monitoring effort** for **offline predictive computation**. Always state this trade-off explicitly in 14-mark answers.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plant dynamics | $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ | LTI continuous-time system |
| 2 | State-feedback law | $u(t) = K x(t_k)$ | Zero-order-hold between events |
| 3 | Measurement error | $e(t) = x(t_k) - x(t)$ | Drift since last event |
| 4 | Event-triggering condition | $\Vert e(t) \Vert > \sigma \Vert x(t) \Vert$ | Fires when drift exceeds threshold |
| 5 | Closed-loop dynamics | $\dot{x}(t) = (A + BK) x(t) + BK e(t)$ | Error acts as disturbance |
| 6 | Lyapunov function | $V(x) = x^T P x, \quad P = P^T > 0$ | Quadratic energy function |
| 7 | Stability condition | $\dot{V}(x) \leq -2\alpha V(x) + \beta \Vert e(t) \Vert^2$ | Exponential decay bound |
| 8 | Input-to-state stability | $\sigma \Vert x \Vert \Vert BK \Vert < \alpha \lambda_{\min}(P) / \lambda_{\max}(P)$ | Coupling of $\sigma$ and stability |
| 9 | Inter-event time (ETC) | $\tau_k \geq \frac{1}{\Vert A + BK \Vert} \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{\sigma}{\rho}\right)$ | Lower bound — Zeno-free |
| 10 | Self-triggered update | $t_{k+1} = t_k + \tau(x(t_k))$ | Predicted next execution |
| 11 | Average sampling rate | $f_{avg} = \frac{1}{\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \sum_k \tau_k}$ | Resource consumption metric |
| 12 | Minimum data rate | $R_{min} = \frac{n \log_2(1+\sigma)}{f_{avg}}$ | Bits/s for reliable control |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $\rho = \Vert BK \Vert / \lambda_{\min}(P)$ is the **normalized disturbance gain**, and $\alpha, \beta > 0$ are tunable decay parameters. The KTU board often asks students to **derive the lower bound on $\tau_k$** — see Section 3 for the full derivation.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN):** Battery-powered nodes transmit only on critical events, extending lifetime from days to **years**.
- **Automotive Cyber-Physical Systems:** CAN/LIN buses offload brake-by-wire updates only when wheel slip exceeds a threshold, reducing bus traffic by **60–80%**.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** PLCs use ETC to coordinate hundreds of actuators without flooding the fieldbus.
- **Autonomous UAV Swarms:** Self-triggered coordination enables bandwidth-efficient swarm communication, where each drone predicts when it must rebroadcast its position.
- **Smart Grids:** Event-triggered demand-response signals cut down cellular data costs in remote substations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Dynamics with Measurement Error

**Given:** Plant $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ and a zero-order-hold event-triggered controller $u(t) = K x(t_k)$ for $t \in [t_k, t_{k+1})$.

**Step 1 — Substitute the control law into the plant:**

$$
\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B K x(t_k)
$$

**Step 2 — Decompose $x(t_k)$ using the error definition $e(t) = x(t_k) - x(t)$:**

$$
x(t_k) = x(t) + e(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Insert the decomposition back into the closed-loop equation:**

$$
\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B K \bigl[ x(t) + e(t) \bigr]
$$

**Step 4 — Group the state terms and the error terms separately:**

$$
\dot{x}(t) = (A + B K)\, x(t) + B K\, e(t)
$$

This is the **fundamental ETC closed-loop model** used throughout KTU Module 3 derivations. The closed-loop is a nominal stable system $A + BK$ (assuming $K$ is designed via LQR/Pole-placement) plus a bounded perturbation term $B K e(t)$ that is *intentionally* kept small by the triggering rule.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Lyapunov-Based Stability of ETC

**Goal:** Show that the ETC system is **input-to-state stable (ISS)** with respect to the error $e(t)$, and that the triggering rule ensures $\| e(t) \| \leq \sigma \| x(t) \|$.

**Step 1 — Choose a quadratic Lyapunov candidate:**

$$
V(x) = x^T P x, \quad P = P^T \succ 0
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate along the trajectories of the closed-loop system:**

$$
\dot{V}(x) = \dot{x}^T P x + x^T P \dot{x}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\dot{x}(t) = (A + BK) x + B K e$:**

$$
\dot{V}(x) = \bigl[(A + BK) x + B K e\bigr]^T P x + x^T P \bigl[(A + BK) x + B K e\bigr]
$$

**Step 4 — Expand and group:**

$$
\dot{V}(x) = x^T (A + BK)^T P x + e^T (BK)^T P x + x^T P (A + BK) x + x^T P B K\, e
$$

**Step 5 — Use the symmetric property $(BK)^T P x = x^T P BK$ (since $P$ is symmetric and the term is a scalar):**

$$
\dot{V}(x) = x^T \bigl[ (A + BK)^T P + P (A + BK) \bigr] x + 2 x^T P B K\, e
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the standard Lyapunov inequality for nominal stability:**

If $K$ is designed such that $(A + BK)^T P + P (A + BK) \preceq -2 \alpha P$ for some $\alpha > 0$, then:

$$
\dot{V}(x) \leq -2 \alpha x^T P x + 2 x^T P B K\, e
$$

**Step 7 — Apply the Cauchy–Schwarz / Young's inequality $2 \vert x^T P B K e \vert \leq \frac{1}{\varepsilon} x^T P B K B K^T P x + \varepsilon \Vert e \Vert^2$.**

After algebraic manipulation and choosing $\varepsilon$ optimally, the bound becomes:

$$
\dot{V}(x) \leq -2 \alpha V(x) + \beta \Vert e(t) \Vert^2
$$

where $\beta = \lambda_{\max}\!\bigl(P B K B K^T P\bigr) / \alpha$.

**Step 8 — Apply the triggering condition $\| e(t) \| \leq \sigma \| x(t) \|$:**

$$
\dot{V}(x) \leq -2 \alpha V(x) + \beta \sigma^2 \Vert x(t) \Vert^2
$$

**Step 9 — Use the spectral bounds $\lambda_{\min}(P) \Vert x \Vert^2 \leq V(x) \leq \lambda_{\max}(P) \Vert x \Vert^2$:**

$$
\dot{V}(x) \leq -2 \alpha V(x) + \frac{\beta \sigma^2}{\lambda_{\min}(P)} V(x)
$$

**Step 10 — Combine terms to obtain the ISS-like decay condition:**

$$
\dot{V}(x) \leq -2 \left[ \alpha - \frac{\beta \sigma^2}{2 \lambda_{\min}(P)} \right] V(x)
$$

**Step 11 — Final stability condition (the KTU golden result):**

For asymptotic stability, we require:

$$
\sigma < \sqrt{\frac{2 \alpha\, \lambda_{\min}(P)}{\beta}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \alpha\, \lambda_{\min}(P)}{\lambda_{\max}(P B K B K^T P)}} \cdot \sqrt{\lambda_{\min}(P)}
$$

Equivalently, in the simplified textbook form expected in KTU exams:

$$
\sigma < \frac{\alpha \lambda_{\min}(P)}{\Vert P B K \Vert \lambda_{\max}(P)}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Minimum Inter-Event Time (Zeno-Free Guarantee)

**Goal:** Prove $\tau_k = t_{k+1} - t_k > 0$ for all $k$, ensuring no Zeno behavior.

**Step 1 — The measurement error grows according to the closed-loop dynamics. Differentiate:**

$$
\dot{e}(t) = -\dot{x}(t) = -(A + BK) x(t) - B K e(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Bound the error growth using norms:**

$$
\frac{d}{dt} \Vert e(t) \Vert \leq \Vert (A + BK) x(t) \Vert + \Vert B K \Vert \Vert e(t) \Vert
$$

**Step 3 — Since the event has *not* fired, we have $\| e(t) \| \leq \sigma \| x(t) \|$. Substituting:**

$$
\frac{d}{dt} \Vert e(t) \Vert \leq \Vert A + BK \Vert \Vert x(t) \Vert + \Vert B K \Vert \sigma \Vert x(t) \Vert
$$

**Step 4 — Factor $\Vert x(t) \Vert$:**

$$
\frac{d}{dt} \Vert e(t) \Vert \leq \bigl( \Vert A + BK \Vert + \sigma \Vert B K \Vert \bigr) \Vert x(t) \Vert
$$

**Step 5 — Define $\rho = \Vert A + BK \Vert + \sigma \Vert B K \Vert$. The error grows at most exponentially until the trigger fires:**

$$
\Vert e(t) \Vert \leq \int_{t_k}^{t} \rho \Vert x(s) \Vert\, ds
$$

**Step 6 — The trigger fires when $\| e(t) \| = \sigma \| x(t) \|$. Solving this differential inequality yields the minimum inter-event time:**

$$
\tau_k \geq \frac{1}{\Vert A + BK \Vert} \ln\!\left( 1 + \frac{\sigma \Vert x(t_k) \Vert}{\Vert B K \Vert \Vert x(t_k) \Vert} \right) = \frac{1}{\Vert A + BK \Vert} \ln\!\left( 1 + \frac{\sigma}{\rho} \right)
$$

**Step 7 — Since $0 < \sigma < 1$ and $\rho > 0$, the logarithm is positive and finite, giving $\tau_k > 0$. This is the Zeno-free guarantee.**

> [!NOTE]
> The "$\ln$" appearing here is a direct consequence of the linear differential inequality. KTU students often forget the $1 / \Vert A + BK \Vert$ factor and lose 1–2 marks.

### 3.4 Derivation: Self-Triggered Next Execution Time

**Step 1 — At $t = t_k$, the controller has $x(t_k)$ available. The next trigger time must satisfy the ETC condition at the boundary:**

$$
\Vert e(t_{k+1}) \Vert = \sigma \Vert x(t_{k+1}) \Vert
$$

**Step 2 — Approximate the future state using the closed-loop dynamics, treating $e \approx 0$ over the prediction horizon (conservative but standard):**

$$
x(t_{k+1}) \approx e^{(A + BK) \tau_k} x(t_k)
$$

**Step 3 — Approximate the future error using the same linear growth bound derived in Section 3.3:**

$$
e(t_{k+1}) \approx \int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} e^{(A + BK)(t_{k+1} - s)} BK\, x(t_k)\, ds
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral and solve for $\tau_k$:**

$$
\tau_k = \frac{1}{\Vert A + BK \Vert} \ln\!\left( 1 + \frac{\sigma}{\Vert B K \Vert \Vert (A + BK)^{-1} \Vert} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — General form: $\tau_k = f\bigl( x(t_k),\, A,\, B,\, K,\, \sigma \bigr)$ — i.e., the next triggering time is a function of the current state and plant/controller parameters.**

In practice, for time-invariant LTI systems, $\tau_k$ often becomes a *constant* $\tau$ (independent of $x$), which simplifies implementation enormously.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing Between ETC and STC

| Criterion | Time-Triggered | Event-Triggered | Self-Triggered |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resource usage | High | Low | Lowest (no monitor) |
| Hardware complexity | Low | Medium | Low (compute-heavy) |
| Average inter-event time | $h$ (fixed) | $\tau_{avg} \gg h$ | $\tau_{avg} \geq \tau_{ETC}$ |
| Zeno-free? | Yes (by design) | Requires proof | Requires proof |
| Real-time guarantee | Strict | Statistical | Statistical |
| Controller computation | Standard | Standard | Increased (predictive) |
| Best for | Safety-critical hard RT | Bandwidth-limited | Battery-limited |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1 — Event-Triggered Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[Plant xdot equals A x plus B u]
    nodeS[Sensor y equals C x]
    nodeED[Event Detector Monitors e and x]
    nodeZB[Zero Order Hold Holds x sub tk]
    nodeC[Controller u equals K x sub tk]
    nodeN[Network Channel]
    nodeP --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeED
    nodeED -- Trigger Fired --> nodeN
    nodeED -- No Trigger --> nodeS
    nodeN --> nodeZB
    nodeZB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeP
```

**Interpretation:** The sensor continuously reads $x(t)$ and forwards it to the **Event Detector** block. The detector compares $\| e(t) \|$ against $\sigma \| x(t) \|$; if the inequality is violated, it sends the state across the network to the controller, which then updates the zero-order hold and the actuator signal.

---

### 4.2 Figure 2 — Self-Triggered Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP2[Plant xdot equals A x plus B u]
    nodeS2[Sensor y equals C x]
    nodeST[Self Trigger Scheduler Computes t sub k plus 1]
    nodeP2 --> nodeS2
    nodeS2 -- At Scheduled Instant --> nodeST
    nodeST -- Next Trigger Time Prediction --> nodeS2
    nodeST -- Update Trigger Time --> nodeC2[Controller u equals K x sub tk]
    nodeC2 --> nodeP2
```

**Interpretation:** The sensor *waits* until the scheduled time $t_{k+1}$, samples the plant, and then the **Self-Trigger Scheduler** block computes the next execution instant based on the freshly acquired state. There is no continuously running event detector.

---

### 4.3 Figure 3 — Triggering Timeline Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeT0[t equals 0 Plant starts]
    nodeTT1[Time Triggered Fixed ticks at 0, 1, 2, 3, 4]
    nodeET1[Event Triggered Ticks at 0, 1.2, 1.5, 2.8, 3.1]
    nodeST1[Self Triggered Ticks at 0, 1.4, 2.6, 3.5]
    nodeT0 --> nodeTT1
    nodeT0 --> nodeET1
    nodeT0 --> nodeST1
```

**Interpretation:** Under identical disturbance conditions, the time-triggered system uses the most transmissions, the event-triggered system uses fewer (only when the state drifts significantly), and the self-triggered system is the most conservative, often firing slightly less frequently than ETC because the prediction is approximate.

---

### 4.4 Figure 4 — Block-Level Functional Flow of Event Detection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX[Current State x of t]
    nodeE[Stored State x sub tk]
    nodeD[Error Computed e equals x sub tk minus x of t]
    nodeTH[Threshold Calculator sigma times x of t]
    nodeCMP[Comparator Triggers if e greater than threshold]
    nodeOUT[Event Signal Output]
    nodeX --> nodeD
    nodeE --> nodeD
    nodeX --> nodeTH
    nodeD --> nodeCMP
    nodeTH --> nodeCMP
    nodeCMP --> nodeOUT
```

**Interpretation:** This is the internal logic of the Event Detector block. The error $e(t)$ is recomputed at every inner-loop tick and compared to the dynamically generated threshold $\sigma \| x(t) \|$. The output is a binary event signal that travels up the architecture to the network and controller.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECPT632]**
*Distinguish between event-triggered control (ETC) and self-triggered control (STC) in networked control systems. State one key advantage and one limitation of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **ETC:** Control is updated only when a state-dependent event condition (e.g., $\| e(t) \| > \sigma \| x(t) \|$) is violated. **Advantage:** Achieves higher resource savings because triggers adapt to actual state behavior. **Limitation:** Requires a continuously running event detector hardware/software module.
- **STC:** The controller itself predicts the next execution instant $t_{k+1}$ using $t_{k+1} = t_k + \tau(x(t_k))$. **Advantage:** Eliminates the dedicated event monitor. **Limitation:** Predictions are typically conservative, leading to slightly longer inter-event times and increased controller-side computation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECPT632]**
*Define the "Zeno phenomenon" in the context of event-triggered control. Why must it be avoided, and how is the Zeno-free property typically established mathematically?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Zeno phenomenon:** Occurs when an infinite number of triggering events happen in a finite time interval, i.e., $\lim_{k \to \infty} t_k < \infty$.
- **Why it must be avoided:** Zeno behavior is physically unrealizable, overloads the communication network, and defeats the purpose of resource-aware control.
- **Mathematical establishment:** Proving a strictly positive lower bound on the inter-event time, $\tau_k = t_{k+1} - t_k \geq \tau_{\min} > 0$ for all $k$. This is typically done by bounding the error growth via the closed-loop dynamics, yielding $\tau_{\min} = \frac{1}{\Vert A+BK \Vert} \ln(1 + \sigma/\rho)$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the closed-loop dynamics of a continuous-time LTI plant $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ under an event-triggered state-feedback controller $u(t) = K x(t_k)$ for $t \in [t_k, t_{k+1})$. Clearly define the measurement error $e(t)$ and obtain the perturbed closed-loop form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using a quadratic Lyapunov function $V(x) = x^T P x$, derive the condition on the relative threshold $\sigma$ that ensures asymptotic stability of the event-triggered closed-loop system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (14 marks):**

**(a) — Closed-Loop Dynamics (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the measurement error signal between two consecutive event instants:
$$e(t) = x(t_k) - x(t), \quad t \in [t_k, t_{k+1})$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Equivalently, the last broadcast state can be written as:
$$x(t_k) = x(t) + e(t)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute the event-triggered control law $u(t) = K x(t_k)$ into the plant:
$$\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B K x(t_k)$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Replace $x(t_k)$ using the error decomposition:
$$\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B K \bigl[ x(t) + e(t) \bigr]$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Rearrange to obtain the standard perturbed closed-loop form:
$$\dot{x}(t) = (A + B K) x(t) + B K e(t)$$

This shows the closed-loop system as a nominal stable system $(A+BK)x$ perturbed by an error-driven disturbance $BK e(t)$. The design of $K$ (e.g., via LQR ensuring $A+BK$ is Hurwitz) and the event-triggering rule on $e(t)$ jointly determine stability.

---

**(b) — Lyapunov Stability Condition on $\sigma$ (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Choose the quadratic Lyapunov candidate:
$$V(x) = x^T P x, \quad P = P^T \succ 0$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Differentiate along the closed-loop trajectory from part (a):
$$\dot{V}(x) = \dot{x}^T P x + x^T P \dot{x} = 2 x^T P \dot{x}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute $\dot{x}(t) = (A+BK) x + B K e$:
$$\dot{V}(x) = 2 x^T P (A + BK) x + 2 x^T P B K e$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Use the Lyapunov inequality $(A + BK)^T P + P (A + BK) \preceq -2 \alpha P$ (which holds for some $\alpha > 0$ since $A + BK$ is Hurwitz):
$$\dot{V}(x) \leq -2 \alpha x^T P x + 2 x^T P B K e$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply Young's inequality $2 \vert x^T P B K e \vert \leq \frac{1}{\alpha} x^T P B K B K^T P x + \alpha \Vert e \Vert^2$. After substitution and using $\lambda_{\min}(P) \Vert x \Vert^2 \leq V(x)$:
$$\dot{V}(x) \leq -2 \alpha V(x) + \frac{\beta}{\lambda_{\min}(P)} \Vert e \Vert^2$$
where $\beta = \Vert P B K \Vert^2 / \alpha$.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Apply the event-triggering condition $\| e(t) \| \leq \sigma \| x(t) \|$:
$$\dot{V}(x) \leq -2 \alpha V(x) + \frac{\beta \sigma^2}{\lambda_{\min}(P)} \Vert x \Vert^2 \leq -2 \alpha V(x) + \frac{\beta \sigma^2 V(x)}{\lambda_{\min}(P) \lambda_{\max}(P)}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** For asymptotic stability, we require the coefficient of $V(x)$ to be strictly negative:
$$\alpha > \frac{\beta \sigma^2}{2 \lambda_{\min}(P) \lambda_{\max}(P)}$$

Solving for $\sigma$:

$$
\sigma < \sqrt{\frac{2 \alpha\, \lambda_{\min}(P)\, \lambda_{\max}(P)}{\beta}} = \frac{\alpha\, \lambda_{\min}(P)}{\Vert P B K \Vert \lambda_{\max}(P)} \cdot \sqrt{\lambda_{\max}(P)}
$$

The simplified KTU-board-expected form is:

$$
\boxed{\sigma < \frac{\alpha\, \lambda_{\min}(P)}{\Vert P B K \Vert\, \lambda_{\max}(P)}}
$$

This explicitly couples the threshold to the decay rate $\alpha$ and the controller/observer gain $K$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** What is a self-triggered control scheme? With a neat architecture diagram, explain how it differs from event-triggered control. State the formula used to compute the next execution instant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A second-order LTI plant has $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$, $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$, and controller $K = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$. Given the triggering threshold $\sigma = 0.1$, compute the minimum inter-event time $\tau_{\min}$ using the formula $\tau_{\min} = \frac{1}{\Vert A + BK \Vert} \ln(1 + \sigma / \Vert BK \Vert)$. Verify that the system is Zeno-free. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (14 marks):**

**(a) — Self-Triggered Scheme Explanation (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A self-triggered control (STC) scheme is a resource-aware control strategy in which the controller itself, based on the current sampled state $x(t_k)$, predicts and pre-schedules the next instant $t_{k+1}$ at which the control law must be updated. The triggering decision is made *inside* the controller's algorithm — no external event detector monitors the plant continuously.

**Architectural Difference from ETC (3 marks):**

- **In ETC:** A separate *event detector* continuously samples the plant, computes the error $e(t) = x(t_k) - x(t)$, and compares it to $\sigma \| x(t) \|$. Communication is triggered reactively.
- **In STC:** The detector is replaced by an *embedded scheduler* inside the controller. After each update, the controller computes $t_{k+1} = t_k + \tau(x(t_k))$ and the sensor is awakened only at that predicted instant. Communication is triggered proactively.

The student should draw a simple block diagram showing: Plant → Sensor → Scheduled Sampler → Self-Trigger Scheduler → Controller → Plant, with a feedback arrow from the scheduler to the sampler indicating the predicted next sampling instant.

**Formula (2 marks):**

$$t_{k+1} = t_k + \tau(x(t_k)), \quad \tau(x(t_k)) = \frac{1}{\Vert A + BK \Vert} \ln\!\left( 1 + \frac{\sigma \Vert x(t_k) \Vert}{\Vert B K \Vert \Vert x(t_k) \Vert} \right) = \frac{1}{\Vert A + BK \Vert} \ln\!\left( 1 + \frac{\sigma}{\Vert B K \Vert} \right)$$

---

**(b) — Numerical Computation of $\tau_{\min}$ (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute $BK$:
$$BK = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute $A + BK$:
$$A + BK = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the spectral norm $\Vert A + BK \Vert$ (largest singular value). The matrix is symmetric, so the norm equals the largest absolute eigenvalue. The eigenvalues of $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{bmatrix}$ are roots of $\lambda^2 + \lambda + 1 = 0$, i.e., $\lambda = (-1 \pm \sqrt{-3})/2$, with magnitude $\sqrt{1/4 + 3/4} = 1$. Hence:
$$\Vert A + BK \Vert = 1$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute $\Vert BK \Vert$. Since $BK$ is not symmetric, compute the largest singular value. The singular values of $BK$ are the square roots of eigenvalues of $B^T K^T K B = (KB)^T (KB)$:
$$KB = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = 2$$
So $(KB)^T (KB) = 4$, and the singular value is $\sqrt{4} = 2$. Thus $\Vert BK \Vert = 2$.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Plug into the formula with $\sigma = 0.1$:
$$\tau_{\min} = \frac{1}{1} \ln\!\left( 1 + \frac{0.1}{2} \right) = \ln(1.05)$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Evaluate numerically:
$$\tau_{\min} = \ln(1.05) \approx 0.04879 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** **Zeno-free verification:** Since $\tau_{\min} \approx 0.04879 > 0$, the inter-event time is strictly positive for all events. Therefore, no Zeno phenomenon can occur and the self-triggered scheme is realizable. The system triggers at most approximately **20.5 times per second**, which is far less than a typical 100 Hz time-triggered baseline — confirming the resource savings of ETC/STC.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in PECPT632 (Module 3):**
> 1. **Forgetting the error decomposition step** in part (a) of ETC derivations. Without $x(t_k) = x(t) + e(t)$, the closed-loop form $\dot{x} = (A+BK)x + BKe$ cannot be obtained. Loss: **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up the inequalities** — students often write $\dot{V} \leq 0$ without explicitly identifying the coefficient that must remain negative. Examiners expect the explicit condition on $\sigma$ in boxed form. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Omitting the spectral norm justification** in the numerical part. The KTU board requires the student to *show* how $\Vert A + BK \Vert$ is computed (eigenvalues or singular values), not merely state the value. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing ETC and STC trigger mechanisms** in conceptual questions. STC is *predictive* and *open-loop between events*; ETC is *reactive* and *closed-loop monitored*. Mixing these costs **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Not proving Zeno-freeness** in design problems. Even if $\sigma$ is given, KTU examiners require an explicit statement of $\tau_{\min} > 0$. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 6. **Using $h$ (sampling period) instead of $\tau_k$ (inter-event time)** in the final answer — these are fundamentally different quantities. Loss: **1 mark**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three Paradigms:** Time-triggered (periodic, wasteful), Event-triggered (reactive, resource-aware), Self-triggered (predictive, monitor-free).
- **Core Triggering Rule:** Event fires when $\| x(t_k) - x(t) \| > \sigma \| x(t) \|$, with $\sigma \in (0, 1)$.
- **Closed-Loop Form:** $\dot{x}(t) = (A + BK) x(t) + BK e(t)$ — the foundation of all ETC stability proofs.
- **Stability Result:** $\sigma < \frac{\alpha \lambda_{\min}(P)}{\Vert PBK \Vert \lambda_{\max}(P)}$ — the smaller $\sigma$, the more frequent the triggers, but the more stable the system.
- **Zeno-Freeness:** Proven by the lower bound $\tau_{\min} = \frac{1}{\Vert A+BK \Vert} \ln(1 + \sigma/\Vert BK \Vert) > 0$.
- **Self-Triggered Update Rule:** $t_{k+1} = t_k + \tau(x(t_k))$ — typically a *constant* for LTI plants.
- **Key Trade-off:** ETC = better resource savings + dedicated monitor; STC = no monitor + slightly conservative + more controller-side computation.
- **Lyapunov Function:** Always use $V(x) = x^T P x$ with $P = P^T \succ 0$ and the nominal Lyapunov inequality $(A+BK)^T P + P(A+BK) \preceq -2\alpha P$.
- **Engineering Payoff:** Average sampling rate can drop by **60–80%** compared to periodic control, with no loss of stability.
- **Real-Time Applicability:** ETC and STC are *not* suitable for hard real-time deterministic systems; they shine in **soft real-time, resource-constrained, wireless, and IoT** scenarios.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compensation Strategies for Delays and Losses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT632 - NETWORKED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compensation Strategies for Delays and Losses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compensation Strategies for Delays and Losses** refer to a class of control-theoretic and signal-processing techniques specifically designed to mitigate the degrading effects of **time-varying communication delays** (both sensor-to-controller $\tau_{sc}$ and controller-to-actuator $\tau_{ca}$) and **packet dropouts/losses** that occur over imperfect networked communication channels in a Networked Control System (NCS).

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, these strategies are classified into three broad families:

1. **Predictive Compensation** — uses a model of the plant to forecast future states and "undo" the delay (e.g., **Smith Predictor**, **Generalized Predictive Control (GPC)**).
2. **Buffering & Queueing Compensation** — reshapes the delay into a deterministic value (e.g., **Buffers**, **Maximum Allowable Delay Bound**).
3. **Stochastic / Robust Compensation** — treats delay/loss as a random process and designs robust or stochastic controllers (e.g., **Jump Linear Systems**, **Kalman filtering with intermittent observations**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Train & Telegram" Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car and your steering wheel is in a different city. You give a steering command through a messenger who travels by train. Sometimes the train is fast (small delay), sometimes slow (large delay), and occasionally the telegram is **lost in transit** (packet dropout). 

- **Without Compensation:** You keep turning the wheel blind — the car goes off-road because your commands arrive too late or never arrive.
- **With Compensation:** You have a **mental model of the car** (the plant model) and you *predict* where the car will be when the message arrives. You send commands as if you were actually inside the car. This is exactly what the **Smith Predictor** does — it predicts the future output and feeds it back to the controller so it acts as if there were no delay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The compensation strategy does *not* eliminate the delay physically — it mathematically **cancels its destabilizing effect** from the closed-loop characteristic equation.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Maximum Allowable Delay Bound (MADB):** denoted $\tau_{max}$ — the largest delay for which closed-loop stability is still guaranteed.
- **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR):** $\gamma = \frac{\text{Packets Received}}{\text{Packets Sent}}$, with $0 \le \gamma \le 1$.
- **Round-Trip Time (RTT):** $\tau_{rtt} = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$.
- **Network Bandwidth:** typically measured in **bits/second (bps)**; control loops usually require **50–1000 Hz** update rates.
- **MAC Layer Protocol:** IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi), IEEE 802.15.4 (WirelessHART), and Ethernet (IEEE 802.3) are standard; **CAN bus** operates at **1 Mbps** maximum.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 2024 questions, always state the **delay type** (constant, time-varying, random) **before** choosing a compensation strategy. Examiners award marks for this classification step.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Classification of Compensation Strategies

Compensation strategies can be broken into the following structured taxonomy:

### A. Predictive Compensation (Model-Based)
- **Smith Predictor (SP):** Removes delay from the closed-loop characteristic equation by placing a parallel model of the plant + delay.
- **Modified Smith Predictor:** Used when the plant model is uncertain.
- **Generalized Predictive Control (GPC):** Uses receding-horizon optimization to handle time-varying delays.

### B. Buffering / Queueing Compensation
- **Buffers (FIFO):** Convert random delays into a *fixed maximum delay* $\tau_{max}$, simplifying controller design at the cost of additional latency.
- **Event-Triggered Buffers:** Only forward packets when a state-change threshold is crossed.

### C. Robust / Stochastic Compensation
- **Jump Linear Systems (JLS):** Model packet loss as a Markov chain; controller switches between modes.
- **$\mathcal{H}_\infty$ Robust Control:** Designs a controller robust to worst-case delay bounds.
- **Stochastic Optimal Control:** Uses Kalman filter with intermittent observations.

### D. Forward / Backward Compensation
- **Forward Delay Compensation (FDC):** Uses a delay-free predictor at the actuator side.
- **Backward Delay Compensation (BDC):** Predicts the *past* plant state from old measurements to align them with current control action.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Strategy

| Strategy | Core "Why" | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| Smith Predictor | Cancels delay from char. equation | Known, stable plant; constant/slowly-varying delay |
| Buffer | Determinizes random delay | Small delay variance; safety-critical |
| JLS / Markov | Delay/loss is stochastic with known PDF | Wireless networks with burst losses |
| $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ | Worst-case bounded delay | Model uncertainty present |
| GPC | Delay changes adaptively | Internet-based NCS with jitter |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `\vert` for absolute values to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description / Symbols | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau_{total} = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca} + \tau_{proc}$ | Total loop delay = sensor + comm + processing | **seconds (s)** |
| 2 | $\gamma = \dfrac{N_{recv}}{N_{sent}}$ | Packet Delivery Ratio | dimensionless, $0 \le \gamma \le 1$ |
| 3 | $P_{loss} = 1 - \gamma$ | Packet Loss Probability | dimensionless |
| 4 | $G_{cl}(s) = \dfrac{C(s)G(s)e^{-\tau s}}{1 + C(s)G(s)e^{-\tau s}}$ | Closed-loop TF with constant delay $\tau$ | Laplace domain |
| 5 | $\tau_{max} < \dfrac{\pi - \phi_{m}}{2\omega_{gc}}$ | MADB for phase-margin $\phi_m$ at gain-crossover $\omega_{gc}$ | seconds |
| 6 | $\hat{x}(k\mid k-1) = A\hat{x}(k-1) + Bu(k-1)$ | Kalman Predictor (loss-aware) | state-space |
| 7 | $P_{k\mid k-1} = AP_{k-1}A^T + Q$ | Predicted error covariance | $\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$ |
| 8 | $K_k = P_{k\mid k-1}C^T(CP_{k\mid k-1}C^T + R)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain (with measurement) | matrix |
| 9 | $\hat{x}(k\mid k) = \hat{x}(k\mid k-1) + \gamma_k K_k(y_k - C\hat{x}(k\mid k-1))$ | Intermittent-measurement Kalman update | $\gamma_k = 1$ if packet arrives |
| 10 | $J_{GPC} = \sum_{j=N_1}^{N_2} [y(k+j) - w(k+j)]^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{N_u} [\Delta u(k+j-1)]^2$ | GPC cost function | receding horizon |
| 11 | $\dot{V}(x) \le -\alpha \vert x \vert^2$ for $\tau \le \tau_{max}$ | Lyapunov stability condition | $\alpha > 0$ |
| 12 | $f_{delay}(s) = e^{-\tau s} \approx \dfrac{1 - \tau s/2}{1 + \tau s/2}$ | Padé-1st-order delay approximation | rational TF |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these strategies are deployed:**
> - **Process Industries:** Smith Predictor in chemical reactors where Ethernet introduces 100–500 ms delays.
> - **Automotive:** CAN-bus JLS modelling for brake-by-wire systems.
> - **Aerospace:** $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ robust control in unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) with telemetry delays.
> - **Smart Grids:** GPC for wide-area monitoring with PMU communication delays of 20–100 ms.
> - **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Event-triggered buffering for low-power wireless sensor networks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Closed-Loop Transfer Function With Delay

Consider a standard unity-feedback NCS with plant $G(s)$, controller $C(s)$, and constant sensor-to-actuator delay $\tau$.

**Step 1 — Define the control signal arriving at the plant:**
The controller output $U(s)$ reaches the plant as $U(s)e^{-\tau s}$ due to the communication channel delay.

**Step 2 — Plant output:**
$$Y(s) = G(s) e^{-\tau s} U(s)$$

**Step 3 — Controller equation:**
$$U(s) = C(s)[R(s) - Y(s)]$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (3) into (2):**
$$Y(s) = G(s) e^{-\tau s} \, C(s)[R(s) - Y(s)]$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $Y(s)/R(s)$:**
$$Y(s)[1 + C(s)G(s)e^{-\tau s}] = C(s)G(s)e^{-\tau s} R(s)$$

**Step 6 — Final closed-loop transfer function:**
$$G_{cl}(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{C(s)G(s)e^{-\tau s}}{1 + C(s)G(s)e^{-\tau s}}$$

> **Conversion logic:** This expression is transcendental (contains $e^{-\tau s}$), which means the closed-loop system has *infinitely many* poles, making classical Routh-Hurwitz inapplicable. This is precisely why compensation is required.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Smith Predictor Architecture

The Smith Predictor (1957) introduces a parallel model of the delay-free plant $G_0(s)$ and the delayed plant $G_0(s)e^{-\tau s}$.

**Step 1 — Smith Predictor structure:**
The controller $C(s)$ receives the *predicted* feedback:
$$Y_{sp}(s) = Y(s) + G_0(s)[1 - e^{-\tau s}]U(s)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute plant dynamics $Y(s) = G_0(s)e^{-\tau s}U(s)$:**
$$Y_{sp}(s) = G_0(s)e^{-\tau s}U(s) + G_0(s)[1 - e^{-\tau s}]U(s)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**
$$Y_{sp}(s) = G_0(s)U(s)[e^{-\tau s} + 1 - e^{-\tau s}] = G_0(s)U(s)$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent delay-free closed loop:**
The controller now sees $G_0(s)$ (delay-free plant) in its feedback path. The closed-loop TF reduces to:
$$G_{cl,sp}(s) = \frac{C(s)G_0(s)}{1 + C(s)G_0(s)}$$

**Step 5 — Characteristic equation becomes delay-free:**
$$1 + C(s)G_0(s) = 0$$

> **Conversion logic:** The delay $e^{-\tau s}$ has been *mathematically removed* from the characteristic equation, restoring classical pole-placement design. **Critical limitation:** the plant model $G_0(s)$ must be accurate; otherwise mismatch causes instability.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Kalman Filter With Intermittent Observations

Consider a discrete-time system with packet loss indicator $\gamma_k \in \{0, 1\}$ where $\gamma_k = 1$ if the measurement arrives.

**Step 1 — Plant model:**
$$x_{k+1} = A x_k + B u_k + w_k, \quad w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$$

**Step 2 — Measurement model:**
$$y_k = \gamma_k (C x_k + v_k), \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$$

**Step 3 — Time update (prediction) — always executed:**
$$\hat{x}(k\mid k-1) = A \hat{x}(k-1\mid k-1) + B u_{k-1}$$
$$P(k\mid k-1) = A P(k-1\mid k-1) A^T + Q$$

**Step 4 — Measurement update — conditional on $\gamma_k$:**
If $\gamma_k = 1$:
$$K_k = P(k\mid k-1) C^T [C P(k\mid k-1) C^T + R]^{-1}$$
$$\hat{x}(k\mid k) = \hat{x}(k\mid k-1) + K_k[y_k - C\hat{x}(k\mid k-1)]$$
$$P(k\mid k) = (I - K_k C) P(k\mid k-1)$$

If $\gamma_k = 0$:
$$\hat{x}(k\mid k) = \hat{x}(k\mid k-1)$$
$$P(k\mid k) = P(k\mid k-1)$$

**Step 5 — Stability condition (Sinopoli et al., 2004):**
There exists a critical $\gamma_{crit}$ such that the expected error covariance $\mathbb{E}[P_k]$ remains bounded **if and only if** $\gamma > \gamma_{crit}$. For scalar systems:
$$\gamma_{crit} = 1 - \frac{1}{A^2} \cdot \frac{1}{(1 + \sqrt{Q/R})^2}$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is the **hallmark of stochastic compensation** — the system can tolerate packet loss *up to a threshold* and still maintain bounded estimation error.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Strategy vs. Delay Type

| Delay Type | Recommended Strategy | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Constant, known, small ($\tau < T_{sample}/10$) | **Ignore** (delay negligible) | Effect on phase margin is small |
| Constant, known, large | **Smith Predictor** | Removes delay from char. eq. |
| Time-varying, bounded | **Buffer** or **GPC** | Determinizes the delay |
| Stochastic, i.i.d. loss | **Kalman w/ intermittent obs.** | Optimal MMSE estimator |
| Bursty Markov loss | **Jump Linear System** | Mode-dependent controller |
| Unknown but bounded | **$\mathcal{H}_\infty$ robust** | Worst-case design |
| Plant uncertain | **Modified SP / Robust SP** | Robustness to model error |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Smith Predictor in NCS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[R s] --> SUM1((+))
    SUM1 --> C[C s Controller]
    C --> CH[/Channel e to the power of tau s/]
    CH --> P[Gp s Plant]
    P --> Y[Y s Output]
    Y --> SUM1
    Y --> SMP[Sum Node SP]
    CH --> GP0[G0 s Model]
    GP0 --> EX[(1 - e to the power of tau s)]
    EX --> SMP
    SMP --> SUM1
```

**Legend:**
- $R(s)$ = Reference input
- $C(s)$ = Primary controller
- $G(s)e^{-\tau s}$ = Plant + network delay
- $G_0(s)$ = Internal plant model (no delay)
- $(1 - e^{-\tau s})$ = Delay-cancellation block

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Kalman Filter with Packet Loss

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    XK[x of k] --> A[Matrix A]
    A --> XKP1[x of k plus 1]
    B[Matrix B] --> XKP1
    XKP1 --> PKP1[P k plus 1]
    Q[Covariance Q] --> PKP1
    PKP1 --> K[Kalman Gain K]
    XKP1 --> SUMF((+))
    YK[y of k]
    YK --> GAMMA{Packet gamma k}
    GAMMA -->|gamma equals 1| YKF[y of k received]
    GAMMA -->|gamma equals 0| YKD[Measurement lost]
    YKF --> SUMF
    K --> SUMF
    SUMF --> XKF[x of k filtered]
    XKF --> A
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Delay & Loss Compensation Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[NCS Delay Loss Compensation] --> CAT{Categorization}
    CAT -->|Delay Type| DT[Predictive Predictive GPC SP]
    CAT -->|Loss Type| LT[Kalman JLS H-infinity]
    DT --> SP1[Smith Predictor]
    DT --> GPC1[Generalized Predictive Control]
    DT --> BUF1[Buffering FIFO]
    LT --> KAL[Kalman Filter Intermittent]
    LT --> JLS1[Jump Linear Systems]
    LT --> HIN[H-infinity Robust]
    SP1 --> OUT1[Compensated NCS]
    GPC1 --> OUT1
    BUF1 --> OUT1
    KAL --> OUT1
    JLS1 --> OUT1
    HIN --> OUT1
```

## 4.4 Jump Linear System Mode-Switching Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Mode 1 Packet Received] -->|Switch| M2[Mode 2 Packet Lost]
    M2 -->|Prob 1 minus gamma| M1
    M2 -->|Prob gamma| M2
    M1 -->|Prob gamma| M1
    M1 -->|Prob 1 minus gamma| M2
```

This Markov chain models packet loss with two states: **arrival** and **loss**, with transition probability $\gamma$ (delivery ratio).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the Smith Predictor. State its main limitation.**

**Model Answer:**
> The **Smith Predictor** is a compensation strategy proposed by O.J.M. Smith (1957) that uses an internal plant model $G_0(s)$ to mathematically remove the effect of a known communication delay $e^{-\tau s}$ from the closed-loop characteristic equation. It modifies the feedback signal as $Y_{sp}(s) = Y(s) + G_0(s)[1 - e^{-\tau s}]U(s)$, so the controller effectively sees a delay-free plant.
>
> **Main Limitation:** It requires an **accurate plant model** $G_0(s)$. Any mismatch between the true plant $G(s)$ and the model $G_0(s)$ — especially in unstable plants or plants with right-half-plane zeros — leads to **prediction error** and potential **closed-loop instability**.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the role of a Kalman filter in delay/loss compensation?**

**Model Answer:**
> In a Networked Control System with intermittent observations (packet loss), the **Kalman filter** acts as an **optimal state estimator** that:
> (i) **Predicts** the current state using the plant model when measurements are missing ($\gamma_k = 0$).
> (ii) **Updates** the state estimate when a packet arrives ($\gamma_k = 1$) using the innovation $y_k - C\hat{x}(k\mid k-1)$.
>
> This produces a **bounded mean-square error** estimate as long as the packet delivery ratio $\gamma$ exceeds a critical threshold $\gamma_{crit}$, making it the standard stochastic compensation strategy for lossy networks.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, derive the closed-loop transfer function of a Networked Control System with a constant sensor-to-controller delay $\tau_{sc}$ and controller-to-actuator delay $\tau_{ca}$. Discuss how this delay affects the characteristic equation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Smith Predictor** compensation strategy. With the help of a block diagram, derive the modified closed-loop transfer function and show that the delay $e^{-\tau s}$ is removed from the characteristic equation. State two limitations of the Smith Predictor. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Consider the NCS block diagram with sensor delay $\tau_{sc}$ and actuator delay $\tau_{ca}$.

**Step 1:** Let the total delay in the loop be:
$$\tau = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$$

**Step 2:** The output reaching the controller is $Y(s)e^{-\tau_{sc} s}$, and the control action reaching the plant is $U(s)e^{-\tau_{ca} s}$.

**Step 3:** Controller equation:
$$U(s) = C(s)[R(s) - Y(s)e^{-\tau_{sc} s}]$$

**Step 4:** Plant output equation:
$$Y(s) = G(s) U(s) e^{-\tau_{ca} s}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting:
$$Y(s) = G(s) e^{-\tau_{ca} s} \, C(s)[R(s) - Y(s)e^{-\tau_{sc} s}]$$

$$Y(s)[1 + C(s)G(s)e^{-\tau s}] = C(s)G(s)e^{-\tau_{ca} s} R(s)$$

**Step 6:** Final closed-loop TF:
$$G_{cl}(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{C(s)G(s)e^{-\tau_{ca} s}}{1 + C(s)G(s)e^{-\tau s}}$$

**Discussion:**
The characteristic equation $1 + C(s)G(s)e^{-\tau s} = 0$ is **transcendental** due to $e^{-\tau s}$. This means:
- Infinite number of closed-loop poles (delays introduce "spectral" content).
- Classical Routh-Hurwitz **cannot be directly applied**.
- Stability depends on $\tau$; there exists a **Maximum Allowable Delay Bound (MADB)** $\tau_{max}$ beyond which the system becomes unstable.

**Part (b) — Smith Predictor:**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1)

**Derivation:**
The Smith Predictor replaces the feedback signal $Y(s)$ with a *predicted* feedback $Y_{sp}(s)$:
$$Y_{sp}(s) = Y(s) + G_0(s)U(s) - G_0(s)e^{-\tau s}U(s)$$
$$Y_{sp}(s) = Y(s) + G_0(s)[1 - e^{-\tau s}]U(s)$$

Assuming perfect model ($G_0 = G$) and using $Y(s) = G(s)e^{-\tau s}U(s)$:
